title: EMOVIS: Emotion-Optimized Image Processing
summary: In cinematography, visual attributes such as color grading, contrast, and brightness are manipulated to reinforce the emotional narrative of a scene. However, conventional Image Signal Processors (ISPs) prioritize scene fidelity, effectively neglecting this expressive dimension. To bring this cinematic capability to real-time camera pipelines during video capture, we introduce EMOVIS (EMotion-Optimized VISual processing). We establish a systematic mapping between a compact set of high-level emotional states (Happy, Calm, Angry, Sad) and low-level ISP controls - including color saturation, local tone mapping, and sharpness - supported by a calibration user study with statistically significant effects across parameters. We propose a control framework that integrates these emotion-driven adjustments into standard ISP hardware without altering the underlying processing stages. Validation via blind A/B testing shows that viewers prefer the emotion-optimized rendering in 87% of trials when the target emotion matches the scene context, indicating that emotion-aligned ISP control improves perceived suitability for expressive visual content.

AI.tldr: EMOVIS introduces emotion-optimized ISP controls (color, tone, sharpness) mapped to four emotional states, achieving 87% viewer preference in blind tests.
AI.motivation: Standard ISPs prioritize scene fidelity but neglect expressive emotional manipulation used in cinematography; the authors aim to bring cinematic emotional control to real-time camera pipelines.
AI.method: (1) Systematic mapping of four emotions (Happy, Calm, Angry, Sad) to low-level ISP parameters (saturation, tone mapping, sharpness) via a calibration user study. (2) Integration into standard ISP hardware without modifying core processing stages. (3) Blind A/B testing validation.
AI.result: Viewers preferred emotion-optimized rendering in 87% of trials when the target emotion matched the scene context.
AI.conclusion: Emotion-aligned ISP control significantly improves perceived suitability for expressive visual content, enabling real-time cinematic-style video capture on conventional hardware.

title: Dante: An Open Source Model Pre-Training and Fine-Tuning Tool for the Dafne Federated Framework for Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Adapting pre-trained deep learning segmentation models to new clinical domains is a persistent challenge in medical image analysis, particularly when annotated data at the target site are scarce. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning strategies offer a principled solution by selectively updating a controlled subset of model parameters, preserving previously acquired representations while reducing the risk of overfitting on small datasets. This paper introduces DAfNe TrainEr (Dante), an open-source module integrating with the Dafne federated segmentation ecosystem as a dedicated training and fine-tuning backend. Dante supports training from scratch with automatic architecture configuration, configurable layer freezing schedules, and Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) extended to N-dimensional convolutional layers through channel-wise factorization. To validate the module, Gradual Unfreezing (GU) and LoRA are assessed across realistic cross-domain MRI transfer scenarios covering abdominal organ segmentation and brain white matter lesion segmentation, under full-data and few-shot conditions. GU reduced the epochs required to reach 85% of peak performance by up to 63.6% compared to training from scratch, while LoRA achieved Dice Similarity Coefficients up to 0.957 in data-rich scenarios. Both strategies outperformed the baseline across all tested domains, with gains amplified by richer pre-training datasets. These results validate Dante as a domain-agnostic fine-tuning module for medical image segmentation in real clinical deployment conditions. Dante code is available at https://github.com/dafne-imaging/dafne-torch-trainer while Dafne ecosystem project is available at https://github.com/dafne-imaging.

AI.tldr: This paper presents Dante, an open-source module for parameter-efficient fine-tuning of deep learning segmentation models in medical imaging. It integrates with the Dafne ecosystem and supports techniques like Gradual Unfreezing (GU) and Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) for N-dimensional convolutions. Experiments on cross-domain MRI tasks show GU reduces training time by up to 63.6% and LoRA achieves Dice up to 0.957, outperforming baselines across diverse clinical scenarios.
AI.motivation: Adapting pre-trained segmentation models to new clinical domains is challenging due to scarce annotated data at target sites. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning is needed to prevent overfitting while preserving learned representations.
AI.method: Dante integrates with the Dafne federated segmentation ecosystem, supporting automatic architecture configuration, configurable layer freezing schedules, and LoRA extended to N-dimensional convolutional layers via channel-wise factorization.
AI.result: GU reduced epochs to reach 85% of peak performance by up to 63.6% vs. training from scratch. LoRA achieved Dice up to 0.957 in data-rich scenarios. Both strategies outperformed baselines across all tested domains.
AI.conclusion: Dante is validated as a domain-agnostic fine-tuning module for medical image segmentation under real clinical deployment conditions.

title: Phase-Corrected Near-Field Microwave Imaging via Inverse Source Reconstruction with Modulated Signals
summary: An inverse source reconstruction (ISR) based 3-D near-field (NF) passive radar microwave imaging method utilizing modulated signals is presented. The modulated signals from a non-cooperative transmitter are scattered by the targets of interest and captured by a fixed reference antenna together with an NF scanning probe at different positions. By normalizing with the reference signals, spatial coherence of the NF observations is obtained, and a single-frequency inverse source solver is subsequently utilized for ISR and image generation. A corresponding phase correction method is proposed for the coherent superposition of multi-frequency images and verified through simulations. In addition, it is shown that for realistic narrowband signals, an incoherent imaging approach is sufficient. The presented technical scheme is validated using a planar scanning system in a typical office room, where software-defined radios are employed for the transmitting and receiving of narrowband orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing signals at Wi-Fi operating frequencies. With the aid of background subtraction and reference signals, images of a mannequin placed in the office room are successfully obtained.

AI.tldr: A 3-D near-field passive radar microwave imaging method using modulated signals (e.g., Wi-Fi) is proposed, employing inverse source reconstruction with reference signal normalization and phase correction for multi-frequency coherent superposition. Validated experimentally in an office room with software-defined radios, successfully imaging a mannequin.
AI.motivation: To enable passive microwave imaging using non-cooperative transmitters (e.g., Wi-Fi signals) without requiring dedicated radar transmitters, leveraging modulated signals already present in the environment for 3-D near-field imaging.
AI.method: 1) Capture scattered modulated signals using a fixed reference antenna and an NF scanning probe. 2) Normalize with reference signals to obtain spatial coherence. 3) Apply a single-frequency inverse source solver for image reconstruction. 4) Propose a phase correction method for coherent multi-frequency image fusion. 5) For narrowband signals, use an incoherent imaging approach. 6) Validate with planar scanning, SDRs, and OFDM signals at Wi-Fi frequencies.
AI.result: Successful imaging of a mannequin in an office room using narrowband OFDM signals at Wi-Fi frequencies, with background subtraction and reference signal processing. Phase correction enables coherent multi-frequency superposition; incoherent imaging sufficient for narrowband cases.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ISR-based passive radar method is viable for 3-D near-field microwave imaging using ambient modulated signals, offering a practical solution for non-cooperative imaging scenarios.

title: Multipath Exploitation in Highly Reflective Environments for Enhanced Microwave Imaging via Inverse Source Reconstruction
summary: Multipath effects significantly influence the quality of microwave imaging in highly reflective environments, while the physical measurement aperture size constrains resolution. It is shown that by exploiting multipath reflections, improved resolution can be achieved while maintaining acceptable artifact levels. Based on image theory, strong scattered fields from an ideal reflection plane can be represented by virtual image sources. Using a single-frequency inverse source solver, the spatially distributed original and image sources are reconstructed and separated, which allows separate application of the imaging algorithm for both of them. The coherent combination of both sets of sources together with appropriate phase correction results in an effective aperture expansion that yields superior resolution. Furthermore, this separation strategy significantly mitigates interference artifacts. Simulation results, supported by theoretical analysis and comparison with a ray-tracing enhanced backprojection algorithm are presented to verify the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a multipath exploitation method for microwave imaging that uses virtual image sources derived from reflection planes to effectively expand the aperture, improving resolution while reducing artifacts.
AI.motivation: Multipath effects degrade microwave imaging quality in reflective environments, and physical aperture size limits resolution. The authors aim to turn multipath into an advantage rather than a nuisance.
AI.method: Using image theory, strong scattered fields from reflection planes are represented as virtual image sources. A single-frequency inverse source solver reconstructs and separates original and image sources, then coherently combines them with phase correction to expand the effective aperture.
AI.result: Simulation results show superior resolution compared to traditional methods and significant mitigation of interference artifacts. Theoretical analysis and comparison with ray-tracing enhanced backprojection confirm effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Exploiting multipath reflections via virtual image sources enables effective aperture expansion, improving resolution while keeping artifacts acceptable in highly reflective environments.

title: 6DMA-Enabled ISAC for Low-Altitude Economy
comment: This manuscript was accepted by IEEE
summary: In this paper, we investigate a six dimensional movable antenna (6DMA) enable integrated sensing and communications (ISAC) network in low-altitude economy. The studied 6DMA can move in a three-dimensional space and rotate around its surface center, making full use of spatial freedom to adapt to the different location distributions of uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs) adjust channel conditions in time. However, since the rotation and location change of 6DMA requires the assistance of a physical device, it is unreasonable for 6DMA to change locations too frequently. Therefore, we propose a hierarchical deep reinforcement learning algorithm based on twin delayed deep deterministic policy gradient. The first layer optimizes the rotation and location of 6DMA with infrequent updates, and the second layer optimizes the UAV flight direction and base station transmit beamforming matrix in each time slot. Under the condition of satisfying the sensing intensity of the sensing target, the total data transmission rate to the UAVs is maximized. The numerical results show that the proposed 6DMA-enable ISAC algorithm through joint optimization of multiple variables performs significantly better than the partially movable scheme and the fixed antenna position scheme.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a hierarchical deep reinforcement learning algorithm for 6DMA-enabled ISAC networks in low-altitude economy, optimizing antenna movement, rotation, UAV flight direction, and beamforming to maximize data transmission rate while meeting sensing requirements.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge that 6DMA (six dimensional movable antenna) cannot change positions too frequently due to physical device constraints, while still needing to adapt to UAV location distributions and changing channel conditions in low-altitude economy ISAC networks.
AI.method: A hierarchical deep reinforcement learning algorithm based on twin delayed deep deterministic policy gradient (TD3). The first layer optimizes 6DMA rotation and location with infrequent updates, while the second layer optimizes UAV flight direction and base station transmit beamforming matrix in each time slot.
AI.result: The proposed 6DMA-enabled ISAC algorithm outperforms partially movable and fixed antenna position schemes in terms of total data transmission rate, while satisfying sensing intensity constraints.
AI.conclusion: Joint optimization of 6DMA movement, rotation, UAV flight direction, and beamforming significantly improves ISAC network performance in low-altitude economy applications.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Uncertainty Analysis in Self-Adaptive Robots Using LLMs
summary: Self-adaptive robots operate in dynamic, unpredictable environments where unaddressed uncertainties can lead to safety violations and operational failures. However, systematically identifying and analyzing these uncertainties, including their sources, impacts, and mitigation strategies, remains a significant challenge given the inherent complexity of real-world environments, dynamic robotic behavior, and the rapid evolution of robotic technologies. To address this, we introduce RoboULM, a human-in-the-loop methodology and tool that supports practitioners in systematically exploring uncertainties at the design stage using large language models (LLMs). Moreover, we present an uncertainty taxonomy that provides a detailed catalog of uncertainties in self-adaptive robots. We evaluated RoboULM with 16 practitioners from four industrial use cases. The results show that RoboULM was perceived as both useful and easy to understand, with the participants particularly valuing structured prompting and iterative refinement support. These findings demonstrate the potential of RoboULM as a viable solution for systematic uncertainty analysis in complex robots.

AI.tldr: RoboULM is a human-in-the-loop methodology using LLMs to help practitioners systematically identify and analyze uncertainties in self-adaptive robots during the design stage, validated through 16 practitioners across four industrial use cases.
AI.motivation: Self-adaptive robots face dynamic, unpredictable environments where unaddressed uncertainties cause safety violations and failures, yet systematically identifying and analyzing these uncertainties remains challenging due to environmental complexity and rapidly evolving robotic technologies.
AI.method: The authors propose RoboULM, a human-in-the-loop methodology and tool supported by LLMs, along with an uncertainty taxonomy cataloging uncertainties in self-adaptive robots. Evaluation was conducted with 16 practitioners across four industrial use cases.
AI.result: RoboULM was perceived as useful and easy to understand; participants particularly valued structured prompting and iterative refinement support.
AI.conclusion: RoboULM demonstrates potential as a viable solution for systematic uncertainty analysis in complex self-adaptive robots.

title: Memorization In Stable Diffusion Is Unexpectedly Driven by CLIP Embeddings
comment: Accepted at CVPR 2026 Findings. Code is available at https://github.com/quasar529/sd-clip-mem
summary: Understanding how textual embeddings contribute to memorization in text-to-image diffusion models is crucial for both interpretability and safety. This paper investigates an unexpected behavior of CLIP embeddings in Stable Diffusion, revealing that the model disproportionately relies on specific embeddings. We categorize input tokens as <startoftext>, <prompt>, <endoftext> and <pad> with corresponding embeddings $\mathbf{v}^{\mathbf{sot}}, \mathbf{v}^{\mathbf{pr}}, \mathbf{v}^{\mathbf{eot}}, \mathbf{v}^{\mathbf{pad}}$. We discover that $\mathbf{v}^{\mathbf{pr}}$ contribute minimally to generation in memorized cases. In contrast, $\mathbf{v}^{\mathbf{pad}}$ strongly affect memorization due to their structural duplication of $\mathbf{v}^{\mathbf{eot}}$, the only embedding explicitly optimized during CLIP training. This duplication unintentionally amplifies the influence of $\mathbf{v}^{\mathbf{eot}}$, causing the model to over-rely on it, thereby driving memorization. Based on these observations, we propose two simple yet effective inference-time mitigation strategies: (1) Replacing the tokenizer's default <pad> from <eot> to the ! token before embedding, and masking the $\mathbf{v}^{\mathbf{eot}}$; (2) Partial masking of $\mathbf{v}^{\mathbf{pad}}$. Both suppress memorization without degrading quality, and are readily deployable without prior detection.

AI.tldr: This paper reveals that memorization in text-to-image diffusion models is driven by CLIP's <pad> embeddings unintentionally amplifying the <endoftext> embedding's influence. The authors propose two simple inference-time fixes—replacing the <pad> token or partially masking pad embeddings—that effectively suppress memorization without sacrificing image quality.
AI.motivation: To understand how textual embeddings contribute to memorization in text-to-image diffusion models, which is crucial for model interpretability and safety.
AI.method: The authors categorize input tokens into four types (<startoftext>, <prompt>, <endoftext>, <pad>) and analyze their contributions. They discover that <pad> embeddings structurally duplicate <endoftext>, amplifying its influence and driving memorization. Based on this, they propose two mitigation strategies: (1) replacing <pad> token with '!' and masking <endoftext> embedding; (2) partial masking of <pad> embeddings.
AI.result: Both proposed strategies effectively suppress memorization without degrading image quality, and are readily deployable without prior detection.
AI.conclusion: Memorization in Stable Diffusion is largely driven by an unintentional structural duplication of <endoftext> in <pad> embeddings. Simple inference-time modifications can effectively mitigate this issue.

title: Information Theory and Statistical Learning
summary:
This manuscript contains preprint of a chapter under consideration for inclusion in the forthcoming third edition of {\em Cover and Thomas's Elements of Information Theory}, posted with permission from Wiley. The table of contents EIT-3 ToC of the new edition can be found at: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1L-m4oQEJw1PJhoxBeMwrrBD8S_HmvzMEkPbYvS24980/edit?usp=sharing . For feedback, please contact abbas@ee.stanford.edu
  Learning and information theory intersect in both model training and the characterization of fundamental performance limits. This manuscript provides a concise and accessible treatment of the first intersection, requiring only basic background in information theory and statistics at the senior undergraduate or first-year graduate level. End-of-chapter exercises make the material well suited for classroom use as well as self-study.
  The chapter focuses on the role of divergence measures in model training, with examples ranging from linear and logistic regression to autoregressive models, variational autoencoders, diffusion models, generative adversarial networks, and score-based models. It introduces the evidence lower bound (ELBO), $f$\!-divergences, and the Fisher divergence. In particular, the treatment of the generative diffusion model provides a more systematic and explicit derivation than is typical in the literature.

AI.tldr: This book chapter explores the intersection of learning and information theory, focusing on divergence measures in model training (from linear regression to diffusion models), with a systematic derivation of generative diffusion models.
AI.motivation: To provide a concise, accessible treatment of how information theory (particularly divergence measures) connects to model training, suitable for classroom use and self-study at the senior undergraduate or first-year graduate level.
AI.method: The chapter introduces and applies the evidence lower bound (ELBO), f-divergences, and Fisher divergence across a range of ML models including linear/logistic regression, autoregressive models, VAEs, diffusion models, GANs, and score-based models, with a particularly systematic derivation of generative diffusion models.
AI.result: A textbook chapter offering a more systematic and explicit derivation of generative diffusion models than typical literature, with end-of-chapter exercises for pedagogical use.
AI.conclusion: Divergence measures serve as a unifying framework connecting information theory to modern ML training, with the chapter providing an accessible bridge between these fields for students and practitioners.

title: StateSMix: Online Lossless Compression via Mamba State Space Models and Sparse N-gram Context Mixing
summary: We present StateSMix, a fully self-contained lossless compressor that couples an online-trained Mamba-style State Space Model (SSM) with sparse n-gram context mixing and arithmetic coding. The model is initialised from scratch and trained token-by-token on the file being compressed, requiring no pre-trained weights, no GPU, and no external dependencies. The SSM (DM=32, NL=2, approximately 120K active parameters per file) provides a continuously-updated probability estimate over BPE tokens, while nine sparse n-gram hash tables (bigram through 32-gram, 16M slots each) add exact local and long-range pattern memorisation via a softmax-invariant logit-bias mechanism that updates only non-zero-count tokens. An entropy-adaptive scaling mechanism modulates the n-gram contribution based on the SSM's predictive confidence, preventing over-correction when the neural model is already well-calibrated. On the standard enwik8 benchmark, StateSMix achieves 2.123 bpb on 1 MB, 2.149 bpb on 3 MB, and 2.162 bpb on 10 MB, beating xz -9e (LZMA2) by 8.7%, 5.4%, and 0.7% respectively. Ablation experiments establish the SSM as the dominant compression engine: it alone accounts for a 46.6% size reduction over a frequency-count baseline and beats xz without any n-gram component, while n-gram tables provide a complementary 4.1% gain through exact context memorisation. OpenMP parallelisation of the training loop yields 1.9x speedup on 4 cores. The system is implemented in pure C with AVX2 SIMD and processes approximately 2,000 tokens per second on commodity x86-64 hardware.

AI.tldr: StateSMix is a lossless compressor combining an online-trained Mamba-style SSM with sparse n-gram context mixing and arithmetic coding, achieving 2.123 bpb on enwik8 (1 MB) and beating xz -9e by up to 8.7%, using pure C with no GPU or pre-trained weights.
AI.motivation: To develop a fully self-contained, high-performance lossless compressor that requires no pre-trained models, no GPU, and no external dependencies, while outperforming traditional compressors like xz through hybrid neural-symbolic context modeling.
AI.method: Combines an online-trained Mamba-style SSM (DM=32, NL=2, ~120K parameters) with nine sparse n-gram hash tables (bigram to 32-gram, 16M slots each), using arithmetic coding and an entropy-adaptive scaling mechanism to modulate n-gram contribution based on SSM confidence.
AI.result: Achieves 2.123 bpb (1 MB), 2.149 bpb (3 MB), 2.162 bpb (10 MB) on enwik8, beating xz -9e by 8.7%, 5.4%, and 0.7%. SSM alone provides 46.6% reduction vs baseline; n-gram adds 4.1%. OpenMP yields 1.9x speedup on 4 cores. Processes ~2,000 tokens/sec on commodity x86-64.
AI.conclusion: StateSMix demonstrates that a lightweight, online-trained SSM combined with sparse n-gram mixing can outperform traditional LZMA2 compression without any pre-training or GPU, establishing the SSM as the primary compression engine with n-gram tables providing complementary gains.

title: CreativityBench: Evaluating Agent Creative Reasoning via Affordance-Based Tool Repurposing
summary: Recent advances in large language models have led to strong performance on reasoning and environment-interaction tasks, yet their ability for creative problem-solving remains underexplored. We study this capability through the lens of creative tool use, where a model repurposes available objects by reasoning about their affordances and attributes rather than relying on canonical usage. As a first step, we introduce CreativityBench, a benchmark for evaluating affordance-based creativity in LLMs. To this end, we build a large-scale affordance knowledge base (KB) with 4K entities and 150K+ affordance annotations, explicitly linking objects, parts, attributes, and actionable uses. Building on this KB, we generate 14K grounded tasks that require identifying non-obvious yet physically plausible solutions under constraints. Evaluations across 10 state-of-the-art LLMs, including closed and open-source models, show that models can often select a plausible object, but fail to identify the correct parts, their affordances, and the underlying physical mechanism needed to solve the task, leading to a significant drop in performance. Furthermore, improvements from model scaling quickly saturate, strong general reasoning does not reliably translate to creative affordance discovery, and common inference-time strategies such as Chain-of-Thought yield limited gains. These results suggest that creative tool use remains a major challenge for current models, and that CreativityBench provides a useful testbed for studying this missing dimension of intelligence, with potential implications for planning and reasoning modules in future agents.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CreativityBench, a benchmark with 4K entities and 150K+ affordance annotations to evaluate LLMs' creative tool use. Results show models can select plausible objects but fail to identify correct parts, affordances, and physical mechanisms, revealing that creative tool use remains a major challenge for current LLMs.
AI.motivation: While LLMs excel at reasoning and environment interaction, their ability for creative problem-solving—specifically repurposing objects based on affordances rather than canonical usage—remains underexplored. The authors aim to fill this gap by systematically evaluating and benchmarking this capability.
AI.method: The authors built a large-scale affordance knowledge base (KB) with 4K entities and 150K+ affordance annotations linking objects, parts, attributes, and actionable uses. Using this KB, they generated 14K grounded tasks requiring non-obvious but physically plausible solutions under constraints, and evaluated 10 state-of-the-art LLMs (both closed and open-source).
AI.result: Models can often select plausible objects but fail to identify correct parts, affordances, and underlying physical mechanisms, leading to significant performance drops. Model scaling quickly saturates, strong general reasoning does not translate to creative affordance discovery, and inference-time strategies like Chain-of-Thought yield limited gains.
AI.conclusion: Creative tool use remains a major challenge for current LLMs. CreativityBench provides a useful testbed for studying this missing dimension of intelligence, with implications for planning and reasoning modules in future agents.

title: A Framework for Exploring and Disentangling Intersectional Bias: A Case Study in Fetal Ultrasound
summary: Bias in medical AI is often framed as a problem of representation. However, in image-based tasks such as fetal ultrasound, performance disparities can arise even when representation is adequate, because predictive accuracy depends strongly on image quality. Image quality is shaped by acquisition conditions and operator expertise, as well as patient-dependent factors such as maternal body mass index (BMI), all of which may correlate with sensitive demographic features. Consequently, observed disparities may reflect the combined influence of demographic, clinical, and acquisition-related factors rather than data imbalance alone, and may obscure underlying interaction or confounding effects. We propose a structured framework to explore and detect intersectional bias, combining unsupervised slice discovery, systematic factor-wise analysis, and targeted intersectional evaluation. In a case study of over 94{,}000 ultrasound images for fetal weight estimation, we analyze bias in a state-of-the-art deep learning (DL) model and the clinical standard Hadlock, a regression formula using biometric measurements. Pixel spacing (PS) -- a parameter considered suboptimal in current acquisition protocols -- emerged as a consistent driver of performance differences, with higher PS associated with improvements of up to 24\% in selected subgroups for both models. Because PS is often adapted in cases of high BMI or low gestational age (GA), this effect carries a substantial risk of confounding. Our intersectional analysis revealed that part of the PS-associated signal is explained by GA, while PS-related improvements persist across BMI strata, highlighting the importance of acquisition-aware and interaction-aware evaluation in medical AI fairness research.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates bias in medical AI beyond mere representation, focusing on how image quality factors (especially pixel spacing) in fetal ultrasound create performance disparities. Using over 94,000 images, they show that pixel spacing—a parameter adjusted for high BMI or low gestational age—drives up to 24% performance improvements, revealing confounding effects that demand acquisition-aware fairness evaluation.
AI.motivation: Current fairness research in medical AI overemphasizes data representation imbalance, neglecting how image quality and acquisition conditions (correlated with demographics) produce performance disparities even with adequate representation.
AI.method: A structured framework combining unsupervised slice discovery, systematic factor-wise analysis, and targeted intersectional evaluation, applied to 94,000+ fetal ultrasound images comparing a deep learning model against the clinical Hadlock regression formula.
AI.result: Pixel spacing (PS) emerged as a consistent driver of performance differences, with higher PS associated with up to 24% improvement in selected subgroups. The effect is partially explained by GA but persists across BMI strata, indicating confounding.
AI.conclusion: Medical AI fairness research must incorporate acquisition-aware and interaction-aware evaluation, as standard representation-focused approaches miss critical confounding effects from technical parameters like pixel spacing.

title: Degeneracy-Aware Agent-Based Resource Allocation for Multi-User MIMO RSMA Network
comment: 6 pages, 7 figures, submitted to IEEE GLOBECOM 2026
summary: This paper proposes a pilot-aware, degeneracy-driven Agent-Based Modelling (ABM) framework for distributed resource allocation in RSMA-enabled multi-user MIMO systems under imperfect Channel State Information (CSI) and residual Successive Interference Cancellation (SIC) error. The centralized RSMA power allocation problem is reformulated as a distributed multi-agent system, where users operate as autonomous agents that iteratively adapt transmit powers based on locally observed feasibility conditions. To capture the joint impact of interference coupling, CSI estimation errors, pilot overhead, and residual SIC error, a novel degeneracy index defined as the ratio of target to achieved signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) is introduced as a unified feasibility metric. This enables a scalable fixed-point power control mechanism that characterizes the feasible operating region without requiring global CSI. Analytical expressions for user-level and system-level outage probabilities are derived under spatially correlated fading, providing insights into reliability under practical impairments. The fundamental interplay between degeneracy, outage probability, and effective throughput is established, revealing that system performance is governed by the feasibility of the bottleneck user. To further enhance resilience, Degeneracy-Weighted Path Robustness (DWPR) and Functional Substitution Score (FSS) are incorporated to exploit path diversity and functional redundancy. Numerical results show that the proposed framework achieves near-centralized performance in sparse networks, while providing notable throughput gains and improved scalability in dense deployments, highlighting its effectiveness for robust and distributed resource management in next-generation wireless systems.

AI.tldr: A pilot-aware, degeneracy-driven ABM framework for distributed resource allocation in RSMA-enabled multi-user MIMO systems under imperfect CSI and residual SIC error, achieving near-centralized performance in sparse networks and improved scalability in dense deployments.
AI.motivation: To address the challenges of centralized resource allocation in RSMA-enabled MIMO systems under practical impairments (imperfect CSI, residual SIC errors, pilot overhead) by proposing a distributed, scalable framework that captures interference coupling and reliability trade-offs.
AI.method: Reformulates centralized RSMA power allocation as a distributed multi-agent system using Agent-Based Modelling. Introduces a degeneracy index (ratio of target to achieved SINR) as a unified feasibility metric, enabling scalable fixed-point power control without global CSI. Incorporates Degeneracy-Weighted Path Robustness (DWPR) and Functional Substitution Score (FSS) for resilience. Derives analytical outage probability expressions under spatially correlated fading.
AI.result: The framework achieves near-centralized performance in sparse networks and provides notable throughput gains and improved scalability in dense deployments, demonstrating effectiveness for robust distributed resource management.
AI.conclusion: System performance is governed by the feasibility of the bottleneck user. The proposed framework is effective for robust and distributed resource management in next-generation wireless systems.

title: Refining Compositional Diffusion for Reliable Long-Horizon Planning
summary: Compositional diffusion planning generates long-horizon trajectories by stitching together overlapping short-horizon segments through score composition. However, when local plan distributions are multimodal, existing compositional methods suffer from mode-averaging, where averaging incompatible local modes leads to plans that are neither locally feasible nor globally coherent. We propose Refining Compositional Diffusion (RCD), a training-free guidance method that steers compositional sampling toward high-density, globally coherent plans. RCD leverages the self-reconstruction error of a pretrained diffusion model as a proxy for the log-density of composed plans, combined with an overlap consistency term that enforces consistency at segment boundaries. We show that the combined guidance concentrates sampling on high-density plans that mitigate mode-averaging. Experiments on challenging long-horizon tasks from OGBench, including locomotion, object manipulation, and pixel-based observations, demonstrate that RCD consistently outperforms existing methods.

AI.tldr: RCD introduces a training-free guidance method that uses self-reconstruction error and overlap consistency to overcome mode-averaging in compositional diffusion planning for long-horizon trajectories.
AI.motivation: Existing compositional diffusion planning methods suffer from mode-averaging when local plan distributions are multimodal, leading to plans that are neither locally feasible nor globally coherent.
AI.method: RCD steers compositional sampling by combining (1) self-reconstruction error of a pretrained diffusion model as a log-density proxy, and (2) an overlap consistency term enforcing segment boundary alignment.
AI.result: RCD consistently outperforms existing methods on challenging long-horizon tasks from OGBench, including locomotion, object manipulation, and pixel-based observations.
AI.conclusion: The combined guidance concentrates sampling on high-density plans, effectively mitigating mode-averaging without requiring additional training.

title: Reasoning-Guided Grounding: Elevating Video Anomaly Detection through Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Video Anomaly Detection (VAD) has traditionally been framed as binary classification or outlier detection, providing neither interpretable reasoning nor precise spatial localization of anomalous events. While Vision-Language Models (VLMs) offer rich scene understanding, they struggle with reliable spatial grounding - often producing hallucinated or geometrically invalid bounding boxes when asked to localize objects. We propose VANGUARD (Video Anomaly Understanding through Reasoning and Grounding), a framework that unifies anomaly classification, spatial grounding, and chain-of-thought reasoning within a single VLM. VANGUARD introduces a three-stage curriculum that progressively layers training objectives: (1) classifier warmup on frozen backbone features, (2) LoRA-adapted spatial grounding, and (3) chain-of-thought generation. To overcome the sparse annotation typical of VAD benchmarks, we employ a teacher-student annotation pipeline in which a VLM (Qwen3-VL-4B) generates structured per-subclip reasoning trajectories based on manual annotations available from the UCA Dataset. Further, GroundingDINO provides bounding box supervision. On UCF-Crime, VANGUARD achieves 94% ROC-AUC with 84% F1 while simultaneously producing interpretable chain-of-thought explanations and spatial grounding of anomalous objects - capabilities absent from prior VAD methods. Ablations confirm that staged training outperforms monolithic optimization, and that structured reasoning acts as an implicit regularizer yielding more balanced predictions than classification-only fine-tuning. Zero-shot transfer to XD-Violence and ShanghaiTech demonstrates cross-domain generalization without target-domain adaptation.

AI.tldr: VANGUARD unifies anomaly classification, spatial grounding, and chain-of-thought reasoning in a single VLM using a three-stage curriculum training, achieving 94% ROC-AUC on UCF-Crime while providing interpretable explanations and bounding boxes for anomalous objects.
AI.motivation: Traditional VAD methods lack interpretable reasoning and spatial localization; VLMs struggle with reliable spatial grounding (hallucinated/invalid bounding boxes).
AI.method: Three-stage curriculum: (1) classifier warmup on frozen backbone features, (2) LoRA-adapted spatial grounding, (3) chain-of-thought generation. Uses teacher-student annotation pipeline (Qwen3-VL-4B + GroundingDINO) to overcome sparse annotations.
AI.result: 94% ROC-AUC, 84% F1 on UCF-Crime. Staged training outperforms monolithic optimization. Structured reasoning acts as implicit regularizer. Zero-shot transfer to XD-Violence and ShanghaiTech.
AI.conclusion: VANGUARD enables interpretable, spatially-grounded anomaly detection that traditional methods cannot provide, with strong performance and cross-domain generalization.

title: Cyclic codes over the ring Z2[u,v](u2(1+u),v2(1+v2))
summary: In this paper, we describe linear and cyclic codes over the ring Z2[u,v](u2(1+u),v2(1+v2)).

AI.tldr: This paper describes linear and cyclic codes over the ring Z2[u,v]/(u²(1+u), v²(1+v²)).
AI.motivation: To explore coding theory over a specific non-chain ring structure, extending algebraic coding methods.
AI.method: Mathematical construction and analysis of linear and cyclic codes over the quotient ring Z2[u,v]/(u²(1+u), v²(1+v²)).
AI.result: Characterization of linear and cyclic codes over this specific ring.
AI.conclusion: The paper contributes to algebraic coding theory by studying codes over a particular non-chain ring.

title: eOptShrinkQ: Near-Lossless KV Cache Compression Through Optimal Spectral Denoising and Quantization
summary:
We show that the key-value (KV) cache in transformer attention heads admits a natural decomposition into a low-rank \emph{shared context} component and a full-rank \emph{per-token} residual, well described by the spiked random matrix model. This observation leads to eOptShrinkQ, a two-stage compression pipeline: optimal singular value shrinkage (eOptShrink) automatically extracts the shared structure, and the residual -- which satisfies the \emph{thin shell property} with delocalized coordinates -- is quantized by TurboQuant~\citep{zandieh2025turboquant}, a recently proposed per-vector scalar quantizer with near-optimal distortion guarantees. By restoring the isotropy that scalar quantization assumes, spectral denoising eliminates the need for both outlier handling and dedicated inner product bias correction, freeing those bits for improved reconstruction.
  The theoretical grounding in random matrix theory provides three guarantees: automatic rank selection via the BBP phase transition, provably near-zero inner product bias on the residual, and coordinate delocalization ensuring near-optimal quantization distortion. Experimentally, we validate eOptShrinkQ on Llama-3.1-8B and Ministral-8B across three levels: per-head MSE and inner product fidelity, where eOptShrinkQ saves nearly one bit per entry over TurboQuant at equivalent quality; end-to-end on LongBench (16 tasks), where eOptShrinkQ at $\sim$2.2 bits per entry outperforms TurboQuant at 3.0 bits; and multi-needle retrieval, where eOptShrinkQ at 2.2 bits closely matches or exceeds uncompressed FP16, suggesting that spectral denoising can act as a beneficial regularizer for retrieval-intensive tasks.

AI.tldr: eOptShrinkQ is a two-stage KV cache compression pipeline that decomposes attention key-value states into a low-rank shared context (via optimal singular value shrinkage) and a residual quantized by TurboQuant, leveraging random matrix theory for automatic rank selection, near-zero inner product bias, and near-optimal distortion, achieving ~2.2 bits per entry that outperforms TurboQuant at 3.0 bits on LongBench and matches FP16 on retrieval tasks.
AI.motivation: KV cache in transformer attention heads can be decomposed into a low-rank shared context and a full-rank per-token residual, described by the spiked random matrix model. This structural insight enables better compression by separating signal from noise before quantization.
AI.method: Two-stage pipeline: (1) eOptShrink — optimal singular value shrinkage automatically extracts the low-rank shared structure using the BBP phase transition for rank selection. (2) TurboQuant — per-vector scalar quantizer with near-optimal distortion guarantees applied to the residual, which satisfies the thin shell property with delocalized coordinates. Spectral denoising restores isotropy, eliminating the need for outlier handling and inner product bias correction.
AI.result: On Llama-3.1-8B and Ministral-8B: (1) Per-head MSE and inner product fidelity: saves nearly one bit per entry over TurboQuant at equivalent quality. (2) LongBench (16 tasks): ~2.2 bits per entry outperforms TurboQuant at 3.0 bits. (3) Multi-needle retrieval: 2.2 bits closely matches or exceeds uncompressed FP16, suggesting spectral denoising acts as a beneficial regularizer.
AI.conclusion: The theoretical grounding in random matrix theory provides automatic rank selection, provably near-zero inner product bias, and coordinate delocalization for near-optimal quantization distortion. Spectral denoising is a powerful pre-processing step that frees bits for improved reconstruction rather than outlier handling.

title: Stable Agentic Control: Tool-Mediated LLM Architecture for Autonomous Cyber Defense
comment: 23 pages total (9 main paper + 16 appendices/references), 2 figures
summary: Agentic systems involved in high-stake decision-making under adversarial pressure need formal guarantees not offered by existing approaches. Motivated by the operational needs of security operations centers (SOCs) that must configure endpoint detection and response (EDR) policies under adversarial pressure, we present a tool-mediated architecture: LLM agents use deterministic tools (Stackelberg best-response, Bayesian observer updates, attack-graph primitives) and select from finite action catalogs enforced at the tool-output interface. A composite Lyapunov function machine-checked in Lean 4 with zero sorry certifies controllability, observability from asymmetric sensor data, and Input-to-State Stability (ISS) robustness under intelligent adversarial disturbance, with two corollaries extending the certificate to any controller or adversary from the catalogs. On 282 real enterprise attack graphs, the claims hold with margin. On paired offensive/defensive telemetry, a tool-mediated Claude Sonnet 4 controller reduces the attacker's expected payoff (game value) by 59% relative to a deterministic greedy baseline, with zero variance across 40 runs at four temperatures. A Claude Haiku 4.5 controller converges to suboptimal game values but stays catalog-bounded over an additional 40 runs, demonstrating that architectural stability is not dependent on the controller capability. The LLM agent's non-determinism furthers creative exploration of strategies, while the tool-mediated architecture ensures system stability.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a tool-mediated architecture for LLM agents in high-stakes adversarial settings (SOC/EDR), using deterministic tools (Stackelberg games, Bayesian updates, attack graphs) and a Lean 4 machine-checked composite Lyapunov function to certify controllability, observability, and ISS robustness. On 282 real attack graphs, a Claude Sonnet 4 controller reduced attacker payoff by 59% vs. greedy baseline with zero variance, and Claude Haiku 4.5 stayed catalog-bounded, proving stability is independent of controller capability.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches do not offer formal guarantees for agentic systems in high-stakes decision-making under adversarial pressure. Security operations centers (SOCs) need to configure EDR policies with rigorous certification, motivating a tool-mediated architecture that combines LLM creativity with formally verifiable tools.
AI.method: The authors propose a tool-mediated architecture where LLM agents use deterministic tools (Stackelberg best-response, Bayesian observer updates, attack-graph primitives) and select actions from finite catalogs enforced at the tool-output interface. A composite Lyapunov function is machine-checked in Lean 4 with zero sorry to certify controllability, observability, and ISS robustness.
AI.result: On 282 real enterprise attack graphs, claims hold with margin. Claude Sonnet 4 reduces attacker expected payoff by 59% vs. greedy baseline with zero variance across 40 runs at four temperatures. Claude Haiku 4.5 converges to suboptimal values but stays catalog-bounded over 40 runs.
AI.conclusion: Architectural stability does not depend on controller capability. LLM non-determinism enables creative strategy exploration, while the tool-mediated architecture ensures formal stability guarantees. Two corollaries extend the certificate to any controller or adversary from the finite catalogs.

title: deSEO: Physics-Aware Dataset Creation for High-Resolution Satellite Image Shadow Removal
comment: 8 pages, 6 figures, 5 tables. Accepted in the annals track at the ISPRS 2026 Congress. Code and materials: https://github.com/AIT-Assistive-Autonomous-Systems/deSEO
summary: Shadows cast by terrain and tall structures remain a major obstacle for high-resolution satellite image analysis, degrading classification, detection, and 3D reconstruction performance. Public resources offering geometry-consistent paired shadow/shadow-free satellite imagery are essentially missing, and most Earth-observation datasets are designed for shadow detection or 3D modelling rather than removal. Existing deep shadow-removal datasets either target ground-level or aerial scenes or rely on unpaired and weakly supervised formulations rather than explicit satellite pairs. We address this gap with deSEO, a geometry-aware and physics-informed methodology that, to the best of our knowledge, is the first to derive paired supervision for satellite shadow removal from the S-EO shadow detection dataset through a fully replicable pipeline. For each tile, deSEO selects a minimally shadowed acquisition as a weak reference and pairs it with shadowed counterparts using temporal and geometric filtering, Jacobian-based orientation normalisation, and LoFTR-RANSAC registration. A per-pixel validity mask restricts learning to reliably aligned regions, enabling supervision despite residual off-nadir parallax. In addition to this paired dataset, we develop a DSM-aware deshadowing model that combines residual translation, perceptual objectives, and mask-constrained adversarial learning. In contrast, a direct adaptation of a UAV-based SRNet/pix2pix architecture fails to converge under satellite viewpoint variability. Our model consistently reduces the visual impact of cast shadows across diverse illumination and viewing conditions, achieving improved structural and perceptual fidelity on held-out scenes. deSEO therefore provides the first reproducible, geometry-aware paired dataset and baseline for shadow removal in satellite Earth observation.

AI.tldr: deSEO is the first geometry-aware, physics-informed pipeline producing paired shadow/shadow-free satellite imagery for deep shadow removal, including a novel DSM-aware deshadowing model that outperforms adapted UAV-based methods.
AI.motivation: Cast shadows from terrain and tall structures degrade satellite image analysis (classification, detection, 3D reconstruction), yet no public resource offers geometry-consistent paired shadow/shadow-free satellite imagery for shadow removal.
AI.method: deSEO derives paired supervision from S-EO shadow detection dataset via temporal/geometric filtering, Jacobian-based orientation normalization, LoFTR-RANSAC registration, and per-pixel validity masks; plus a DSM-aware deshadowing model with residual translation, perceptual objectives, and mask-constrained adversarial learning.
AI.result: The proposed model consistently reduces visual impact of cast shadows across diverse illumination/viewing conditions, achieving improved structural and perceptual fidelity on held-out scenes, while UAV-based SRNet/pix2pix adaptation fails to converge.
AI.conclusion: deSEO provides the first reproducible, geometry-aware paired dataset and baseline for satellite shadow removal, filling a critical gap in Earth observation.

title: Phy2-ExposNet: A Physics-Informed Neural Network for EMF Exposure Mapping in Complex Urban Environments
summary: Accurate electromagnetic field (EMF) exposure mapping is critical for wireless network planning, environmental monitoring, and the deployment of next generation communication systems. The mapping results can be converted into the form of a radio map, a key technology in digital twin communication systems, used to describe the wireless signal propagation characteristics at every location in a specific area. Existing deep learning approaches treat propagation estimation as a pure regression problem and do not enforce physical consistency in the predicted fields. In this paper, we propose Phy2-ExposNet, a novel neural network framework that decouples exposure mapping into a physics-informed estimation stage and a transformer-based residual refinement stage. It first estimates the fields under two physical constraints and then refines the resulting exposure map by capturing long range interactions and complex spatial propagation patterns. Experiments demonstrate that the proposed method achieves lower estimation error while significantly reducing model complexity compared to existing approaches. It achieves around 15% relative error reduction over strong baselines, while using over 80% fewer parameters than conventional physics-informed models. Ablation results further reveal that the physics-informed design is crucial for capturing complex propagation effects, particularly in boundary and shadow regions.

AI.tldr: Phy2-ExposNet is a physics-informed neural network for EMF exposure mapping that uses a two-stage approach (physics estimation + transformer refinement), achieving 15% lower error with 80% fewer parameters than existing models.
AI.motivation: Accurate EMF exposure mapping is critical for wireless network planning and digital twin communication systems, but existing deep learning methods treat propagation estimation as pure regression and lack physical consistency enforcement.
AI.method: A two-stage neural network framework: (1) physics-informed estimation stage under two physical constraints, (2) transformer-based residual refinement stage capturing long-range interactions and complex spatial propagation patterns.
AI.result: Achieves ~15% relative error reduction over strong baselines while using >80% fewer parameters than conventional physics-informed models. Physics-informed design is crucial for boundary and shadow regions.
AI.conclusion: Phy2-ExposNet effectively combines physical consistency with efficient deep learning, outperforming existing approaches with significantly lower model complexity.

title: Benchmarking Local Language Models for Social Robots using Edge Devices
comment: Accepted for 22nd IEEE International Conference on Advanced Robotics and its Social Impact (June 2026) in Vienna, Austria
summary:
Social-educational robots designed for socially interactive pedagogical support, such as the Robot Study Companion (RSC), rely on responsive, privacy-preserving interaction despite severely limited compute. However, there is a gap in systematic benchmarking of language models for edge computing in pedagogical applications. This paper benchmarks 25 open-source language models for local deployment on edge hardware. We evaluate each model across three dimensions: inference efficiency (tokens per second, energy consumption), general knowledge (a six-category MMLU subset), and teaching effectiveness (LLM-rated pedagogical quality), validated against five independent human raters using the Raspberry Pi(RPi)4 as the primary platform, with additional comparisons on the RPi5 and a laptop GPU.
  Results reveal pronounced trade-offs: throughput and energy efficiency vary by over an order of magnitude across models, MMLU accuracy ranges from near-random to 57.2%, and teaching effectiveness does not correlate monotonically with either metric. Among the evaluated models, Granite4 Tiny Hybrid (7B) achieves a strong overall balance, reaching 2.5 tokens per second, 0.90 tokens per joule, and 54.6% MMLU accuracy; high MMLU accuracy does not appear necessary for strong teaching scores. Human validation on four representative models preserved the automated rank ordering (Pearson r = 0.967, n = 4). Based on these findings, we propose a three-tier local inference architecture for the RSC that balances responsiveness and accuracy on resource-constrained hardware.

AI.tldr: This paper benchmarks 25 open-source language models on edge hardware (Raspberry Pi) for educational robots, evaluating inference efficiency, general knowledge (MMLU), and teaching effectiveness. Results show massive performance trade-offs and propose a three-tier inference architecture for the Robot Study Companion.
AI.motivation: There is a gap in systematic benchmarking of language models for edge computing in pedagogical applications, despite the need for privacy-preserving, responsive interaction in social-educational robots like the Robot Study Companion (RSC).
AI.method: 25 open-source language models were benchmarked on Raspberry Pi 4 (with additional RPi5 and laptop GPU comparisons) across three dimensions: inference efficiency (tokens/sec, energy), general knowledge (MMLU subset), and teaching effectiveness (LLM-rated pedagogical quality validated against 5 human raters).
AI.result: Throughput and energy vary by over an order of magnitude; MMLU accuracy ranges from near-random to 57.2%; teaching effectiveness does not correlate monotonically with efficiency or accuracy. Granite4 Tiny Hybrid (7B) achieves the best balance (2.5 t/s, 0.90 t/J, 54.6% MMLU). Human validation preserved automated rank ordering (Pearson r=0.967, n=4).
AI.conclusion: A three-tier local inference architecture is proposed for the RSC to balance responsiveness and accuracy on resource-constrained hardware.

title: Approaching human parity in the quality of automated organoid image segmentation
summary:
Organoids are complex, three dimensional, self-organizing cell cultures which manifest organ-like features and represent a powerful platform for studying human disease and developing treatment options. Organoid development is characterized by dynamic morphological and cellular organization, which mimic some aspects of organ development. To study these rapid changes over the course of organoid development, advanced imaging and analytical tools are critical to accurately monitor the trajectory of organoid growth and investigate disease processes.
  In this work, we focus on computer vision and machine learning techniques to automatically measure the size and shape of developing spheroids derived from pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs), which are typically the starting material for generating organoid cultures. To facilitate this task, we introduce a composite method that combines the Segment Anything Model (SAM), a general-purpose foundation model, with an existing domain-specific tool. This composite method is evaluated together with several existing tools by testing them on organoid image data and comparing with the results of manual image segmentation. We find that no single existing tool is able to segment the test images with sufficient accuracy across all test conditions, but the newly introduced composite method produces consistent and accurate results for all but a very small fraction of the most challenging images. Finally, we compare the accuracy of this method to the variability between manual segmentations by independent annotators (inter-observer variability) and find that by one measure it performs at the level of inter-observer variability and by others it performs very close to it.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a composite method combining SAM with a domain-specific tool to automatically measure the size and shape of developing spheroids from iPSCs. The method outperforms existing tools and approaches inter-observer variability accuracy.
AI.motivation: Organoid development involves rapid morphological changes that require advanced imaging and analytical tools. Accurate automated measurement of spheroid size and shape is needed to monitor organoid growth and study disease processes, but existing tools lack sufficient accuracy across all conditions.
AI.method: A composite method combining Segment Anything Model (SAM) with an existing domain-specific tool for segmenting spheroids in organoid image data. Evaluation against existing tools and manual segmentation by multiple annotators.
AI.result: No single existing tool achieved sufficient accuracy across all test conditions. The composite method produced consistent and accurate results for all but the most challenging images, and performed at or very close to inter-observer variability levels.
AI.conclusion: The composite SAM-based approach offers a reliable automated solution for spheroid segmentation, performing near human-level accuracy, which can facilitate high-throughput organoid analysis.

title: Skew polycyclic over finite chain rings associated to trinomials
summary: This work studies skew polycyclic codes over finite chain rings defined by central trinomials. For this class of codes, we investigate Hamming equivalence in the non-commutative (skew) setting. We introduce an equivalence relation on the defining trinomials and demonstrate that it admits a group-theoretic characterization in terms of a group of binomials equipped with the Schur multiplication. We determine the conditions under which skew polycyclic codes are Hamming equivalent to those defined by the specific trinomial $x^n-(x^\ell+1)$. This reduces the classification problem for these codes, up to Hamming equivalence, to a canonical case. Finally, we determine the size of the corresponding equivalence class using the decomposition of the unit group of the underlying chain ring.

AI.tldr: This paper studies skew polycyclic codes over finite chain rings defined by central trinomials, introduces an equivalence relation on trinomials with group-theoretic characterization using Schur multiplication, reduces classification to a canonical trinomial case, and determines the equivalence class size via unit group decomposition.
AI.motivation: To classify skew polycyclic codes over finite chain rings up to Hamming equivalence by establishing equivalence relations on defining central trinomials and reducing the classification problem to a canonical form.
AI.method: Introduced an equivalence relation on defining trinomials with group-theoretic characterization using a group of binomials under Schur multiplication; analyzed conditions for Hamming equivalence to the canonical trinomial $x^n-(x^\ell+1)$; used unit group decomposition of the underlying chain ring to determine equivalence class sizes.
AI.result: Demonstrated that Hamming equivalence of skew polycyclic codes can be reduced to a canonical trinomial case $x^n-(x^\ell+1)$, and derived the size of the equivalence class via decomposition of the unit group of the finite chain ring.
AI.conclusion: The classification of skew polycyclic codes up to Hamming equivalence is simplified to a canonical form, with the equivalence class size determined algebraically through unit group analysis.

title: An End-to-End Framework for Building Large Language Models for Software Operations
summary: In the field of software operations, Large Language Models (LLMs) have attracted increasing attention. However, existing research has not yet achieved efficient and effective end-to-end intelligent operations due to low-quality data, fragmented knowledge and insufficient learning. To explore the potential of LLMs in software operations, we propose OpsLLM, a domain-specific LLM that supports both knowledge-based question answering (QA) and root cause analysis (RCA). Moreover, we disclose the detailed workflow for building LLMs specifically in the software operations domain. First, a Human-in-the-Loop mechanism is introduced to curate highquality data from a large collection of operational raw data and construct a fine-tuning dataset. Then, based on the data, supervised fine-tuning is conducted to achieve a base model. Furthermore, we introduce a domain process reward model (DPRM) during the reinforcement learning stage to optimize the accuracy and reliability of the fine-tuned model on RCA tasks. Experimental results on the tasks with diverse difficulties demonstrate that OpsLLMs effectively learns and aligns with the operational domain knowledge infused, outperforming existing open-source and closed-source LLMs in accuracy with improvements of 0.2%~5.7% on QA tasks and 2.7% ~70.3% on RCA tasks, while exhibiting strong transferability. Moreover, we will open-source three versions of OpsLLM with 7B, 14B and 32B parameters, along with a 15K fine-tuning dataset.

AI.tldr: OpsLLM is a domain-specific large language model for software operations, designed to handle knowledge-based QA and root cause analysis. It uses a Human-in-the-Loop mechanism for data curation, supervised fine-tuning, and a domain process reward model (DPRM) for reinforcement learning. Results show OpsLLM outperforms existing models on QA (0.2%-5.7%) and RCA (2.7%-70.3%) tasks, with open-source releases at 7B, 14B, and 32B scales.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM research in software operations suffers from low-quality data, fragmented knowledge, and insufficient learning, and has not yet achieved efficient end-to-end intelligent operations.
AI.method: OpsLLM pipeline: (1) Human-in-the-Loop mechanism for high-quality data curation from raw operational data; (2) Supervised fine-tuning to build a base model; (3) Domain process reward model (DPRM) for reinforcement learning to improve accuracy and reliability on RCA tasks.
AI.result: OpsLLM outperforms existing open-source and closed-source LLMs: 0.2%-5.7% improvement on QA tasks and 2.7%-70.3% improvement on RCA tasks, with strong transferability across tasks of varying difficulty.
AI.conclusion: OpsLLM effectively learns and aligns with operational domain knowledge, achieving superior performance. The authors will open-source three model versions (7B, 14B, 32B) and a 15K fine-tuning dataset to support further research.

title: Computing Thiele Rules on Interval Elections and their Generalizations
summary:
Approval-based committee voting has received significant attention in the social choice community. Among the studied rules, Thiele rules, and especially Proportional Approval Voting (PAV), stand out for desirable properties such as proportional representation, Pareto optimality, and support monotonicity. Their main drawback is that computing a Thiele outcome is NP-hard in general.
  A glimpse of hope comes from the fact that Thiele rules are better behaved under structured preferences. On the candidate interval (CI) domain, they are computable in polynomial time via a linear program (LP) that has a totally unimodular constraint matrix. Surprisingly, this approach fails for the related voter interval (VI) domain, and the complexity of the problem has repeatedly been posed as an open question. Our main result resolves this question: although the relevant matrix is not totally unimodular, the ``standard'' LP still admits at least one optimal integral solution, and we provide a fast algorithm for finding it.
  Our technique naturally extends to the voter-candidate interval (VCI) domain, also known as the 1-dimensional voter-candidate range (1D-VCR) domain, and to the linearly consistent (LC) domain, both of which generalize the candidate and voter interval domains. Although both the VCI and LC domains have been studied in social choice, their relationship was unknown. We show, through connections to graph theory, that LC strictly contains VCI. We also provide an alternative definition of LC that is closer in spirit to VCI and has a natural interpretation in approval elections; this equivalence may be of independent interest. Finally, we study an alternative tree-based generalization of VCI and show that Thiele rules become NP-hard to compute on this domain.

AI.tldr: This paper resolves the open question of computing Thiele rules (like PAV) on voter interval (VI) domains by showing the standard LP still yields optimal integral solutions despite the matrix not being totally unimodular. It extends results to VCI, LC domains, and shows NP-hardness on a tree-based generalization.
AI.motivation: Thiele rules (especially PAV) have desirable properties like proportional representation and Pareto optimality, but computing them is NP-hard in general. The complexity on voter interval (VI) domains was an open question that this paper resolves.
AI.method: The authors show that while the constraint matrix for VI domain is not totally unimodular, the standard LP still has at least one optimal integral solution, and provide a fast algorithm to find it. They extend this to VCI and LC domains, and establish that LC strictly contains VCI via graph theory connections.
AI.result: 1) Resolves the open problem: Thiele rules on VI domain are computable in polynomial time via LP. 2) Extends to VCI and LC domains. 3) Shows LC strictly contains VCI. 4) Provides alternative definition of LC. 5) Shows Thiele rules become NP-hard on a tree-based generalization of VCI.
AI.conclusion: Thiele rules are tractable on structured preference domains (CI, VI, VCI, LC) but become NP-hard again on a tree-based generalization, establishing a complexity frontier.

title: Canonical Optimization for MIMO MAC Design
summary: Resource allocation in the multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) multiple access channel (MAC) is a fundamental problem in multiuser communications, yet it is increasingly treated as non-convex and computationally intractable. This has motivated a large body of heuristic machine learning and successive-approximation methods. Results here show that the MIMO MAC admits canonical convex formulations and present four solvers that together characterize its capacity region. maxRMAC performs weighted sum-rate maximization under per-user energy constraints, minPMAC finds the minimum weighted energy required to support target rates, maxRESMAC performs weighted sum-rate maximization under a total energy constraint, and admMAC tests rate-region feasibility. The solvers exploit the polymatroid structure of the MAC rate region and the separability of the dual Lagrangian across frequency tones, which reduces the problem to parallel per-tone covariance optimizations solved via limited-memory Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno (L-BFGS) over Cholesky-like covariance factors. Experiments on spatially correlated MIMO orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing (OFDM) channels show that the proposed solvers match a commercial convex solver in solution quality while running up to two orders of magnitude faster and scaling to regimes where the commercial solver times out. Through broadcast channel (BC) to MAC duality, the same solvers also enable optimal precoder design for the MIMO BC. All solvers are open-sourced and available at https://github.com/muhd-umer/canonical-mac.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that MIMO MAC resource allocation can be formulated as convex problems, and presents four fast solvers (maxRMAC, minPMAC, maxRESMAC, admMAC) that exploit polymatroid structure and Lagrangian separability to achieve up to 100× speedup over commercial solvers while matching solution quality.
AI.motivation: Resource allocation in MIMO MAC is a fundamental problem in multiuser communications, but is increasingly treated as non-convex and computationally intractable, leading to heuristic ML and successive-approximation methods.
AI.method: The authors show MIMO MAC admits canonical convex formulations and present four solvers exploiting polymatroid structure and dual Lagrangian separability across frequency tones, reducing to parallel per-tone covariance optimizations via L-BFGS over Cholesky-like factors.
AI.result: Experiments on spatially correlated MIMO-OFDM channels show the solvers match a commercial convex solver in solution quality while running up to two orders of magnitude faster and scaling to regimes where the commercial solver times out.
AI.conclusion: The MIMO MAC resource allocation problem is convex, not non-convex, and the proposed open-sourced solvers provide fast, scalable solutions that also work for MIMO BC via duality.

title: Robust Path Tracking for Vehicles via Continuous-Time Residual Learning: An ICODE-MPPI Approach
summary: Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) control is a powerful sampling-based strategy for nonlinear autonomous systems. However, its performance is often bottlenecked by the fidelity of nominal dynamics. We propose ICODE-MPPI, a robust framework that leverages Input Concomitant Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (ICODEs) to learn and compensate for unmodeled residual dynamics. Unlike discrete-time learners, ICODEs maintain physical consistency and temporal continuity during the MPPI prediction horizon. High-fidelity simulations on complex trajectories demonstrate that ICODE-MPPI achieves up to a 69\% reduction in cross-tracking error under persistent disturbances compared to standard MPPI control. Furthermore, our analysis confirms that ICODE-MPPI significantly suppresses control chattering, yielding smoother steering commands and superior robust performance.

AI.tldr: ICODE-MPPI uses Input Concomitant Neural ODEs to learn residual dynamics, reducing cross-tracking error by up to 69% and suppressing control chattering compared to standard MPPI control.
AI.motivation: Standard MPPI control performance is bottlenecked by the fidelity of nominal dynamics, especially under unmodeled residual dynamics and persistent disturbances.
AI.method: The framework integrates Input Concomitant Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (ICODEs) into MPPI to learn and compensate for unmodeled residual dynamics while maintaining physical consistency and temporal continuity.
AI.result: Up to 69% reduction in cross-tracking error under persistent disturbances; significant suppression of control chattering and smoother steering commands.
AI.conclusion: ICODE-MPPI is a robust control framework that outperforms standard MPPI by learning residual dynamics through continuous-time neural ODEs, achieving superior robustness and smoother control.

title: Learning to Segment using Summary Statistics and Weak Supervision
summary: Medical experts often manually segment images to obtain diagnostic statistics and discard the resulting annotations. We aim to train segmentation models to alleviate this burden, but constrained to the retained summary statistics (e.g., the area of the annotated region). Empirical results suggest that statistics alone are insufficient for this task, but adding weak information in the form of a few pixels within the area of interest significantly improves performance. We use a novel loss function that combines terms for image reconstruction quality, matching to summary statistics, and overlap between the predicted foreground and the weak supervisory signal. Experiments on standard image, ultrasound (breast cancer), and Computed Tomography (CT) scan (kidney tumors) data demonstrate the utility and potential of the approach.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a method to train segmentation models using only summary statistics (like area) and a few labeled pixels, instead of full manual annotations, achieving improved performance through a novel loss function combining reconstruction quality, statistics matching, and weak signal overlap.
AI.motivation: Medical experts manually segment images for diagnostic statistics but discard the annotations. The goal is to train segmentation models using only the retained summary statistics (e.g., area) to reduce annotation burden.
AI.method: A novel loss function combining three terms: (1) image reconstruction quality, (2) matching to summary statistics, and (3) overlap between predicted foreground and a weak supervisory signal (a few labeled pixels).
AI.result: Summary statistics alone are insufficient for training segmentation models, but adding weak information (a few pixels within the area of interest) significantly improves performance. Validated on standard images, ultrasound (breast cancer), and CT scan (kidney tumors) data.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively leverages minimal supervision (summary statistics + a few pixels) for medical image segmentation, showing promise for reducing manual annotation workloads.

title: Single-Period Portfolio Selection via Information Projection
summary: We study the single-period portfolio selection problem under Constant Relative Risk-Aversion (CRRA) utility through the information-theoretic lens. Assuming only that the market payoff vector has finite support, we show that the Certainty-Equivalent (CE) growth rate under CRRA utility can be exactly decomposed into a portfolio-induced Rényi divergence term, a Rényi entropy term of the risk-tilted market law, and a log-partition term. In this setting, the Rényi order has a clear operational meaning: it exactly coincides with the investor's coefficient of relative risk aversion. We further show that CRRA portfolio selection is equivalent to a Rényi information-projection problem. Using a variational representation of Rényi divergence, we obtain a Blahut-Arimoto-style alternating optimization with a closed-form auxiliary update and a KL-type portfolio step. In the low risk-aversion regime, this method empirically requires fewer iterations than both direct CRRA utility optimization and Cover's method.

AI.tldr: This paper links CRRA portfolio selection to Rényi divergence and entropy, showing risk aversion equals Rényi order, and proposes a Blahut-Arimoto-style optimization method that is more efficient in low risk-aversion regimes.
AI.motivation: To connect single-period CRRA portfolio selection with information-theoretic concepts (Rényi divergence, Rényi entropy), providing a deeper theoretical understanding and more efficient optimization algorithms.
AI.method: The authors derive an exact decomposition of CRRA certainty-equivalent growth rate into Rényi divergence, Rényi entropy, and log-partition terms. They reformulate portfolio selection as a Rényi information-projection problem and develop a Blahut-Arimoto-style alternating optimization with closed-form auxiliary updates and KL-type portfolio steps.
AI.result: The Rényi order parameter is shown to coincide exactly with the investor's coefficient of relative risk aversion. The proposed alternating optimization method empirically requires fewer iterations than direct CRRA optimization and Cover's method in the low risk-aversion regime.
AI.conclusion: The information-theoretic perspective on CRRA portfolio selection yields both theoretical insights (operational meaning of Rényi order) and practical algorithmic improvements, particularly for low risk-aversion settings.

title: On the Invariants of Softmax Attention
summary:
Softmax attention maps every query--key interaction into a probability distribution, but the underlying structure remains largely unexplored. We define the \emph{energy field}, the row-centered attention logit, and show that it exhibits invariant properties across models, architectures, and inputs.
  Two classes of invariants emerge. \emph{Mechanism-level} invariants follow from the algebraic structure of softmax attention. They include a per-row zero-sum constraint, a rank bound determined by the head dimension, and spectral signatures that follow from them. \emph{Model-level} regularities are not required by the mechanism, yet hold in every autoregressive language model we test, spanning several architecture families. The energy field distributes its variance over key positions without concentrating at a few. This delocalization traces to a property of the key matrix we call \emph{key incoherence}.
  These invariants have practical consequences. The rank bound confines the energy field to a low-dimensional subspace. Key incoherence yields a per-head training monitor. All results are verified at multiple context lengths and input texts.

AI.tldr: This paper discovers invariant properties of the energy field (row-centered attention logits) in softmax attention, including mechanism-level invariants (zero-sum constraint, rank bound by head dimension, spectral signatures) and model-level regularities (variance delocalization due to key incoherence), with practical implications like a rank bound and a training monitor.
