AI.motivation: While softmax attention maps query-key interactions into probability distributions, the underlying structure of attention logits remains largely unexplored. The authors aim to uncover invariant properties that hold across models, architectures, and inputs.
AI.method: The authors define the energy field as row-centered attention logits, then mathematically derive mechanism-level invariants from softmax attention's algebraic structure. They empirically verify model-level regularities (variance delocalization, key incoherence) across multiple autoregressive language model families, context lengths, and input texts.
AI.result: Two classes of invariants found: (1) Mechanism-level: per-row zero-sum constraint, rank bound determined by head dimension, and spectral signatures. (2) Model-level: energy field variance is delocalized over key positions due to key incoherence. The rank bound confines energy to a low-dimensional subspace, and key incoherence provides a per-head training monitor.
AI.conclusion: Softmax attention exhibits structured invariants beyond probability distributions, with practical consequences for understanding attention mechanism limitations and monitoring training dynamics.

title: Making the Invisible Visible: Understanding the Mismatch Between Organizational Goals and Worker Experiences in AI Adoption
summary: While AI is often introduced into organizations to drive innovation and efficiency, many adoption efforts fail as workers resist and struggle to integrate these systems. These failures point to a deeper issue: workers, the very people expected to collaborate with AI, are often invisible in decisions about how AI is designed and used. Drawing on interviews with professionals who interact with AI systems daily in healthcare, finance, and management, we examine the disconnect between organizational expectations and worker experiences. We identify key barriers, including poor usability and interoperability, misaligned expectations, limited control, and insufficient communication. These challenges highlight a gap between how organizations implement AI and the evolving worker needs, tasks, and workflows that it fails to support. We argue that successful adoption requires recognizing workers as central to AI integration and propose adaptation strategies at the individual, task, and organizational levels to better align AI systems with real-world practices.

AI.tldr: The paper examines why AI adoption fails in organizations, finding that workers are often excluded from AI design decisions. Through interviews in healthcare, finance, and management, it identifies key barriers (poor usability, misaligned expectations, limited control) and argues that successful AI integration requires centering workers' needs through adaptation strategies at individual, task, and organizational levels.
AI.motivation: To understand why AI adoption efforts fail despite promises of innovation and efficiency, particularly focusing on the overlooked role of workers who are expected to collaborate with these systems.
AI.method: Qualitative interviews with professionals who interact with AI systems daily across healthcare, finance, and management sectors.
AI.result: Identified key barriers: poor usability and interoperability, misaligned expectations, limited worker control, and insufficient communication between organizational decisions and worker experiences.
AI.conclusion: Successful AI adoption requires recognizing workers as central to integration and implementing multi-level adaptation strategies (individual, task, organizational) to align AI systems with real-world practices.

title: Copula-Based Analysis of Fluid Antenna-Assisted Over-the-Air Computation
summary: This letter studies an uplink over-the-air computation (AirComp) framework in which multiple user equipments are equipped with fluid-antenna (FA) arrays and operate over spatially correlated fading channels. By explicitly modeling channel dependence using the Gumbel copula, closed-form analytical expressions are derived for the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the mean-squared error (MSE) of the aggregated function. The proposed analysis provides a quantitative performance characterization of AirComp under spatial correlation and provides analytical insights into the role of FA-assisted transmission in correlated wireless environments. Numerical results validate the derived expressions and show that FA deployment can substantially reduce the MSE compared with conventional fixed-antenna systems, although the achievable gain decreases as spatial correlation becomes stronger.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes an uplink AirComp system with FA arrays and spatially correlated fading, using Gumbel copula to model channel dependence. Closed-form MSE CDF expressions are derived, showing FA deployment reduces MSE compared to fixed antennas, with gains diminishing under stronger spatial correlation.
AI.motivation: To quantitatively characterize the performance of over-the-air computation (AirComp) with fluid-antenna (FA) arrays under spatially correlated fading channels, and to understand the role of FA-assisted transmission in correlated wireless environments.
AI.method: The authors model channel dependence using the Gumbel copula and derive closed-form analytical expressions for the CDF of the MSE of the aggregated function in an uplink AirComp system with FA arrays.
AI.result: FA deployment substantially reduces MSE compared with conventional fixed-antenna systems; however, the achievable gain decreases as spatial correlation becomes stronger.
AI.conclusion: The derived expressions are validated by numerical results, confirming the performance benefits of FA systems in AirComp, while highlighting the limiting effect of spatial correlation on the achievable gain.

title: RLDX-1 Technical Report
summary: While Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs) have shown remarkable progress toward human-like generalist robotic policies through the versatile intelligence (i.e. broad scene understanding and language-conditioned generalization) inherited from pre-trained Vision-Language Models, they still struggle with complex real-world tasks requiring broader functional capabilities (e.g. motion awareness, memory-aware decision making, and physical sensing). To address this, we introduce RLDX-1, a general-purpose robotic policy for dexterous manipulation built on the Multi-Stream Action Transformer (MSAT), an architecture that unifies these capabilities by integrating heterogeneous modalities through modality-specific streams with cross-modal joint self-attention. RLDX-1 further combines this architecture with system-level design choices, including synthesizing training data for rare manipulation scenarios, learning procedures specialized for human-like manipulation, and inference optimizations for real-time deployment. Through empirical evaluation, we show that RLDX-1 consistently outperforms recent frontier VLAs (e.g. $π_{0.5}$ and GR00T N1.6) across both simulation benchmarks and real-world tasks that require broad functional capabilities beyond general versatility. In particular, RLDX-1 shows superiority in ALLEX humanoid tasks by achieving success rates of 86.8% while $π_{0.5}$ and GR00T N1.6 achieve around 40%, highlighting the ability of RLDX-1 to control a high-DoF humanoid robot under diverse functional demands. Together, these results position RLDX-1 as a promising step toward reliable VLAs for complex, contact-rich, and dynamic real-world dexterous manipulation.

AI.tldr: RLDX-1 is a robotic policy for dexterous manipulation that uses a Multi-Stream Action Transformer (MSAT) to integrate heterogeneous sensory modalities, outperforming existing VLAs (π₀.₅, GR00T N1.6) notably on ALLEX humanoid tasks (86.8% vs ~40% success rates).
AI.motivation: Current VLAs struggle with complex real-world tasks requiring broader functional capabilities beyond general scene understanding, such as motion awareness, memory-aware decision making, and physical sensing.
AI.method: RLDX-1 uses the Multi-Stream Action Transformer (MSAT), which integrates heterogeneous modalities through modality-specific streams with cross-modal joint self-attention, along with system-level designs like synthetic data for rare scenarios, learning procedures for human-like manipulation, and inference optimizations for real-time deployment.
AI.result: RLDX-1 consistently outperforms π₀.₅ and GR00T N1.6 across simulation and real-world tasks, achieving 86.8% success rate on ALLEX humanoid tasks compared to ~40% for the baselines.
AI.conclusion: RLDX-1 is a promising step toward reliable VLAs for complex, contact-rich, and dynamic real-world dexterous manipulation.

title: One Sequence to Segment Them All: Efficient Data Augmentation for CT and MRI Cross-Domain 3D Spine Segmentation
summary: Deep learning-based medical image segmentation is increasingly used to support clinical diagnosis and develop new treatment strategies. However, model performance remains limited by the scarcity of high-quality annotated data and insufficient generalization across imaging protocols. This limitation is particularly evident in MRI and CT, where models are typically trained on a single acquisition sequence and exhibit reduced robustness when applied to unseen sequences or contrasts. Although data augmentation is widely used to improve general robustness on medical images, its impact on cross-modality generalization has not been quantitatively explored. In this work, we study a targeted set of data augmentation techniques designed to improve cross-modality transfer. We train three spine segmentation models, each on a single-modality/sequence dataset, and evaluate them across seven out-of-distribution datasets (spanning CT and MRI), reflecting a realistic single-sequence training and multi-sequence/contrast/modality deployment scenario. Our results demonstrate substantial performance gains on unseen domains (average Dice gain of 155 %) while preserving in-domain accuracy (average Dice decrease of 0.008 %), including effective transfer between CT and MRI. To mitigate the computational cost typically associated with strong data augmentation, we implement GPU-optimized augmentations that maintain, and even improve, training efficiency by approximately 10 %. We release our approach as an open-source toolbox, enabling seamless integration into commonly used frameworks such as nnUNet and MONAI. These augmentations significantly enhance robustness to heterogeneous clinical imaging scenarios without compromising training speed.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that targeted GPU-optimized data augmentation significantly improves cross-modality generalization (155% average Dice gain on unseen domains) for medical image segmentation models trained on single-modality data, with minimal in-domain accuracy loss (0.008%) and 10% training efficiency improvement.
AI.motivation: Medical image segmentation models struggle with generalization across different imaging modalities (CT and MRI) and acquisition sequences due to scarce high-quality annotated data. The impact of data augmentation on cross-modality generalization has not been quantitatively explored.
AI.method: The authors train three spine segmentation models on single-modality/sequence datasets, apply a targeted set of GPU-optimized data augmentation techniques, and evaluate across seven out-of-distribution datasets spanning CT and MRI. They implement efficient GPU-optimized augmentations and release an open-source toolbox compatible with nnUNet and MONAI.
AI.result: Average Dice gain of 155% on unseen domains, effective CT-to-MRI transfer, only 0.008% average Dice decrease on in-domain data, and approximately 10% training efficiency improvement.
AI.conclusion: GPU-optimized data augmentation significantly enhances robustness to heterogeneous clinical imaging scenarios without compromising training speed, enabling practical cross-modality deployment.

title: Generalized Function-Correcting Partition Codes
summary: We introduce generalized function-correcting partition codes (GFCPCs) that simultaneously protect multiple partitions of the message space against different numbers of errors. Given partitions with respective distance requirements, a GFCPC is a systematic encoding that guarantees, for each partition, a specified minimum Hamming distance between codewords whose messages lie in different blocks. This framework unifies and generalizes both function-correcting partition codes, which protect multiple functions with a common error-correction level, and function-correcting codes with data protection, which assign different levels of protection to data and a single function. We present a multi-step construction procedure for these codes and demonstrate it with some examples. We derive general upper and lower bounds on the optimal redundancy, including the upper bound which considers the join of different combinations of the partitions. We define the distance requirement matrix $\mathcal{D}$ for the GFCPCs and use it to characterize the optimal redundancy in terms of the shortest length of an associated $\mathcal{D}$-code. For two partitions of message space over the binary field, we establish improved lower bounds on the optimal redundancy under specific neighborhood conditions on the partitions. Through several examples, we demonstrate that the proposed framework can yield strictly smaller redundancy than both the sum of the individual FCPC redundancies and the redundancy of a single FCPC designed for the join partition with the highest distance (strongest protection required).

AI.tldr: This paper introduces generalized function-correcting partition codes (GFCPCs) that protect multiple partitions of the message space against different numbers of errors, unifying several previous coding frameworks. It provides construction methods, bounds on optimal redundancy, and demonstrates cases where GFCPCs outperform existing approaches.
AI.motivation: To unify and generalize existing coding frameworks (function-correcting partition codes and function-correcting codes with data protection) by enabling simultaneous protection of multiple message partitions with different error-correction requirements, achieving better redundancy efficiency.
AI.method: The authors define GFCPCs with systematic encoding that ensures minimum Hamming distance between codewords from different blocks of each partition. They propose a multi-step construction procedure, derive upper/lower bounds on optimal redundancy using a distance requirement matrix D, and analyze improved lower bounds for binary field with two partitions under specific neighborhood conditions.
AI.result: The framework yields strictly smaller redundancy than both (1) the sum of individual FCPC redundancies and (2) a single FCPC designed for the join partition with the highest distance requirement. The optimal redundancy is characterized via the shortest length of an associated D-code.
AI.conclusion: GFCPCs provide a more efficient and unified approach for protecting multiple message partitions with heterogeneous error-correction needs, with demonstrated redundancy gains over existing methods.

title: Delay, Plateau, or Collapse: Evaluating the Impact of Systematic Verification Error on RLVR
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has become a powerful approach for improving the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs). While RLVR is designed for tasks with verifiable ground-truth answers, real-world verifiers (e.g., static code checkers) can introduce errors into the reward signal. Prior analyses have largely treated such errors as random and independent across samples, concluding that errors merely slow training with limited effect on final performance. However, practical verifiers tend to exhibit systematic errors. This introduces a risk of models learning unwanted consistent behavior from a structurally incorrect reward signal. In this work, we study the impact of such systematic verification errors on RLVR. Through controlled experiments on arithmetic tasks, we show that systematic false negatives lead to similar effects as random noise. On the other hand, systematic false positives can cause a wide range of behaviors from sub-optimal plateaus to performance collapse. Crucially, these outcomes are not determined by the overall error rate but by the specific pattern of introduced errors, making pre-hoc mitigation difficult. Our results show that, in contrast to prior conclusions, realistic verification errors can critically shape RLVR outcomes and that verifier quality has to be understood beyond its sample-level error rate.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that systematic verifier errors, especially false positives, can critically degrade RLVR performance, contrary to prior work claiming errors only slow training.
AI.motivation: Prior work treats verifier errors as random and independent, concluding they only slow training. However, real-world verifiers (e.g., static code checkers) exhibit systematic errors, whose impact on RLVR is poorly understood.
AI.method: Controlled experiments on arithmetic tasks where systematic false negatives and false positives are injected into the reward signal, comparing their effects to random noise.
AI.result: Systematic false negatives behave similarly to random noise. Systematic false positives cause severe issues (sub-optimal plateaus to performance collapse), and outcomes depend on error patterns, not just overall error rate.
AI.conclusion: Realistic systematic verification errors can critically shape RLVR outcomes; verifier quality must be assessed beyond sample-level error rates.

title: Programmatic Context Augmentation for LLM-based Symbolic Regression
summary: Symbolic regression (SR), the task of discovering mathematical expressions that best describe a given dataset, remains a fundamental challenge in scientific discovery. Traditional approaches, primarily based on genetic algorithms and related evolutionary methods, have proven useful but suffer from scalability and expressivity limitations. Recently, large language model (LLM)-based evolutionary search methods have been introduced into SR and show promise. However, existing LLM-based approaches typically rely on scalar evaluation metrics, such as mean squared error, as the sole source of feedback during the search process, thereby overlooking the rich information embedded in the dataset. To address this limitation, we propose a novel LLM-based evolutionary search framework that incorporates programmatic context augmentation. By enabling code-based interactions with the dataset, our method can actively perform data analysis and extract informative signals, beyond aggregated evaluation scores. We evaluate our framework on advanced benchmarks, such as LLM-SRBench, and demonstrate superior efficiency and accuracy compared to strong baselines.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an LLM-based evolutionary search framework for symbolic regression that augments the search process with programmatic context, enabling code-based data analysis beyond simple scalar metrics, achieving better efficiency and accuracy on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based SR approaches rely solely on scalar evaluation metrics (e.g., MSE) as feedback, overlooking rich information embedded in the dataset.
AI.method: A novel LLM-based evolutionary search framework that incorporates programmatic context augmentation, enabling code-based interactions with the dataset to actively perform data analysis and extract informative signals beyond aggregated evaluation scores.
AI.result: Superior efficiency and accuracy compared to strong baselines on advanced benchmarks such as LLM-SRBench.
AI.conclusion: The proposed LLM-based evolutionary search framework with programmatic context augmentation demonstrates superior efficiency and accuracy compared to strong baselines on advanced benchmarks like LLM-SRBench.

title: Enabling Indoor Multi-Person Tracking With 6G mmWave ISAC Systems
comment: submitted to ieee for possible publication
summary: Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) has emerged as a key technology for 6G wireless networks. In this paper, wireless sensing for the indoor multi-person tracking is explored with 6G mmWave ISAC systems. To limit the sensing overhead, a sparse deployment of sensing reference signals (RS) is applied in the orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing (OFDM) frame, where the channel state information (CSI) at the sensing RS is extracted for the multi-person tracking. To enable a robust tracking of multiple persons in a complex indoor environment, three key mechanisms are proposed: 1) a modified moving target indicator (MTI) scheme is proposed to remove the static environmental clutter under a sparse RS time spacing; 2) an effective target identification mechanism is developed to exclude false target points; 3) the Kalman filter with a penalty association algorithm is designed to associate the detected points with the right tracks, especially handling the crossover case of two tracks. With the above mechanisms, multiple persons can be effectively tracked with an extremely low sensing overhead. An mmWave bistatic ISAC prototype system at 26 GHz with 500 MHz bandwidth has been developed to validate our design, where the overhead of the sensing RS is less than 0.005\%. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed design achieves a median position error of 12 cm for multi-person tracking with path-crossing in the indoor environment with a single receiver.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a sparse-sensing, mmWave ISAC-based indoor multi-person tracking system using OFDM, with a 26 GHz prototype achieving 12 cm median error at under 0.005% sensing overhead.
AI.motivation: To enable efficient indoor multi-person tracking with 6G ISAC systems while minimizing sensing overhead (reference signal usage), addressing challenges of static clutter, false detections, and track crossover in complex indoor environments.
AI.method: Three mechanisms: (1) modified moving target indicator (MTI) for static clutter removal under sparse RS spacing; (2) target identification to exclude false points; (3) Kalman filter with penalty association for track association and crossover handling.
AI.result: Median position error of 12 cm for multi-person tracking with path-crossing in indoor environments, using a 26 GHz bistatic ISAC prototype with 500 MHz bandwidth and sensing RS overhead less than 0.005%.
AI.conclusion: The proposed sparse-sensing approach enables robust multi-person tracking with extremely low overhead, validated experimentally with a real mmWave ISAC prototype.

title: Neural Control: Adjoint Learning Through Equilibrium Constraints
summary: Many physical AI tasks are governed by implicit equilibrium: an agent actuates a subset of degrees of freedom (boundary DoFs), while the remaining free DoFs settle by minimizing a total potential energy. Even seemingly basic tasks such as bending a deformable linear object (DLO) to a target shape can exhibit strongly nonlinear behavior due to multi-stability: the same boundary conditions may yield multiple equilibrium shapes depending on the actuation trajectory. However, learning and control in such systems is brittle because the actuation-to-configuration map is defined only implicitly, and naive backpropagation through iterative equilibrium solvers is memory- and compute-intensive. We propose Neural Control, a boundary-control framework that computes trajectory-dependent, memory-efficient proxy gradients by differentiating equilibrium conditions via an adjoint formulation, avoiding unrolling of solver iterations. To improve robustness over long horizons, we integrate these sensitivities into a receding-horizon MPC scheme that repeatedly re-anchors optimization to realized equilibria and mitigates basin-switching in multi-stable regimes. We evaluate Neural Control in simulation and on physical robots manipulating DLOs, and show improved performance over gradient-free baselines such as SPSA and CEM.

AI.tldr: Neural Control is a boundary-control framework for implicit equilibrium systems (e.g., deformable objects) that uses adjoint-based proxy gradients and receding-horizon MPC to efficiently handle multi-stability, outperforming gradient-free baselines in DLO manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: Physical AI tasks with implicit equilibria (e.g., bending deformable linear objects) exhibit multi-stability and nonlinear behavior, making learning/control brittle due to memory/compute costs of naive backpropagation through iterative equilibrium solvers.
AI.method: Proposes adjoint-based proxy gradients (differentiating equilibrium conditions without unrolling solver iterations) integrated into a receding-horizon MPC scheme that re-anchors optimization to realized equilibria to handle multi-stable basin-switching.
AI.result: Demonstrated improved performance over gradient-free baselines (SPSA, CEM) in both simulation and physical robot DLO manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: Adjoint-based proxy gradients with receding-horizon MPC provide memory-efficient and robust control for implicit equilibrium systems with multi-stability.

title: NucEval: A Robust Evaluation Framework for Nuclear Instance Segmentation
summary: In computational pathology, nuclear instance segmentation is a fundamental task with many downstream clinical applications. With the advent of deep learning, many approaches, including convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and vision transformers (ViTs), have been proposed for this task, along with both machine learning-based and non-machine learning-based pre- and post-processing techniques to further boost performance. However, one fundamental aspect that has received less attention is the evaluation pipeline. In this study, we identify four key issues associated with nuclear instance segmentation evaluation and propose corresponding solutions. Our proposed modifications, namely handling vague regions, score normalization, overlapping instances, and border uncertainty, are integrated into a unified framework called NucEval, which enables robust evaluation of nuclear instance segmentation. We evaluate this pipeline using the NuInsSeg dataset, which provides unique characteristics that make it particularly suitable for this study, as well as two additional external datasets, with three CNN- and ViT-based nuclear instance segmentation models, to demonstrate the impact of these modifications on instance segmentation metrics. The code, along with complete guidelines and illustrative examples, is publicly available at: https://github.com/masih4/nuc_eval.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces NucEval, a robust evaluation framework for nuclear instance segmentation that addresses four key issues: vague regions, score normalization, overlapping instances, and border uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation pipelines for nuclear instance segmentation have received less attention despite many advances in models and preprocessing techniques. The authors identify four key issues in existing evaluation approaches that can lead to unreliable or inconsistent performance assessment.
AI.method: The authors propose four modifications: (1) handling vague regions (ambiguous boundaries), (2) score normalization for fair comparison, (3) dealing with overlapping instances, and (4) addressing border uncertainty. These are integrated into a unified framework called NucEval.
AI.result: The pipeline is evaluated on the NuInsSeg dataset (which has unique suitable characteristics) and two external datasets, using three CNN- and ViT-based nuclear instance segmentation models, demonstrating significant impact on instance segmentation metrics.
AI.conclusion: NucEval enables more robust and reliable evaluation of nuclear instance segmentation, with code and guidelines publicly available.

title: Pareto-type finite-block optimality for source codes: a constrained Markov example
summary: We study a Pareto-type notion of finite-block optimality for injective source codes, where two codes are compared through the full sequence of expected block lengths. As a concrete and fully analyzable test case, we revisit the four-symbol constrained Markov source introduced by Dalai and Leonardi in their "meaningful example'' on constrained-source decodability. For each admissible nonempty string $u=x_1^m \in \mathscr{A} \subset \mathscr{X}^+$, let $$ K(u):=-\log_2 \mathbb{P}(X_1^m=u) $$ denote its information cost. We construct a canonical injective binary mapping $C:\mathscr{A} \to \{0,1\}^+$ by ordering admissible strings by increasing $K(u)$, then by length and lexicographic order, and assigning binary strings in shortlex order. For the length-$n$ block $X_1^n$ we prove $$ \mathbb{E}[|C(X_1)|]=\tfrac32, \qquad \mathbb{E}[|C(X_1^n)|]<\tfrac32\,n\quad (n\ge 2). $$ Moreover, for every fixed $$ 0<c<\frac{\sqrt2}{18\sqrtπ} $$ we have $$ \mathbb{E}[|C(X_1^n)|]\le \tfrac32\,n-\frac{c}{\sqrt n} $$ for all sufficiently large $n$. Thus, for this source, the reversible Dalai-Leonardi code is not Pareto-optimal with respect to finite-block average length. The proof is based on an exact enumeration of admissible strings by information cost and on a shortlex gap identity implying that each cost class splits evenly between lengths $K(u)-1$ and $K(u)$. The example is simple, but it already exhibits the kind of finite-block Pareto comparison that seems natural for injective source coding under source constraints.

AI.tldr: This paper studies Pareto-optimality for injective source codes using a four-symbol constrained Markov source. The authors construct a canonical binary code and prove it is not Pareto-optimal for finite-block average length, showing that the reversible Dalai-Leonardi code saves less than 1.5n bits on average, with a negative correction of order 1/√n.
AI.motivation: To understand finite-block optimality for injective source codes under source constraints, using Pareto comparisons based on expected block lengths, beyond asymptotic or single-block criteria.
AI.method: Construct a canonical injective binary mapping by ordering admissible strings by information cost K(u), then by length and lexicographic order, assigning binary strings in shortlex order. Use exact enumeration of admissible strings by information cost and a shortlex gap identity showing cost classes split evenly between lengths K(u)-1 and K(u).
AI.result: For the four-symbol constrained Markov source: E[|C(X_1)|] = 3/2, E[|C(X_1^n)|] < 3/2 n for n≥2, and for large n, E[|C(X_1^n)|] ≤ 3/2 n - c/√n for any c < √2/(18√π).
AI.conclusion: The reversible Dalai-Leonardi code is not Pareto-optimal with respect to finite-block average length for this source. The example demonstrates that finite-block Pareto comparisons are natural and meaningful for injective source coding under constraints.

title: Agentic AI-Based Joint Computing and Networking via Mixture of Experts and Large Language Models
comment: 16 pages, 16 figures, 9 tables, a version of this work is due to be submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Future sixth-generation (6G) mobile networks are envisioned to be equipped with a diverse set of powerful, yet highly specialized, optimization experts. Such a promising vision is concurrently expected to give rise to the need for scalable mechanisms that can select, combine, and orchestrate such experts based on high-level intent and uncertainty descriptions. In this paper, we propose an agentic artificial intelligence (AI)-based network optimization framework that integrates mixture of experts (MoE) architectures with large language models (LLMs). Under the proposed framework, the employed LLM acts as a semantic gate to reason over operator objectives and dynamically compose suitable optimization agents. The proposed framework is formulated in a model-agnostic manner and bridges human-readable network intents with low-level resource allocation decisions, enabling flexible optimization across heterogeneous objectives and operating conditions. As a representative instantiation, we apply the framework to a joint communication and computing network and design a library of specialized optimization experts covering throughput, fairness, and delay-driven objectives under both regular and robust conditions. Numerical simulations demonstrate that the proposed agentic MoE framework consistently achieves near-optimal performance compared to exhaustive expert combinations while outperforming individual experts across diverse objectives, including delay minimization and throughput maximization.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an agentic AI-based network optimization framework that combines mixture of experts (MoE) with large language models (LLMs) for 6G networks. The LLM acts as a semantic gate to reason over operator objectives and dynamically compose optimization agents. Applied to joint communication and computing networks, it achieves near-optimal performance across various objectives like delay and throughput.
AI.motivation: 6G mobile networks will have many specialized optimization experts but lack scalable mechanisms to select, combine, and orchestrate them based on high-level intent and uncertainty descriptions.
AI.method: Proposes an agentic AI framework integrating MoE architectures with LLMs, where the LLM acts as a semantic gate to reason over operator objectives and dynamically compose suitable optimization agents in a model-agnostic manner.
AI.result: Numerical simulations show the proposed agentic MoE framework consistently achieves near-optimal performance compared to exhaustive expert combinations and outperforms individual experts across diverse objectives including delay minimization and throughput maximization.
AI.conclusion: The framework bridges human-readable network intents with low-level resource allocation decisions, enabling flexible optimization across heterogeneous objectives and operating conditions for 6G networks.

title: Are you with me? A Framework for Detecting Mental Model Discrepancies in Task-Based Team Dialogues
comment: Accepted to Proceedings of the Annual Meeting of the Cognitive Science Society 2026
summary: Humans typically use natural language to update teammates on task states. Since not all updates are communicated, discrepancies arise between the team members' mental models that negatively affect overall team performance. How can we categorize such discrepancies? Do misalignments detected in team dialogue predict future mental model misalignments? Traditional shared mental model (SMM) assessment methods rely on retrospective expert coding that cannot capture real-time coordination dynamics. We propose a framework to identify and categorize four types of mental model discrepancies: unsupported beliefs, false beliefs, belief contradictions, and omissions, all of which can naturally emerge in team dialogues. Using dialogues from twenty dyad teams performing collaborative object identification tasks across four sequential levels, we demonstrate that these discrepancy patterns contain predictive signals. Averaging historical discrepancy counts achieves meaningful prediction accuracy using uniform weighting as an exploratory baseline, with differential predictability across discrepancy types.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a framework to identify four types of mental model discrepancies (unsupported beliefs, false beliefs, belief contradictions, omissions) in team dialogues and shows their predictive power for future misalignments using data from dyad teams.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of real-time assessment methods for shared mental model discrepancies that negatively impact team performance, moving beyond retrospective expert coding.
AI.method: A framework categorizing four types of discrepancies from team dialogues, tested on twenty dyad teams performing collaborative identification tasks across four levels, using historical discrepancy counts with uniform weighting as a baseline predictor.
AI.result: Discrepancy patterns contain predictive signals for future misalignments, with differential predictability across the four discrepancy types. Uniform weighting achieves meaningful prediction accuracy as an exploratory baseline.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enables real-time categorization of mental model discrepancies from natural dialogue, offering a predictive approach to anticipate future misalignments in team coordination.

title: Near-field Wideband Multi-User Localization using NFMR-Net
summary: This paper proposes a deep learning based method for wideband near-field multi-user localization. In particular, the proposed approach utilizes the Zadoff-Chu (ZC) sequence based pilots to mitigate the inter-user interference, which in turn aids the estimation of the multi-tap channel matrix. From this channel matrix, we extract the line-of-sight (LoS) array response based on the delay-tap energy profile. The LoS delay-tap is further refined using parabolic interpolation to obtain the coarse estimate of range parameter. Next, the extracted LoS array response is used to obtain the coarse angle estimate using 2D MUSIC algorithm. These coarse estimates are further refined using the near-field music refinement network (NFMR-Net), which involves separate sub-networks for range and angle estimations. Through numerical analysis, the proposed NFMR-Net is demonstrated to outperform conventional 2D MUSIC algorithm.

AI.tldr: Proposes NFMR-Net, a deep learning method using ZC sequence pilots and near-field MUSIC refinement for wideband multi-user localization, outperforming conventional 2D MUSIC.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of wideband near-field multi-user localization, where inter-user interference and multi-path effects degrade conventional methods like 2D MUSIC.
AI.method: Uses ZC sequence-based pilots to mitigate inter-user interference; extracts LoS array response via delay-tap energy profile; refines coarse range via parabolic interpolation and coarse angle via 2D MUSIC; further refines both using NFMR-Net with separate sub-networks for range and angle.
AI.result: NFMR-Net outperforms conventional 2D MUSIC algorithm in numerical analysis.
AI.conclusion: Deep learning refinement (NFMR-Net) improves near-field localization accuracy over traditional MUSIC-based methods.

title: On Surprising Effects of Risk-Aware Domain Randomization for Contact-Rich Sampling-based Predictive Control
summary: Domain randomization (DR) is widely used in policy learning to improve robustness to modeling error, but remains underexplored in contact-rich sampling-based predictive control (SPC), where rollout quality is highly sensitive to uncertainty. In this work, we take the first step by studying risk-aware DR in predictive sampling on a simple yet representative Push-T task, comparing average, optimistic, and pessimistic rollout aggregations under randomized model instances. Our initial results suggest that DR affects not only robustness to model error, but also the effective cost landscape seen by the sampling-based optimizer, by reshaping the basin of attraction around contact-producing actions. This opens up potential for exploring better grounded risk-aware contact-rich SPC under model uncertainty. Video: https://youtu.be/f1F0ALXxhSM

AI.tldr: This paper explores risk-aware domain randomization in sampling-based predictive control for contact-rich tasks, finding that randomization reshapes the cost landscape and affects robustness as well as optimizer behavior.
AI.motivation: Domain randomization is widely used in policy learning but remains underexplored in contact-rich sampling-based predictive control (SPC), where rollout quality is highly sensitive to model uncertainty.
AI.method: The authors study risk-aware DR in predictive sampling on the Push-T task, comparing average, optimistic, and pessimistic rollout aggregations under randomized model instances.
AI.result: DR not only improves robustness to model error but also reshapes the effective cost landscape by altering the basin of attraction around contact-producing actions.
AI.conclusion: This opens up potential for better grounded risk-aware contact-rich SPC under model uncertainty.

title: Boundary-Aware Uncertainty Quantification for Wildfire Spread Prediction
summary: Reliable wildfire spread prediction is vital for risk-aware emergency planning, yet most deep learning models lack principled uncertainty quantification (UQ). Further, for boundary-sensitive cases like wildfire spread, evaluating models with global metrics alone is often insufficient. To shift the focus of UQ evaluation toward a more operationally relevant approach, the Fire-Centered Evaluation Region (FCER) framework is introduced as a spatially conditioned protocol to characterize UQ within critical fire zones. Using FCER, an Ensemble is compared against an distilled single-pass student model on the WildfireSpreadTS dataset. The student model demonstrates comparable calibration and complementary uncertainty ranking in boundary-relevant regimes. Code is available at https://github. com/jonasvilhofunk/WildfireUQ-FCER

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Fire-Centered Evaluation Region (FCER) framework for spatially conditioned uncertainty quantification in wildfire spread prediction, and shows that a distilled single-pass student model achieves comparable calibration and complementary uncertainty ranking to an Ensemble model on the WildfireSpreadTS dataset.
AI.motivation: Most deep learning models for wildfire spread prediction lack principled uncertainty quantification, and global metrics alone are insufficient for evaluating boundary-sensitive cases like wildfire spread. The authors aim to shift UQ evaluation toward a more operationally relevant approach.
AI.method: The FCER (Fire-Centered Evaluation Region) framework is introduced as a spatially conditioned protocol to characterize UQ within critical fire zones. An Ensemble model is compared against a distilled single-pass student model on the WildfireSpreadTS dataset.
AI.result: The student model demonstrates comparable calibration and complementary uncertainty ranking in boundary-relevant regimes compared to the Ensemble model.
AI.conclusion: FCER provides a more operationally relevant evaluation approach for UQ in wildfire spread prediction, and distilled single-pass models can be viable alternatives to ensembles in this domain.

title: New bounds on the covering radius of orthogonal arrays of even strength
summary: We obtain new linear programming (LP) and constructive bounds for the covering radius of binary orthogonal arrays of strength $2k$. Our LP bounds develop in two alternative scenarios. First, if a point $y \in F_2^n$, where the covering radius of some orthogonal array $C \subset F_2^n$ of strength $2k$ is realized, is such that the farthest point of $C$ to $y$ is not antipodal to $y$ we obtain a bound which is better than the Tiet{ä}v{ä}inen (or Fazekas-Levenshtein) bound for non-tight arrays (i.e., the cardinality strictly exceeds the Rao lower bound). Second, if all points where the covering radius is realized are such that their antipodes are in $C$, we obtain a bound which depends on the cardinality of $C$ and is again better whenever the orthogonal array is not tight. We further describe three infinite families of binary orthogonal arrays related to the duals of BCH, Melas, and Zetterberg codes. For these families, we derive lower bounds on the covering radius by applying techniques from algebraic curves over finite fields, while the improved linear programming methods developed in this paper provide upper bounds, leading in some cases to fairly close estimates.

AI.tldr: This paper develops improved linear programming bounds for the covering radius of binary orthogonal arrays of strength 2k, with two scenarios depending on antipodal properties, and studies three infinite families (dual BCH, Melas, Zetterberg codes) using algebraic curves for lower bounds and LP for upper bounds, yielding close estimates.
AI.motivation: To obtain better bounds on the covering radius of binary orthogonal arrays, improving upon existing bounds (e.g., Tietäväinen or Fazekas-Levenshtein) for non-tight arrays, and to provide concrete estimates for specific infinite families of orthogonal arrays.
AI.method: Two linear programming bound scenarios: (1) when the farthest point is not antipodal to y; (2) when antipodes of all covering-radius-realizing points lie in C. Also uses algebraic curves over finite fields to derive lower bounds for duals of BCH, Melas, and Zetterberg codes.
AI.result: New LP bounds that are better than existing bounds for non-tight arrays. Lower bounds from algebraic curves and upper bounds from LP give fairly close estimates for the three infinite families of orthogonal arrays.
AI.conclusion: The new LP methods improve known bounds for covering radii of binary orthogonal arrays, and combining them with algebraic curve techniques yields tight estimates for important code families.

title: Generate, Filter, Control, Replay: A Comprehensive Survey of Rollout Strategies for LLM Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a central post-training tool for improving the reasoning abilities of large language models (LLMs). In these systems, the rollout, the trajectory sampled from a prompt to termination, including intermediate reasoning steps and optional tool or environment interactions, determines the data the optimizer learns from, yet rollout design is often underreported. This survey provides an optimizer-agnostic view of rollout strategies for RL-based post-training of reasoning LLMs. We formalize rollout pipelines with unified notation and introduce Generate-Filter-Control-Replay (GFCR), a lifecycle taxonomy that decomposes rollout pipelines into four modular stages: Generate proposes candidate trajectories and topologies; Filter constructs intermediate signals via verifiers, judges, critics; Control allocates compute and makes continuation/branching/stopping decisions under budgets; and Replay retains and reuses artifacts across rollouts without weight updates, including self-evolving curricula that autonomously generate new training tasks. We complement GFCR with a criterion taxonomy of reliability, coverage, and cost sensitivity that characterizes rollout trade-offs. Using this framework, we synthesize methods spanning RL with verifiable rewards, process supervision, judge-based gating, guided and tree/segment rollouts, adaptive compute allocation, early-exit and partial rollouts, throughput optimization, and replay/recomposition for self-improvement. We ground the framework with case studies in math, code/SQL, multimodal reasoning, tool-using agents, and agentic skill benchmarks that evaluate skill induction, reuse, and cross-task transfer. Finally, we provide a diagnostic index that maps common rollout pathologies to GFCR modules and mitigation levers, alongside open challenges for building reproducible, compute-efficient, and trustworthy rollout pipelines.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive survey formalizing rollout design for RL-based post-training of reasoning LLMs, introducing the GFCR (Generate-Filter-Control-Replay) lifecycle taxonomy to decompose rollout pipelines into four modular stages, along with a criterion taxonomy of reliability, coverage, and cost sensitivity.
AI.motivation: Rollout design in RL-based LLM post-training is often underreported despite critically determining the data optimizers learn from. There is a need for a unified, optimizer-agnostic framework to systematically analyze and compare rollout strategies across diverse reasoning tasks.
AI.method: The paper proposes GFCR (Generate-Filter-Control-Replay), a four-stage lifecycle taxonomy for rollout pipelines, complemented by a criterion taxonomy (reliability, coverage, cost sensitivity). It surveys methods across RL with verifiable rewards, process supervision, judge-based gating, guided/segment rollouts, adaptive compute allocation, early-exit strategies, throughput optimization, and replay/recomposition for self-improvement.
AI.result: The framework synthesizes rollout strategies spanning math, code/SQL, multimodal reasoning, tool-using agents, and agentic skill benchmarks. It provides a diagnostic index mapping common rollout pathologies to GFCR modules and mitigation levers, alongside identified open challenges.
AI.conclusion: A modular, optimizer-agnostic view of rollout design enables systematic understanding and improvement of RL-based post-training for reasoning LLMs. The GFCR taxonomy helps diagnose pathologies and guide the development of reproducible, compute-efficient, and trustworthy rollout pipelines.

title: Learning Correct Behavior from Examples: Validating Sequential Execution in Autonomous Agents
summary: As autonomous agents become increasingly sophisticated, validating their sequential behavior presents a significant challenge. Traditional testing approaches require manual specification, exact sequence matching, or thousands of training examples. We present a novel algorithm that automatically learns correct behavior from just 2-10 passing execution traces and validates new executions against this learned model. Our approach combines dominator analysis from compiler theory with multimodal large language model-powered semantic understanding to identify essential states and handle non-deterministic behavior. The system constructs a generalized ground truth model using Prefix Tree Acceptors, merges traces through multi-tiered equivalence detection, and validates new executions via topological subsequence matching. In controlled experiments, our system achieved high accuracy in detecting product bugs and false successes using only 3 training traces. This approach provides explainable validation results with coverage metrics and works across diverse domains including UI testing, code generation, and robotic processes.

AI.tldr: A novel algorithm that learns correct sequential behavior from just 2-10 execution traces using dominator analysis and multimodal LLMs, achieving high accuracy in detecting bugs across UI testing, code generation, and robotics.
AI.motivation: Validating sequential behavior of autonomous agents is challenging because traditional testing requires manual specification, exact sequence matching, or thousands of training examples, which are costly and impractical.
AI.method: Combines dominator analysis from compiler theory with multimodal LLM-powered semantic understanding to identify essential states and handle non-determinism. Uses Prefix Tree Acceptors to construct generalized ground truth models, merges traces via multi-tiered equivalence detection, and validates executions through topological subsequence matching.
AI.result: Achieved high accuracy in detecting product bugs and false successes using only 3 training traces in controlled experiments.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides explainable validation with coverage metrics and generalizes across diverse domains including UI testing, code generation, and robotic process automation.

title: Joint Communication and Trajectory Design for Movable Antenna Systems
summary: Movable antennas (MAs) have attracted significant attention in wireless communications due to their ability to reconfigure channel conditions by flexibly adjusting the antenna positions within a confined region. However, MA movement generally incurs a non-negligible delay, which may significantly limit the data transmission time at optimized positions. To tackle this challenge, this paper investigates a new joint communication and trajectory optimization problem, where each MA transmits while moving along an optimized trajectory to prolong the effective data transmission time. Focusing on a single-MA system, our goal is to maximize the average data rate by optimizing the MA's positions over time, subject to its maximum velocity constraints. However, this continuous-time antenna position optimization problem is highly non-convex and challenging to solve. To tackle this challenge, we first consider a special case with two channel paths and derive the optimal MA trajectory in closed form. For other general cases, we ingeniously reformulate the average rate maximization problem into a fixed-hop shortest path problem in graph theory by sampling the antenna movement region into a multitude of discrete points, and solve it optimally. Simulation results demonstrate that our proposed algorithm can significantly improve the data rate compared to other baseline schemes.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes optimizing the trajectory of a movable antenna (MA) that transmits data while moving, rather than stopping at fixed positions, to maximize the average data rate under velocity constraints. The problem is reformulated as a shortest path graph problem and solved optimally.
AI.motivation: MA movement incurs non-negligible delay, limiting data transmission time at optimized positions. This paper aims to extend effective transmission time by having the antenna transmit while moving along an optimized trajectory.
AI.method: Formulates a continuous-time position optimization problem to maximize average data rate under velocity constraints. Solves a special two-path case in closed form; for general cases, reformulates as a fixed-hop shortest path problem by sampling the region into discrete points.
AI.result: Simulation results show the proposed algorithm significantly improves data rate compared to baseline schemes.
AI.conclusion: Moving antennas along optimized trajectories during transmission can overcome the delay penalty of repositioning, improving effective data rates.

title: Height Control and Optimal Torque Planning for Jumping With Wheeled-Bipedal Robots
comment: 6 pages, 16 figures. Accepted for publication at ICARM 2021
summary: This paper mainly studies the accurate height jumping control of wheeled-bipedal robots based on torque planning and energy consumption optimization. Due to the characteristics of underactuated, nonlinear estimation, and instantaneous impact in the jumping process, accurate control of the wheeled-bipedal robot's jumping height is complicated. In reality, robots often jump at excessive height to ensure safety, causing additional motor loss, greater ground reaction force and more energy consumption. To solve this problem, a novel wheeled-bipedal jumping dynamical model(W-JBD) is proposed to achieve accurate height control. It performs well but not suitable for the real robot because the torque has a striking step. Therefore, the Bayesian optimization for torque planning method(BOTP) is proposed, which can obtain the optimal torque planning without accurate dynamic model and within few iterations. BOTP method can reduce 82.3% height error, 26.9% energy cost with continuous torque curve. This result is validated in the Webots simulation platform. Based on the torque curve obtained in the W-JBD model to narrow the searching space, BOTP can quickly converge (40 times on average). Cooperating W-JBD model and BOTP method, it is possible to achieve the height control of real robots with reasonable times of experiments.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a W-JBD dynamical model and a BOTP torque planning method for accurate height jumping control of wheeled-bipedal robots, achieving 82.3% height error reduction and 26.9% energy cost saving with continuous torque curves.
AI.motivation: Robots often jump at excessive heights to ensure safety, causing additional motor loss, greater ground reaction force, and more energy consumption due to the underactuated, nonlinear, and instantaneous impact characteristics of jumping.
AI.method: Proposes a Wheeled-Bipedal Jumping Dynamical model (W-JBD) for accurate height control, then introduces Bayesian Optimization for Torque Planning (BOTP) to obtain optimal continuous torque curves without requiring an accurate dynamic model, using W-JBD results to narrow the search space for fast convergence.
AI.result: BOTP reduces height error by 82.3% and energy cost by 26.9% with continuous torque curves, converging in about 40 iterations on average, validated in Webots simulation.
AI.conclusion: By combining the W-JBD model and BOTP method, accurate height control of real wheeled-bipedal robots becomes feasible with a reasonable number of experiments.

title: DINO Soars: DINOv3 for Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation of Remote Sensing Imagery
summary: The remote sensing (RS) domain suffers from a lack of densely labeled datasets, which are costly to obtain. Thus, models that can segment RS imagery well without supervised fine-tuning are valuable, but existing solutions fall behind supervised methods. Recently, DINOv3 surpassed SOTA RS foundation models on the GEO-bench segmentation benchmark without pre-training on RS data. Additionally, DINO.txt has enabled open vocabulary semantic segmentation (OVSS) with the DINOv3 backbone. We leverage these developments to form an OVSS model for RS imagery, free of RS-domain fine-tuning. Our model, CAFe-DINO (Cost Aggregation + Feature Upsampling with DINO) exploits the strong OVSS performance of DINOv3 for RS imagery via cost aggregation and training-free upsampling of text-image similarity scores. The robust latent of the DINOv3 backbone eliminates the need for fine-tuning on RS imagery; we instead fine-tune our model on a RS-targeted subset of COCO-Stuff. CAFe-DINO achieves state-of-the-art performance on key RS segmentation datasets, outperforming OVSS methods fine-tuned on RS data. Our code and data are publicly available at https://github.com/rfaulk/DINO_Soars.

AI.tldr: CAFe-DINO is an open-vocabulary semantic segmentation model for remote sensing imagery that achieves SOTA performance without any RS-domain fine-tuning, using cost aggregation and training-free upsampling on DINOv3 backbone, fine-tuned only on a RS-targeted subset of COCO-Stuff.
AI.motivation: Remote sensing domain lacks densely labeled datasets which are costly to obtain. Existing segmentation models that work without supervised fine-tuning fall behind supervised methods, motivating the need for a model that can segment RS imagery well without RS-specific fine-tuning.
AI.method: CAFe-DINO leverages DINOv3 backbone (already strong on RS without RS pre-training) and DINO.txt for open vocabulary semantic segmentation. It uses cost aggregation and training-free upsampling of text-image similarity scores. The model is fine-tuned only on a RS-targeted subset of COCO-Stuff, avoiding any direct RS-domain fine-tuning.
AI.result: CAFe-DINO achieves state-of-the-art performance on key RS segmentation datasets, outperforming OVSS methods that were fine-tuned on RS data.
AI.conclusion: The DINOv3 backbone's robust latent representations eliminate the need for fine-tuning on RS imagery, enabling effective open vocabulary semantic segmentation for remote sensing without costly labeled datasets.

title: Leveraging Code Automorphisms for Improved Syndrome-Based Neural Decoding
comment: 6 pages, 7 figures, submitted to IEEE for possible publication. Code to reproduce all results is available at: https://github.com/lebidan/sbnd
summary: Syndrome-based neural decoding (SBND) has emerged as a promising deep learning approach for soft-decision decoding of high-rate, short-length codes. However, this approach still has substantial room for improvement. In this paper, we show how to leverage code automorphisms to enhance the ability of existing SBND models to learn and generalize through data augmentation during training and inference. As a result, for the short high-rate codes considered, we obtain models that closely approach MLD performance using small datasets and proper training. Our findings also suggest that many prior results for SBND models in the literature underestimate their true correction capability due to undertraining. Code to reproduce all results is available at: https://github.com/lebidan/sbnd.

AI.tldr: This paper improves syndrome-based neural decoding by leveraging code automorphisms for data augmentation, achieving near-MLD performance with small datasets and proper training, while revealing prior results were undertrained.
AI.motivation: Existing SBND models have substantial room for improvement in soft-decision decoding of high-rate, short-length codes, and prior results may underestimate true correction capability.
AI.method: The authors leverage code automorphisms to perform data augmentation during both training and inference phases of SBND models.
AI.result: Models closely approach maximum likelihood decoding (MLD) performance using small datasets and proper training; prior results in the literature underestimate true correction capability due to undertraining.
AI.conclusion: Code automorphism-based data augmentation significantly enhances SBND model learning and generalization, revealing that earlier benchmarks were undertrained.

title: When Safety Geometry Collapses: Fine-Tuning Vulnerabilities in Agentic Guard Models
comment: This paper got accepted for AAAI 2026 Summer Symposium Series
summary: A guard model fine-tuned on entirely benign data can lose all safety alignment -- not through adversarial manipulation, but through standard domain specialization. We demonstrate this failure across three purpose-built safety classifiers -- LlamaGuard, WildGuard, and Granite Guardian -- deployed as protection layers in agentic AI pipelines, and show that it originates in the destruction of latent safety geometry: the structured harmful -- benign representational boundary that guides classification. We extract per-layer safety subspaces via SVD on class-conditional activation differences and track how this boundary evolves under benign fine-tuning. Granite Guardian undergoes complete collapse --  refusal rate drops from 85\% to 0\%, CKA falls to zero, and 100\% of outputs become ambiguous -- a severity exceeding prior findings on general-purpose LLMs, explained by the specialization hypothesis: concentrated safety representations are efficient but catastrophically brittle. To mitigate this, we propose Fisher-Weighted Safety Subspace Regularization (FW-SSR), a training-time penalty combining (i) curvature-aware direction weights derived from diagonal Fisher information and (ii) an adaptive $λ_t$ that scales with task-safety gradient conflict. FW-SSR recovers 75\% refusal on Granite Guardian (CKA = 0.983) and reduces WildGuard's Attack Success Rate to 3.6\% -- below the unmodified baseline -- by actively sharpening the safety subspace rather than merely anchoring it. Across all three models, structural representational geometry (CKA, Fisher score) predicts safety behavior more reliably than absolute displacement metrics, establishing geometry-based monitoring as a necessary component of guard model evaluation in agentic deployments.

AI.tldr: Guard models fine-tuned on benign data can completely lose safety alignment due to destruction of latent safety geometry. FW-SSR regularization recovers safety by preserving this geometric structure.
AI.motivation: Safety guard models are deployed as protection layers in agentic AI pipelines, but their robustness under domain specialization (not adversarial attacks) is unexplored. The paper aims to investigate whether benign fine-tuning can silently destroy safety alignment.
AI.method: (1) Extract per-layer safety subspaces via SVD on class-conditional activation differences. (2) Track safety geometry evolution under benign fine-tuning using CKA and Fisher scores. (3) Propose FW-SSR: a training-time penalty combining diagonal Fisher information weights and adaptive λt based on task-safety gradient conflict.
AI.result: Granite Guardian collapses completely (85%→0% refusal, CKA→0). FW-SSR recovers 75% refusal (CKA=0.983) and reduces WildGuard's ASR to 3.6% (below unmodified baseline). Geometry metrics (CKA, Fisher score) predict safety better than displacement metrics.
AI.conclusion: Safety representations in guard models are efficient but catastrophically brittle under specialization. Geometry-based monitoring is necessary for guard model evaluation in agentic deployments. FW-SSR effectively preserves safety by sharpening rather than anchoring the safety subspace.

title: Terminus-4B: Can a Smaller Model Replace Frontier LLMs at Agentic Execution Tasks?
summary: Modern coding agents increasingly delegate specialized subtasks to subagents, which are smaller, focused agentic loops that handle narrow responsibilities like search, debugging or terminal execution. This architectural pattern keeps the main agent's context window clean by isolating verbose outputs (e.g. build logs, test results, etc.) within the subagent context. Typically when agents employ subagents for such tasks, they use frontier models as these subagents. In this paper, we investigate whether a finetuned small language model (SLM) can achieve comparable performance to frontier models in the task of agentic terminal execution. We present Terminus-4B, which is a post-trained Qwen3-4B model via Supervised Finetuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning (RL) using rubric-based LLM-as-judge reward, specifically for this task. In our extensive evaluation spanning various frontier models, training ablations and main agent configurations, we find that Terminus-4B is able to reduce the token usage of the main agent by up to ~30% compared to the No Subagent baseline with no impact to agent performance on benchmarks like SWE-Bench Pro and our internal SWE-Bench C# benchmark, which tends to be heavy in verbose execution tasks. Furthermore, Terminus-4B improves key metrics showing the main agent relying on the outputs of the subagent and doing fewer terminal execution tasks by itself. We see that our model not only closes the gap between the Vanilla Qwen model and frontier models like Claude Sonnet / Opus / GPT-5.3-Codex, but often even exceeds their performance.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Terminus-4B, a finetuned small language model (4B parameters) that matches or exceeds frontier models (like Claude, GPT-5.3-Codex) in subagent terminal execution tasks, reducing main agent token usage by ~30% without performance loss on SWE-Bench benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Modern coding agents use subagents for specialized tasks to keep the main agent's context window clean. Typically, frontier models are used as subagents which is expensive. The authors investigate whether a small finetuned model can achieve comparable performance, aiming to reduce cost and token consumption.
AI.method: The authors post-trained Qwen3-4B via Supervised Finetuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning (RL) with a rubric-based LLM-as-judge reward system, specifically optimized for agentic terminal execution tasks.
AI.result: Terminus-4B reduces main agent token usage by ~30% compared to No Subagent baseline with no impact on agent performance on SWE-Bench Pro and internal SWE-Bench C# benchmark. It improves key metrics showing the main agent relies more on subagent outputs and does fewer terminal execution tasks itself. The model closes and often exceeds the gap between Vanilla Qwen and frontier models.
AI.conclusion: A small finetuned SLM (4B parameters) can replace frontier models in subagent terminal execution tasks, achieving better or equal performance while significantly reducing token consumption and cost.

title: Identification of Clock Ensemble Noise Parameters Using Differential Measurement Analysis
comment: Accepted for presentation at the EFTF 2026 conference
summary: The paper addresses the critical problem of identifying unknown parameters of an atomic clock ensemble. The ensemble model is considered as a set of individual clock models, where each clock is described by a second-order linear stochastic state-space model. The paper presents identification procedure for model unknown parameters based solely on the availability of differential measurements - that is, the measured pairwise phase differences between a designated pivot clock and all other clocks within the ensemble. Specifically, each clock model is defined by the following set of unknown parameters: the variances characterizing the white frequency noise and random walk frequency noise, the drift, and the (co)variance of the measurement noise. Two distinct identification methods are designed to estimate the unknown clock model parameters. The accuracy of the identified sets of parameters are demonstrated on a simulation scenario/real data combining atomic H-maser (AHM) clocks.

AI.tldr: This paper presents methods to identify unknown parameters of atomic clock ensembles using only differential phase measurements between a pivot clock and others.
AI.motivation: The critical problem of identifying unknown parameters of an atomic clock ensemble from limited differential measurements.
AI.method: Two distinct identification methods designed to estimate unknown clock model parameters (white frequency noise, random walk frequency noise, drift, measurement noise) using only pairwise phase differences from a pivot clock.
AI.result: Accuracy demonstrated through simulations and real data combining atomic H-maser (AHM) clocks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed identification procedures effectively estimate atomic clock ensemble parameters from differential measurements.

title: Learning Reactive Dexterous Grasping via Hierarchical Task-Space RL Planning and Joint-Space QP Control
summary: In this work, we propose a hybrid hierarchical control framework for reactive dexterous grasping that explicitly decouples high-level spatial intent from low-level joint execution. We introduce a multi-agent reinforcement learning architecture, specialized into distinct arm and hand agents, that acts as a high-level planner by generating desired task-space velocity commands. These commands are then processed by a GPU-parallelized quadratic programming controller, which translates them into feasible joint velocities while strictly enforcing kinematic limits and collision avoidance. This structural isolation not only accelerates training convergence but also strictly enforces hardware safety. Furthermore, the architecture unlocks zero-shot steerability, allowing system operators to dynamically adjust safety margins and avoid dynamic obstacles without retraining the policy. We extensively validate the proposed framework through a rigorous simulation-to-reality pipeline. Real-world hardware experiments on a 7-DoF arm equipped with a 20-DoF anthropomorphic hand demonstrate highly robust zero-shot transferability for dexterous grasping to a diverse set of unseen objects, highlighting the system's ability to reactively recover from unexpected physical disturbances in unstructured environments.

AI.tldr: A hybrid hierarchical control framework combining multi-agent RL (high-level planner) with GPU-parallelized QP control (low-level executor) for reactive dexterous grasping, enabling zero-shot steerability and robust sim-to-real transfer on a 7-DoF arm + 20-DoF hand.
AI.motivation: To address the challenges of reactive dexterous grasping by decoupling high-level spatial planning from low-level joint control, improving training convergence, safety enforcement, and enabling dynamic adaptability without retraining.
AI.method: Multi-agent RL architecture (arm + hand agents) generates task-space velocity commands → GPU-parallelized quadratic programming (QP) controller translates to joint velocities with kinematic/safety constraints.
AI.result: Zero-shot sim-to-real transfer demonstrated on 7-DoF arm + 20-DoF hand, robust grasping of diverse unseen objects, reactive recovery from physical disturbances.
AI.conclusion: The hierarchical decoupling effectively improves training efficiency, safety, and zero-shot steerability for real-world dexterous grasping.

title: Sentinel2Cap: A Human-Annotated Benchmark Dataset for Multimodal Remote Sensing Image Captioning
summary: Image captioning has become an important task in computer vision, enabling models to generate natural language descriptions of visual content. While several datasets exist for natural images and high-resolution optical remote sensing imagery, the availability of captioning datasets for multimodal satellite data remains limited, particularly for SAR imagery and medium-resolution sensors. We introduce Sentinel2Cap, a human-annotated multimodal captioning dataset containing Sentinel-1 SAR and Sentinel-2 multi-spectral image patches at 10 m and 20 m spatial resolution with diverse land cover compositions. Captions are created manually and carefully validated to ensure both semantic accuracy and linguistic quality. To evaluate Sentinel2Cap, we perform a zero-shot captioning using the Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct model across three image modalities: RGB, multi-spectral, and SAR pseudo-RGB representations. Results show that RGB images achieve the highest captioning performance, while SAR images remain more challenging for vision-language models. Providing modality-specific contextual prompts consistently improves performance across all metrics. These findings highlight both the challenges of multimodal remote sensing image captioning and the potential value of human-annotated datasets for advancing research in cross-modal scene understanding. All the material is publicly avaiable.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Sentinel2Cap, a new human-annotated multimodal captioning dataset for SAR (Sentinel-1) and multi-spectral (Sentinel-2) satellite imagery at 10m/20m resolution. Zero-shot evaluation using Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct shows RGB outperforms SAR, while modality-specific prompts improve all modalities.
AI.motivation: Existing captioning datasets focus on natural images or high-resolution optical remote sensing imagery; there is a lack of human-annotated captioning datasets for multimodal satellite data, particularly SAR and medium-resolution sensors.
AI.method: The authors curated Sentinel2Cap with Sentinel-1 SAR and Sentinel-2 multi-spectral patches (10m/20m resolution), manually annotated and validated captions. They performed zero-shot captioning using Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct across RGB, multi-spectral, and SAR pseudo-RGB formats.
AI.result: RGB images achieve the best captioning performance; SAR images are the most challenging. Modality-specific prompts consistently improve metrics across all image types.
AI.conclusion: The dataset fills a gap in multimodal remote sensing captioning. Results highlight challenges in SAR understanding and the value of human-annotated data for cross-modal scene understanding.

title: Design and Analysis of Quantum Dual-Containing CSS LDPC Codes based on Quasi-Dyadic Matrices
summary: Building scalable quantum computers requires quantum error-correcting codes that enable reliable operations in the presence of noise. Motivated by such need, this paper introduces two constructions of high-rate, quantum dual-containing (DC) Calderbank-Shor-Steane (CSS) low-density parity-check (LDPC) codes based on quasi-dyadic matrices. Their DC structure enables the transversal implementation of the Hadamard gate, and, jointly with the sparsity of their parity-check matrices enable low-complexity decoding via a standard binary belief-propagation algorithm. We provide several theoretical results concerning the cycle properties of these CSS codes. We also investigate their automorphism groups as well as their minimum distance. Furthermore, through numerical simulations, we show that the quantum CSS LDPC codes obtained through these constructions achieve better finite-length error rate performance than existing DC codes across different block lengths and code rates.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces two new constructions of high-rate quantum CSS LDPC codes based on quasi-dyadic matrices that enable transversal Hadamard gates, low-complexity decoding, and achieve better error-rate performance than existing dual-containing codes.
AI.motivation: Building scalable quantum computers requires quantum error-correcting codes that enable reliable operations in the presence of noise.
AI.method: Two constructions of high-rate, quantum dual-containing (DC) Calderbank-Shor-Steane (CSS) low-density parity-check (LDPC) codes based on quasi-dyadic matrices.
AI.result: The quantum CSS LDPC codes achieve better finite-length error rate performance than existing DC codes across different block lengths and code rates.
AI.conclusion: The proposed codes enable transversal Hadamard gate implementation and low-complexity decoding, making them promising for scalable quantum computing.

title: From Synthesis to Clinical Assistance: A Strategy-Aware Agent Framework for Autism Intervention based on Real Clinical Dataset
summary: The development of AI-assisted Early Intensive Behavioral Intervention (EIBI) for Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) is severely constrained by data scarcity. Furthermore, while Applied Behavior Analysis (ABA) serves as the gold standard for clinical intervention, general-purpose Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle to strictly adhere to its standardized procedures, often resulting in interactions that are linguistically fluent but strategically inconsistent. To address these challenges, we introduce \textsc{ASDAgent}, a strategy-aware framework designed to unify high-fidelity intervention dialogue synthesis and clinical decision support. \textsc{ASDAgent} incorporates two specialized components to solve distinct problems: (i) a \textsc{DoctorAgent} equipped with an Observe-Think-Act-Correct (O-T-A-C) reasoning loop, which resolves the issue of strategy collapse in LLMs by making ABA execution explicit and controllable; and (ii) a \textsc{ChildAgent} that utilizes probabilistic behavior modeling to mitigate data homogeneity, simulating diverse and non-deterministic ASD response patterns. Experiments demonstrate that dialogues generated by \textsc{ASDAgent} closely mirror the strategy distribution of human therapists (KL divergence: 0.083). In real autism intervention, \textsc{ASDAgent} achieves nearly 80\% strategic consistency with human experts. Moreover, we show that synthetic data produced by \textsc{ASDAgent} effectively distills professional clinical knowledge into small language models (SLMs), significantly enhancing their therapeutic capabilities.

AI.tldr: ASDAgent is a strategy-aware framework that uses a DoctorAgent with O-T-A-C reasoning and a ChildAgent with probabilistic behavior modeling to generate high-fidelity ABA-compliant intervention dialogues for ASD therapy, achieving near-human strategic consistency and enabling knowledge distillation into small language models.
AI.motivation: Data scarcity severely constrains AI-assisted EIBI development for ASD, and general-purpose LLMs fail to adhere to standardized ABA procedures, producing linguistically fluent but strategically inconsistent interactions.
AI.method: ASDAgent incorporates two specialized components: (i) DoctorAgent with an Observe-Think-Act-Correct (O-T-A-C) reasoning loop to enforce explicit and controllable ABA execution, and (ii) ChildAgent using probabilistic behavior modeling to simulate diverse, non-deterministic ASD response patterns, mitigating data homogeneity.
AI.result: Generated dialogues closely match human therapist strategy distributions (KL divergence: 0.083), achieve nearly 80% strategic consistency with human experts in real intervention, and synthetic data effectively distills clinical knowledge into small language models (SLMs), enhancing their therapeutic capabilities.
