It uses hierarchical group-based preference optimization for data-efficient offline alignment, fine-tunes two experts on opposing preferences, and constructs a continuous policy spectrum at inference via linear weight interpolation, justified by linear mode connectivity theory.
AI.result: SAGE generates scenarios with a superior balance of adversariality and realism and enables more effective closed-loop training of driving policies compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: Steerable adversarial scenario generation via multi-objective preference alignment and weight interpolation is a viable and flexible approach for safety assessment and policy training in autonomous driving.

title: Mix3R: Mixing Feed-forward Reconstruction and Generative 3D Priors for Joint Multi-view Aligned 3D Reconstruction and Pose Estimation
summary: Recent trends in sparse-view 3D reconstruction have taken two different paths: feed-forward reconstruction that predicts pixel-aligned point maps without a complete geometry, and generative 3D reconstruction that generates complete geometry but often with poor input-alignment. We present Mix3R, a novel generative 3D reconstruction method which mixes feed-forward reconstruction and 3D generation into a single framework in an aligned manner. Mix3R generates a 3D shape in two stages: a sparse voxel generation stage and a textured geometry generation stage. Unlike pure generative methods, our first-stage generation jointly produces a coarse 3D structure (sparse voxels), per-view point maps and camera parameters aligned to that 3D structure. This is made possible by introducing a Mixture-of-Transformers architecture that inserts global self-attentions to a feed-forward reconstruction model and a 3D generative model, both pretrained on large-scale data. This design effectively retains the pretrained priors but enables better 2D-3D alignment. Based on the initial aligned generations of sparse 3D voxels and point maps, we compute an overlap-based attention bias that is directly added to another pretrained textured geometry generation model, enabling it to correctly place input textures onto generated shapes in a training-free manner. Our design brings mutual benefits to both feed-forward reconstruction and 3D generation: The feed-forward branch learns to ground its predictions to a generative 3D prior, and conversely, the 3D generation branch is conditioned on geometrically informative features from the feed-forward branch. As a result, our method produces 3D shapes with better input alignment compared with pure 3D generative methods, together with camera pose estimations more accurate than previous feed-forward reconstruction methods. Our project page is at https://jsnln.github.io/mix3r/

AI.tldr: Mix3R combines feed-forward 3D reconstruction and generative 3D reconstruction into a unified framework using a Mixture-of-Transformers architecture, achieving both complete geometry and accurate input alignment in two stages: sparse voxel generation and textured geometry generation.
AI.motivation: Existing sparse-view 3D reconstruction methods have a trade-off: feed-forward methods produce pixel-aligned point maps but lack complete geometry, while generative methods produce complete geometry but often have poor input alignment. The authors aim to bridge this gap.
AI.method: Mix3R uses a Mixture-of-Transformers architecture with global self-attentions inserted into both a feed-forward reconstruction model and a 3D generative model, both pretrained on large-scale data. It operates in two stages: (1) sparse voxel generation jointly with per-view point maps and camera parameters, and (2) textured geometry generation using overlap-based attention bias from the first stage, enabling training-free texture placement.
AI.result: The method produces 3D shapes with better input alignment than pure generative methods, and camera pose estimations more accurate than previous feed-forward reconstruction methods.
AI.conclusion: The two branches mutually benefit each other: the feed-forward branch learns to ground predictions to a generative 3D prior, and the generative branch is conditioned on geometrically informative features from the feed-forward branch, leading to improved performance in both alignment and accuracy.

title: RouteHijack: Routing-Aware Attack on Mixture-of-Experts LLMs
summary:
Safety alignment is critical for the responsible deployment of large language models (LLMs). As Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures are increasingly adopted to scale model capacity, understanding their safety robustness becomes essential. Existing adversarial attacks, however, have notable limitations. Prompt-based jailbreaks rely on heuristic search and transfer poorly, model intervention methods require privileged access to internal representations, and optimization-based input attacks remain output-centric and are fundamentally limited to MoE models due to the non-differentiable routing mechanism.
  In this paper, we present RouteHijack, a routing-aware jailbreak for MoE LLMs. Our key insight is that safety behavior is concentrated in a small subset of experts, creating an opportunity to steer model behavior by influencing routing decisions through input optimization. Building on this observation, RouteHijack first performs response-driven expert localization to identify safety-critical and harmful experts by contrasting activations under safe refusals and harmful completions. It then constructs adversarial suffixes with a routing-aware objective that suppresses safety experts, promotes harmful experts, and prevents early-stage refusal during generation. At inference time, the optimized suffix is appended to a malicious prompt, requiring only input access. Across seven MoE LLMs, RouteHijack achieves a 69.3\% average attack success rate (ASR), outperforming prior optimization-based attack by $3.2\times$. RouteHijack also transfers zero-shot across five sibling MoE variants, raising average ASR from 27.7\% to 61.2\%, and further generalizes to three MoE-based VLMs, increasing average ASR from 2.47\% to 38.7\%. These findings expose a fundamental vulnerability in sparse expert architectures and highlight the need for defenses beyond output-level alignment.

AI.tldr: RouteHijack is a routing-aware jailbreak attack for Mixture-of-Experts LLMs that achieves 69.3% ASR by identifying and manipulating safety-critical experts through input optimization, significantly outperforming prior methods.
AI.motivation: Existing adversarial attacks on LLMs have limitations: prompt-based jailbreaks rely on heuristic search and transfer poorly, model intervention methods require privileged access, and optimization-based attacks struggle with MoE models due to non-differentiable routing mechanisms.
AI.method: RouteHijack first performs response-driven expert localization to identify safety-critical and harmful experts by contrasting activations under safe refusals vs. harmful completions. It then constructs adversarial suffixes with a routing-aware objective that suppresses safety experts, promotes harmful experts, and prevents early-stage refusal, requiring only input access at inference time.
AI.result: Achieves 69.3% average ASR across seven MoE LLMs (3.2x improvement over prior optimization-based attacks). Transfers zero-shot across five sibling MoE variants (ASR from 27.7% to 61.2%) and generalizes to three MoE-based VLMs (ASR from 2.47% to 38.7%).
AI.conclusion: The findings expose a fundamental vulnerability in sparse expert architectures and highlight the need for defenses beyond output-level alignment, suggesting that routing mechanisms themselves need protection.

title: Adaptive Dual-Path Framework for Covert Semantic Communication
comment: 16 oages, 13 figures, Accepted by IEEE Transactions on Communications
summary: This paper proposes a novel adaptive dual-path framework for covert semantic communication (SemCom), which integrates covert information transmission with task-oriented semantic coding. Unlike conventional covert communication methods that embed hidden messages through power-domain signal superposition, our framework embeds covert data within task-specific features via semantic-level intrinsic encoding. This new architecture introduces dual encoding paths with adaptive block selection: an Explicit path for public task execution and a Stego path that jointly encodes both public and covert information through contrastive representation alignment. A Gumbel-Softmax enabled adaptive path selection mechanism dynamically activates network blocks based on task require- ments. We formulate a multi-objective optimization framework that simultaneously ensures accurate semantic understanding and reliable covert transmission. We rigorously evaluate our framework's security against a powerful, independently trained attacker. Experimental results on the Cityscapes dataset demon- strate a state-of-the-art level of covertness: our method suppresses the attacker's detection accuracy to a near-random guessing level of 56.12%. This robust security is achieved while simultaneously maintaining superior performance on the primary semantic tasks compared to the baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an adaptive dual-path framework for covert semantic communication that embeds secret data into task-specific semantic features rather than traditional power-domain signal superposition. It uses dual encoding paths (Explicit and Stego) with Gumbel-Softmax based adaptive block selection, achieving near-random guessing detection rates (56.12%) while maintaining high primary task performance.
AI.motivation: To enable covert semantic communication that embeds hidden information within task-oriented semantic coding, overcoming limitations of conventional power-domain covert communication methods by leveraging semantic-level intrinsic encoding for better stealthiness.
AI.method: The framework consists of: (1) Dual encoding paths with adaptive block selection (Explicit path for public tasks, Stego path for joint public/covert encoding); (2) Contrastive representation alignment between the two paths; (3) Gumbel-Softmax enabled adaptive path selection mechanism for dynamic block activation; (4) Multi-objective optimization balancing semantic understanding and covert transmission.
AI.result: On the Cityscapes dataset, the method achieves state-of-the-art covertness by suppressing attacker detection accuracy to 56.12% (near random guessing), while maintaining better primary semantic task performance compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: The adaptive dual-path framework successfully integrates covert transmission with semantic communication, offering superior security against powerful attackers without sacrificing primary task performance.

title: OGPO: Sample Efficient Full-Finetuning of Generative Control Policies
summary: Generative control policies (GCPs), such as diffusion- and flow-based control policies, have emerged as effective parameterizations for robot learning. This work introduces Off-policy Generative Policy Optimization (OGPO), a sample-efficient algorithm for finetuning GCPs that maintains off-policy critic networks to maximize data reuse and propagate policy gradients through the full generative process of the policy via a modified PPO objective, using critics as the terminal reward. OGPO achieves state-of-the-art performance on manipulation tasks spanning multi-task settings, high-precision insertion, and dexterous control. To our knowledge, it is also the only method that can fine-tune poorly-initialized behavior cloning policies to near full task-success with no expert data in the online replay buffer, and does so with few task-specific hyperparameter tuning. Through extensive empirical investigations, we demonstrate the OGPO drastically outperforms methods alternatives on policy steering and learning residual corrections, and identify the key mechanisms behind its performance. We further introduce practical stabilizers, including success-buffer regularization, conservative advantages, $χ^2$ regularization, and Q-variance reduction, to mitigate critic over-exploitation across state- and pixel-based settings. Beyond proposing OGPO, we conduct a systematic empirical study of GCP finetuning, identifying the stabilizing mechanisms and failure modes that govern successful off-policy full-policy improvement.

AI.tldr: OGPO is a sample-efficient off-policy algorithm for fine-tuning generative control policies (diffusion/flow-based) in robotics, using critic networks and a modified PPO objective. It achieves SOTA on manipulation tasks, can recover poor BC policies without expert data, and introduces practical stabilizers to mitigate critic over-exploitation.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for fine-tuning generative control policies suffer from sample inefficiency, requiring large amounts of online interaction. The authors aim to develop an algorithm that maximizes data reuse and can effectively propagate gradients through the full generative process of the policy.
AI.method: OGPO maintains off-policy critic networks to maximize data reuse and propagates policy gradients through the full generative process via a modified PPO objective, using critics as terminal reward. It introduces stabilizers: success-buffer regularization, conservative advantages, chi-squared regularization, and Q-variance reduction.
AI.result: OGPO achieves state-of-the-art performance on manipulation tasks including multi-task settings, high-precision insertion, and dexterous control. It is the only method that fine-tunes poorly-initialized BC policies to near full task-success with no expert data in the online replay buffer, with minimal hyperparameter tuning.
AI.conclusion: OGPO drastically outperforms alternative methods on policy steering and learning residual corrections. The paper also provides a systematic empirical study of GCP finetuning, identifying key stabilizing mechanisms and failure modes for successful off-policy full-policy improvement.

title: Dynamic Distillation and Gradient Consistency for Robust Long-Tailed Incremental Learning
summary: The task of Long-tailed Class Incremental Learning (LT-CIL) addresses the sequential learning of new classes from datasets with imbalanced class distributions. This scenario intensifies the fundamental problem of catastrophic forgetting, inherent to continual learning, with the dual challenges of under-learning minority classes and overfitting majority classes. To tackle these combined issues, this paper proposes two main techniques. First, we introduce gradient consistency regularization, which leverages the moving average of gradients to suppress abrupt fluctuations and stabilize the training process. Second, we dynamically adjust the weight of the distillation loss by measuring the degree of class imbalance with normalized entropy. This adaptive weighting establishes an optimal balance between retaining old knowledge and acquiring new information. Experiments on the CIFAR-100-LT, ImageNetSubset-LT, and Food101-LT benchmarks show that our method achieves consistent accuracy improvements of up to 5.0\%. Furthermore, we demonstrate dramatic gains in the challenging 'In-ordered' setting, where tasks progress from majority to minority classes, highlighting our method's robustness in mitigating forgetting under unfavorable learning dynamics. This enhanced performance is achieved without a significant increase in computational overhead, demonstrating the practicality of our framework.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes gradient consistency regularization and adaptive distillation loss weighting to address catastrophic forgetting in Long-tailed Class Incremental Learning, achieving up to 5% accuracy improvements on benchmarks without significant computational overhead.
AI.motivation: Long-tailed Class Incremental Learning (LT-CIL) intensifies the catastrophic forgetting problem with dual challenges: under-learning minority classes and overfitting majority classes, which existing continual learning methods fail to adequately address.
AI.method: (1) Gradient consistency regularization using moving average of gradients to suppress fluctuations and stabilize training; (2) Dynamically adjusted distillation loss weight based on normalized entropy measuring class imbalance, balancing old knowledge retention and new knowledge acquisition.
AI.result: Consistent accuracy improvements up to 5.0% on CIFAR-100-LT, ImageNetSubset-LT, and Food101-LT benchmarks; dramatic gains in the 'In-ordered' setting (majority→minority classes); no significant computational overhead increase.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively mitigates forgetting in LT-CIL with practical computational efficiency, particularly robust under unfavorable learning dynamics where tasks progress from majority to minority classes.

title: Predicting Euler Characteristics and Constructing Topological Structure Using Machine Learning Techniques
comment: Corresponding authors: Hee Young Kwon and Changyeon Won
summary: This study proposes a novel approach to extract topological properties, specifically the Euler characteristic, from input images using neural networks without relying on large pre-existing datasets but with a single geometric image. Inspired by solid-state physics, where topological properties of magnetic structures are derived from spin field analysis, our model generates a unit vector field from an image, interpreted as a spin configuration. The Euler characteristic is then predicted by computing the skyrmion number of this generated spin configuration. Remarkably, the network learns to construct chiral magnetic textures without access to ground-truth chiral spin configurations, relying instead on only a single, simple geometric image and the straightforward skyrmion number computation. Furthermore, spin configurations generated by independently trained networks can be non-unique due to inherent degrees of freedom. To constrain these degrees of freedom and further refine the spin configuration, we incorporate a magnetic Hamiltonian, comprising exchange interaction, Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya (DM) interaction, and anisotropy, as an additional, physics-informed loss function. We validate the model's efficacy on complex geometrical shapes and demonstrate its applicability to practical tasks.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a neural network approach that extracts topological properties (Euler characteristic) from images by generating spin configurations and computing skyrmion numbers, using only a single geometric image for training, with physics-informed loss (magnetic Hamiltonian) to constrain solutions.
AI.motivation: To extract topological properties (Euler characteristic) from images using neural networks without requiring large pre-existing datasets, inspired by solid-state physics where topological properties of magnetic structures are derived from spin field analysis.
AI.method: A neural network generates a unit vector field (spin configuration) from an input image; the Euler characteristic is predicted via skyrmion number computation; a magnetic Hamiltonian (exchange, DM interaction, anisotropy) is added as a physics-informed loss to constrain non-unique spin configurations.
AI.result: Validated on complex geometrical shapes, demonstrating that the model can learn to construct chiral magnetic textures without ground-truth spin configurations and produce meaningful topological predictions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively extracts topological properties using minimal data (single image) and physics-informed constraints, showing promise for practical topological analysis tasks.

title: Replacing Parameters with Preferences: Federated Alignment of Heterogeneous Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have broad potential in privacy-sensitive domains such as healthcare and finance, yet strict data-sharing constraints render centralized training infeasible. Federated Learning mitigates this issue by enabling decentralized training, but practical deployments face challenges due to client heterogeneity in computational resources, application requirements, and model architectures. Under extreme model and data heterogeneity, replacing parameter aggregation with preference-based collaboration offers a more suitable interface, as it eliminates the need for direct parameter or data exchange. Motivated by this, we propose MoR, a federated alignment framework that combines GRPO with Mixture-of-Rewards for heterogeneous VLMs. In MoR, each client locally trains a reward model from local preference annotations, capturing specific evaluation signals without exposing raw data. To combine these heterogeneous supervision signals, MoR introduces a Mixture-of-Rewards mechanism with learned routing, which adaptively fuses client reward models according to the input and alignment objective. The server then optimizes a base VLM using GRPO with a KL penalty to a reference model, enabling preference alignment without requiring client models to share architectures or parameters. Experiments on diverse public vision-language benchmarks demonstrate that MoR consistently outperforms federated alignment baselines in generalization and cross-client adaptability. Our approach provides a scalable solution for privacy-preserving alignment of heterogeneous VLMs under federated settings.

AI.tldr: MoR is a federated alignment framework using GRPO + Mixture-of-Rewards to align heterogeneous VLMs without sharing raw data, parameters, or architectures, achieving strong generalization across clients.
AI.motivation: VLMs are useful in privacy-sensitive domains (healthcare, finance) but data-sharing constraints prevent centralized training. Federated learning faces challenges from client heterogeneity in resources, requirements, and architectures, making parameter aggregation impractical. There is a need for preference-based collaboration that avoids direct parameter/data exchange.
AI.method: MoR combines GRPO with Mixture-of-Rewards. Each client trains a local reward model from private preference annotations. A Mixture-of-Rewards mechanism with learned routing adaptively fuses these heterogeneous reward signals. The server optimizes a base VLM using GRPO with KL penalty to a reference model for preference alignment.
AI.result: Experiments on diverse vision-language benchmarks show MoR consistently outperforms federated alignment baselines in generalization and cross-client adaptability.
AI.conclusion: MoR provides a scalable, privacy-preserving solution for aligning heterogeneous VLMs in federated settings without requiring shared architectures or parameter exchange.

title: Dual-Foundation Models for Unsupervised Domain Adaptation
comment: 15 pages, 6 figures. Accepted at the 28th International Conference on Pattern Recognition (ICPR 2026)
summary: Semantic segmentation provides pixel-level scene understanding essential for autonomous driving and fine-grained perception tasks. However, training segmentation models requires costly, labor-intensive annotations on real-world datasets. Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA) addresses this by training models on labeled synthetic data and adapting them to unlabeled real images. While conceptually simple, adaptation is challenging due to the domain gap, i.e., differences in visual appearance and scene structure between synthetic and real data. Prior approaches bridge this gap through pixel-level mixing or feature-level contrastive learning. Yet, these techniques suffer from two major limitations: (1) reliance on high-confidence pseudo-labels restricts learning to a subset of the target domain, and (2) prototype-based contrastive methods initialize class prototypes from source-trained models, yielding biased and unstable anchors during adaptation. To address these issues, we propose a dual-foundation UDA framework that leverages two complementary foundation models. First, we employ the Segment Anything Model (SAM) with superpixel-guided prompting to enable learning from a broader range of target pixels beyond high-confidence predictions. Second, we incorporate DINOv3 to construct stable, domain-invariant class prototypes through its robust representation learning. Our method achieves consistent improvements of +1.3% and +1.4% mIoU over strong UDA baselines on GTA-to-Cityscapes and SYNTHIA-to-Cityscapes, respectively.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a dual-foundation UDA framework using SAM with superpixel-guided prompting to expand target pixel learning and DINOv3 for stable class prototypes, achieving +1.3% and +1.4% mIoU improvements on standard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing UDA methods suffer from two limitations: (1) reliance on high-confidence pseudo-labels restricts learning to a subset of the target domain, and (2) prototype-based contrastive methods use biased and unstable anchors initialized from source-trained models.
AI.method: The method uses two complementary foundation models: SAM with superpixel-guided prompting to enable broader target pixel learning beyond high-confidence predictions, and DINOv3 to construct stable, domain-invariant class prototypes via robust representation learning.
AI.result: Achieves consistent improvements of +1.3% mIoU on GTA-to-Cityscapes and +1.4% mIoU on SYNTHIA-to-Cityscapes over strong UDA baselines.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging dual foundation models (SAM and DINOv3) effectively addresses pseudo-label and prototype bias issues in unsupervised domain adaptation for semantic segmentation.

title: AsymK-Talker: Real-Time and Long-Horizon Talking Head Generation via Asymmetric Kernel Distillation
summary: Recent advances in diffusion models have markedly enhanced the visual fidelity of audio-driven talking head generation. Nevertheless, existing methods are constrained by three critical limitations: causal inefficiency that impedes real-time inference, incompatibility with temporally coherent conditioning, and progressive drift over long-horizon generation, collectively hindering their deployment in real-time applications. To overcome these challenges, we introduce AsymK-Talker, a novel diffusion-distillation method designed for real-time and long-horizon talking head generation. AsymK-Talker comprises three key components: (1) Kernel-Conditioned Loop Generation (KCLG), a causal, chunk-wise generation paradigm that leverages motion kernels to enable temporally consistent propagation; (2) Temporal Reference Encoding (TRE), which converts a static identity reference into a time-aware latent representation to enhance audio-visual synchronization; and (3) Asymmetric Kernel Distillation (AKD), a teacher-student distillation framework wherein the teacher model conditions on ground-truth motion kernels for supervision, while the student learns to generate from generated kernels, thereby ensuring robustness during extended generation sequences. AsymK-Talker achieves promising results on both visual fidelity and lip synchronization metrics.

AI.tldr: AsymK-Talker is a diffusion-distillation method for real-time, long-horizon talking head generation, addressing causal inefficiency, temporal incoherence, and progressive drift via Kernel-Conditioned Loop Generation, Temporal Reference Encoding, and Asymmetric Kernel Distillation.
AI.motivation: Existing audio-driven talking head generation methods face three key limitations: (1) causal inefficiency preventing real-time inference, (2) incompatibility with temporally coherent conditioning, and (3) progressive drift over long sequences. These collectively hinder real-time deployment.
AI.method: AsymK-Talker has three components: (1) Kernel-Conditioned Loop Generation (KCLG) - a causal, chunk-wise generation using motion kernels for temporally consistent propagation; (2) Temporal Reference Encoding (TRE) - converts static identity into time-aware latent representation for better audio-visual synchronization; (3) Asymmetric Kernel Distillation (AKD) - teacher-student framework where teacher uses ground-truth motion kernels while student learns from generated kernels for robustness in long sequences.
AI.result: AsymK-Talker achieves promising results on both visual fidelity and lip synchronization metrics.
AI.conclusion: The proposed AsymK-Talker effectively overcomes causal inefficiency, temporal incoherence, and progressive drift, making it suitable for real-time and long-horizon talking head generation applications.

title: FinSTaR: Towards Financial Reasoning with Time Series Reasoning Models
summary: Time series (TS) reasoning models (TSRMs) have shown promising capabilities in general domains, yet they consistently fail on financial domain, which exhibit unique characteristics. We propose a general 2x2 capability taxonomy for TSRMs by crossing 1) single-entity vs. multi-entity analysis with 2) assessment of the current state vs. prediction of future behavior. We instantiate this taxonomy in the financial domain -- where the distinction between deterministic assessment and stochastic prediction is particularly critical -- as ten financial reasoning tasks, forming the FinTSR-Bench benchmark based on S&P stocks. To this end, we propose FinSTaR (Financial Time Series Thinking and Reasoning), trained on FinTSR-Bench with distinct chain-of-thought (CoT) strategies tailored to each category. For assessment, which is deterministic (i.e., computable from observable data), we employ Compute-in-CoT, a programmatic CoT that enables models to derive answers directly from raw prices. For prediction, which is inherently stochastic (i.e., subject to unobservable factors), we adopt Scenario-Aware CoT, which generates diverse scenarios before making a judgment, mirroring how financial analysts reason under uncertainty. The proposed method achieves 78.9% average accuracy on FinTSR-Bench, substantially outperforming LLM and TSRM baselines. Furthermore, we show that the four capability categories are complementary and mutually reinforcing through joint training, and that Scenario-Aware CoT consistently improves prediction accuracy over standard CoT. Code is publicly available at: https://github.com/seunghan96/FinSTaR.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes FinTSR-Bench, a financial time series reasoning benchmark with a 2x2 taxonomy (single/multi-entity × assessment/prediction), and FinSTaR, a model trained with two specialized CoT strategies (Compute-in-CoT for deterministic assessment, Scenario-Aware CoT for stochastic prediction), achieving 78.9% accuracy and outperforming existing LLM/TSRM baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing time series reasoning models (TSRMs) perform well in general domains but consistently fail in the financial domain due to its unique characteristics. There is a need for a structured capability taxonomy and specialized reasoning strategies tailored to financial time series.
AI.method: Proposes a 2x2 capability taxonomy (single/multi-entity × assessment/prediction), instantiated as ten financial reasoning tasks in FinTSR-Bench using S&P stocks. FinSTaR is trained with two distinct CoT strategies: Compute-in-CoT (programmatic, deterministic) for assessment tasks and Scenario-Aware CoT (generating diverse scenarios) for prediction tasks.
AI.result: FinSTaR achieves 78.9% average accuracy on FinTSR-Bench, significantly outperforming LLM and TSRM baselines. Shows that the four capability categories are complementary and mutually reinforcing, and Scenario-Aware CoT consistently improves prediction accuracy over standard CoT.
AI.conclusion: The proposed taxonomy and specialized CoT strategies effectively address the unique challenges of financial time series reasoning, with different capability categories benefiting from joint training and prediction tasks requiring scenario-based reasoning under uncertainty.

title: Say the Mission, Execute the Swarm: Agent-Enhanced LLM Reasoning in the Web-of-Drones
comment: 15 pages, 5 figures. This paper has been accepted for presentation at the 27th IEEE International Symposium on a World of Wireless, Mobile and Multimedia Networks (WoWMoM 2026)
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly explored as high-level reasoning engines for cyber-physical systems, yet their application to real-time UAV swarm management remains challenging due to heterogeneous interfaces, limited grounding, and the need for long-running closed-loop execution. This paper presents a mission-agnostic, agent-enhanced LLM framework for UAV swarm control, where users express mission objectives in natural language and the system autonomously executes them through grounded, real-time interactions. The proposed architecture combines an LLM-based Agent Core with a Model Context Protocol (MCP) gateway and a Web-of-Drones abstraction based on W3C Web of Things (WoT) standards. By exposing drones, sensors, and services as standardized WoT Things, the framework enables structured tool-based interaction, continuous state observation, and safe actuation without relying on code generation. We evaluate the framework using ArduPilot-based simulation across four swarm missions and six state-of-the-art LLMs. Results show that, despite strong reasoning abilities, current general-purpose LLMs still struggle to achieve reliable execution - even for simple swarm tasks - when operating without explicit grounding and execution support. Task-specific planning tools and runtime guardrails substantially improve robustness, while token consumption alone is not indicative of execution quality or reliability.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an LLM-based framework for UAV swarm control using standardized WoT abstractions and MCP gateway, but finds that current general-purpose LLMs still struggle with reliable execution in real-time cyber-physical systems.
AI.motivation: LLMs are promising for cyber-physical reasoning but face challenges in real-time UAV swarm management due to heterogeneous interfaces, limited grounding, and need for long-running closed-loop execution.
AI.method: The framework combines an LLM-based Agent Core, an MCP gateway, and a Web-of-Drones abstraction based on W3C WoT standards to enable structured tool-based interaction and safe actuation without code generation.
AI.result: Evaluated across four swarm missions and six LLMs; current LLMs struggle with reliable execution even for simple tasks. Task-specific planning tools and runtime guardrails improve robustness, while token consumption alone doesn't indicate execution quality.
AI.conclusion: Current general-purpose LLMs need explicit grounding and execution support for reliable UAV swarm control; task-specific tools and guardrails are essential for robustness.

title: SoDa2: Single-Stage Open-Set Domain Adaptation via Decoupled Alignment for Cross-Scene Hyperspectral Image Classification
summary: Cross-scene hyperspectral image (HSI) classification stands as a fundamental research topic in remote sensing, with extensive applications spanning various fields. Owing to the inclusion of unknown categories in the target domain and the existence of domain shift across different scenes, open-set domain adaptation techniques are commonly employed to address cross-scene HSI classification. However, existing open-set cross-scene HSI classification methods still face two critical challenges: (1) domain shift issues arising from the direct alignment of mixed spectral-spatial features; (2) high computational costs caused by two-stage training strategies. To address these issues, this paper proposes a single-stage open-set domain adaptation method with decoupled alignment (SoDa$^2$) for cross-scene HSI classification. A contribution-aware dual-modality feature extraction is customized to disentangle the characteristics from spectral sequence signals and spatial details, selectively and adaptively enhancing discriminative features. The decoupled alignment module minimizes the Maximum Mean Discrepancy to independently reduce the spectral discrepancy and the spatial discrepancy between the source and target domains, extracting more fine-grained domain-invariant features. A cost-effective single-stage dual-branch framework is designed to learn MMD-constrainted aligned features and constraint-free intrinsic features for adaptive distinction between known and unknown classes. This framework employs a Gaussian Mixture Model to model the squared cosine similarity distribution between the two feature types, enabling open-set recognition without prior knowledge of unknown classes. Extensive experiments on three groups of HSI datasets demonstrate that SoDa$^2$ outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior classification accuracy and model transferability for open-set cross-scene tasks.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes SoDa$^2$, a single-stage open-set domain adaptation method with decoupled alignment for cross-scene HSI classification. It uses contribution-aware dual-modality feature extraction and decoupled alignment to reduce spectral and spatial discrepancies independently, achieving superior performance on three HSI datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing open-set cross-scene HSI classification methods face two critical challenges: (1) domain shift issues from direct alignment of mixed spectral-spatial features; (2) high computational costs caused by two-stage training strategies.
AI.method: SoDa$^2$ proposes: (1) contribution-aware dual-modality feature extraction to disentangle spectral and spatial features; (2) decoupled alignment module minimizing MMD to independently reduce spectral and spatial discrepancies; (3) single-stage dual-branch framework learning MMD-constrained aligned features and constraint-free intrinsic features, using GMM to model squared cosine similarity distribution for open-set recognition.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on three groups of HSI datasets demonstrate SoDa$^2$ outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior classification accuracy and model transferability for open-set cross-scene tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed single-stage decoupled alignment approach effectively addresses domain shift and computational cost issues, providing a more efficient and accurate solution for open-set cross-scene HSI classification.

title: Disease Is a Spectral Perturbation
summary: We propose a novel method of understanding disease transformation from a healthy baseline with biomarker-level explainability. By modeling the biomarker covariance matrices of healthy controls and disease states, the perturbation can be individually characterized to accomplish mechanistic explanations of disease trajectories, both at a molecular level and for individual patients. Given a cohort of n patients each measured on p biomarkers, we define the biomarker "Hamiltonian" H = X^T X / n \in R^{p \times p}, where X \in R^{n \times p} is the covariant biomarker matrix. The eigenvectors of H define a set of normal modes of biomarker coordination, and the eigenvalues quantify the energy carried by each mode. In the healthy state, the reference Hamiltonian H_0 governs this structure where disease perturbs H_0 by an additive operator ΔH, thus shifting eigenvalues and rotating eigenvectors in proportion to the severity of pathological disruption. We formalize this framework, derive the spectral change given a disease perturbation, and demonstrate that the projection of a newly diagnosed patient's cumulative biomarker covariance structure onto disease-discriminant eigenmodes constitutes an optimal prognostic statistic for greater precision in disease prognosis. This work serves as a veritable white paper with application across a panoply of disease frameworks from cancer to neurodegenerative disorders.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Hamiltonian-based framework for modeling disease progression from a healthy baseline, using spectral analysis of biomarker covariance matrices to derive prognostic statistics and mechanistic explanations at both molecular and individual patient levels.
AI.motivation: To understand disease transformation from a healthy baseline with biomarker-level explainability, enabling mechanistic explanations of disease trajectories at molecular and individual levels.
AI.method: Defines a biomarker "Hamiltonian" H = X^T X / n from covariant biomarker data. Uses spectral decomposition (eigenvalues/eigenvectors) to define normal modes. Models disease as a perturbation ΔH to the healthy reference Hamiltonian H_0, shifting eigenvalues and rotating eigenvectors proportional to pathology severity.
AI.result: Projecting a newly diagnosed patient's biomarker covariance onto disease-discriminant eigenmodes constitutes an optimal prognostic statistic for greater precision in disease prognosis.
AI.conclusion: This Hamiltonian framework provides a versatile approach applicable across diverse disease frameworks (cancer to neurodegenerative disorders), offering both mechanistic insight and improved prognostic precision.

title: Real-Time Evaluation of Autonomous Systems under Adversarial Attacks
summary:
Most evaluations of autonomous driving policies under adversarial conditions are conducted in simulation, due to cost efficiency and the absence of physical risk. However, purely virtual testing fails to capture structural inconsistencies, supervision constraints, and state-representation effects that arise in real-world data and fundamentally shape policy robustness. This work presents an offline trajectory-learning and adversarial robustness evaluation framework grounded in real-world intersection driving data. Within a controlled data contract, we train and compare three trajectory-learning paradigms: Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP)-based Behavior Cloning (BC), Transformer-based object-tokenized BC, and inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) formulated within a Generative Adversarial Imitation Learning (GAIL) framework. Models are evaluated using Average Displacement Error (ADE) and Final Displacement Error (FDE).
  Inference-time robustness is assessed by subjecting trained policies to gradient-based adversarial perturbations across multiple intersection scenarios, yielding a structured robustness evaluation matrix. Results show that state-structure design and architectural inductive biases critically influence adversarial stability, leading to markedly different robustness profiles despite comparable nominal prediction accuracy (ADE < 0.08). Inference-time Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) attacks induce final displacement errors of up to approximately 8 meters. The proposed framework establishes a scalable benchmark for studying offline trajectory learning and adversarial robustness in real-world autonomous driving settings.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an offline trajectory-learning and adversarial robustness evaluation framework using real-world intersection driving data. It compares MLP-based Behavior Cloning, Transformer-based object-tokenized BC, and GAIL-based IRL, evaluating their robustness against gradient-based adversarial perturbations. Results show that architectural inductive biases significantly affect adversarial stability, with PGD attacks causing displacement errors up to 8 meters, even when models have similar nominal accuracy.
AI.motivation: Most autonomous driving policy evaluations under adversarial conditions are done purely in simulation, which fails to capture real-world structural inconsistencies, supervision constraints, and state-representation effects that fundamentally impact policy robustness. The authors aim to bridge this gap by creating an evaluation framework grounded in real-world driving data.
AI.method: The authors use real-world intersection driving data within a controlled data contract to train three trajectory-learning paradigms: MLP-based Behavior Cloning, Transformer-based object-tokenized BC, and IRL within a GAIL framework. Robustness is assessed via inference-time gradient-based adversarial perturbations (PGD attacks) across multiple intersection scenarios, producing a structured robustness evaluation matrix.
AI.result: State-structure design and architectural inductive biases critically influence adversarial stability. Models show markedly different robustness profiles despite comparable nominal prediction accuracy (ADE < 0.08). Inference-time PGD attacks induce final displacement errors up to approximately 8 meters.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework establishes a scalable benchmark for studying offline trajectory learning and adversarial robustness in real-world autonomous driving settings, highlighting the importance of architecture choice beyond nominal accuracy.

title: SOAR: Real-Time Joint Optimization of Order Allocation and Robot Scheduling in Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems
summary: Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems (RMFS) rely on mobile robots for automated inventory transportation, coordinating order allocation and robot scheduling to enhance warehousing efficiency. However, optimizing RMFS is challenging due to strict real-time constraints and the strong coupling of multi-phase decisions. Existing methods either decompose the problem into isolated sub-tasks to guarantee responsiveness at the cost of global optimality, or rely on computationally expensive global optimization models that are unsuitable for dynamic industrial environments. To bridge this gap, we propose SOAR, a unified Deep Reinforcement Learning framework for real-time joint optimization. SOAR transforms order allocation and robot scheduling into a unified process by utilizing soft order allocations as observations. We formulate this as an Event-Driven Markov Decision Process, enabling the agent to perform simultaneous scheduling in response to asynchronous system events. Technically, we employ a Heterogeneous Graph Transformer to encode the warehouse state and integrate phased domain knowledge. Additionally, we incorporate a reward shaping strategy to address sparse feedback in long-horizon tasks. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world industrial datasets, in collaboration with Geekplus, demonstrate that SOAR reduces global makespan by 7.5\% and average order completion time by 15.4\% with sub-100ms latency. Furthermore, sim-to-real deployment confirms its practical viability and significant performance gains in production environments. The code is available at https://github.com/200815147/SOAR.

AI.tldr: SOAR is a unified Deep Reinforcement Learning framework for real-time joint optimization of order allocation and robot scheduling in Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems, achieving significant efficiency gains with sub-100ms latency.
AI.motivation: Existing RMFS optimization methods either sacrifice global optimality for real-time responsiveness by decomposing into isolated sub-tasks, or rely on computationally expensive global models unsuitable for dynamic industrial environments. There is a need for a joint optimization approach that meets real-time constraints.
AI.method: SOAR uses an Event-Driven Markov Decision Process for unified order allocation and robot scheduling, with soft order allocations as observations. It employs a Heterogeneous Graph Transformer for encoding warehouse state and integrates phased domain knowledge with reward shaping to handle sparse feedback.
AI.result: On synthetic and real-world industrial datasets (in collaboration with Geekplus), SOAR reduces global makespan by 7.5% and average order completion time by 15.4% with sub-100ms latency. Sim-to-real deployment confirms practical viability.
AI.conclusion: SOAR effectively bridges the gap between real-time responsiveness and global optimality for RMFS optimization, demonstrating significant performance gains in production environments.

title: Enhancing Self-Supervised Talking Head Forgery Detection via a Training-Free Dual-System Framework
summary: Supervised talking head forgery detection faces severe generalization challenges due to the continuous evolution of generators. By reducing reliance on generator-specific forgery patterns, self-supervised detectors offer stronger cross-generator robustness. However, existing research has mainly focused on building stronger detectors, while the discriminative capacity of trained detectors remains insufficiently exploited. In particular, for score-based self-supervised detectors, the limited discriminative ability on hard cases is often reflected in unreliable anomaly ordering, leaving room for further refinement. Motivated by this observation, we draw inspiration from the dual-system theory of human cognition and propose a Training-Free Dual-System (TFDS) framework to further exploit the latent discriminative capacity of existing score-based self-supervised detectors. TFDS treats anomaly-like scores as the basis of System-1, using lightweight threshold-based routing to partition samples into confident and uncertain subsets. System-2 then revisits only the uncertain subset, performing fine-grained evidence-guided reasoning to refine the relative ordering of ambiguous samples within the original score distribution. Extensive experiments demonstrate consistent improvements across datasets and perturbation settings, with the gains arising mainly from corrected ordering within the uncertain subset. These findings show that existing self-supervised talking head forgery detectors still contain underexploited discriminative cues that can be effectively unlocked through training-free dual-system reasoning.

AI.tldr: Proposes Training-Free Dual-System (TFDS) framework that exploits latent discriminative capacity of existing self-supervised talking head forgery detectors by treating anomaly scores as System-1 and refining uncertain cases via System-2 evidence-guided reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing self-supervised detectors for talking head forgery detection have limited discriminative ability on hard cases, reflected in unreliable anomaly ordering, leaving room for further refinement without building stronger detectors.
AI.method: Inspired by dual-system theory of human cognition, TFDS uses System-1 (anomaly-like scores + lightweight threshold-based routing) to partition samples into confident and uncertain subsets, then System-2 revisits only uncertain subset for fine-grained evidence-guided reasoning to refine ordering.
AI.result: Consistent improvements across datasets and perturbation settings, with gains arising mainly from corrected ordering within the uncertain subset.
AI.conclusion: Existing self-supervised talking head forgery detectors contain underexploited discriminative cues that can be effectively unlocked through training-free dual-system reasoning.

title: Kernel Affine Hull Machines for Compute-Efficient Query-Side Semantic Encoding
summary: Transformer-based semantic retrieval is highly effective, yet in many deployments the dominant cost lies in online query encoding rather than corpus indexing. We study the fixed-teacher query-adaptation problem and ask whether repeated neural inference can be replaced by a lightweight, analytically explicit estimator without degrading decision-relevant retrieval quality. We propose Kernel Affine Hull Machines (KAHMs), which map inexpensive lexical features into a frozen semantic embedding space by estimating prototype-mixture weights in a rigorously specified RKHS and refining prototypes via normalized least-mean-squares, yielding a transparent decomposition of encoding error into posterior-approximation, generalization, and teacher-noise components. On a controlled Austrian-law benchmark (5,000 queries; 84 laws; 10,762 units), KAHM attains the strongest teacher-space reconstruction among matched learned adapters (MSE 0.000091, R^2 0.9071, cosine 0.9536) and consistently leads rank-sensitive metrics, including mean reciprocal rank at 20 (MRR@20, the average inverse rank of the first relevant result within the top 20), Hit rate at 20 (Hit@20, the fraction of queries with at least one relevant result in the top 20), and Top-1 accuracy (the fraction of queries whose correct item is ranked first), with scores of 0.504, 0.694, and 0.411, respectively. It also reduces per-query latency by a factor of 8.5 relative to direct transformer encoding. These results demonstrate that, in fixed-teacher regimes, lightweight geometric estimators can substitute for online neural encoding, preserving retrieval performance while substantially improving efficiency and interpretability.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Kernel Affine Hull Machines (KAHMs) to replace expensive online query encoding with lightweight, analytically explicit estimators in fixed-teacher semantic retrieval. KAHM achieves strong reconstruction quality (MSE 0.000091, R² 0.9071) and competitive retrieval metrics (MRR@20 0.504, Hit@20 0.694, Top-1 0.411) while reducing per-query latency by 8.5x on an Austrian-law benchmark.
AI.motivation: The dominant cost in many transformer-based semantic retrieval deployments is online query encoding rather than corpus indexing. The authors aim to replace heavy repeated neural inference with a lightweight, interpretable estimator without sacrificing retrieval quality.
AI.method: KAHM maps inexpensive lexical features into a frozen semantic embedding space by estimating prototype-mixture weights in a rigorously specified RKHS and refining prototypes via normalized least-mean-squares. The encoding error is decomposed into posterior-approximation, generalization, and teacher-noise components.
AI.result: On a controlled Austrian-law benchmark (5,000 queries; 84 laws; 10,762 units), KAHM achieves the strongest teacher-space reconstruction (MSE 0.000091, R² 0.9071, cosine 0.9536), leads in MRR@20 (0.504), Hit@20 (0.694), and Top-1 accuracy (0.411), and reduces per-query latency by 8.5x vs. direct transformer encoding.
AI.conclusion: In fixed-teacher regimes, lightweight geometric estimators like KAHM can substitute for online neural encoding, preserving retrieval performance while substantially improving efficiency and interpretability.

title: Workspace-Bench 1.0: Benchmarking AI Agents on Workspace Tasks with Large-Scale File Dependencies
summary: Workspace learning requires AI agents to identify, reason over, exploit, and update explicit and implicit dependencies among heterogeneous files in a worker's workspace, enabling them to complete both routine and advanced tasks effectively. Despite its importance, existing relevant benchmarks largely evaluate agents on pre-specified or synthesized files with limited real-world dependencies, leaving workspace-level evaluation underexplored. To this end, we introduce Workspace-Bench, a benchmark for evaluating AI agents on Workspace Learning invOlving Large-Scale File Dependencies. We construct realistic workspaces with 5 worker profiles, 74 file types, 20,476 files (up to 20GB) and curate 388 tasks, each with its own file dependency graph, evaluated across 7,399 total rubrics that require cross-file retrieval, contextual reasoning, and adaptive decision-making. We further provide Workspace-Bench-Lite, a 100-task subset that preserves the benchmark distribution while reducing evaluation costs by about 70%. We evaluate 4 popular agent harnesses and 7 foundation models. Experimental results show that current agents remain far from reliable workspace learning, where the best reaches only 68.7%, substantially below the human result of 80.7%, and the average performance across agents is only 47.4%.

AI.tldr: Workspace-Bench is a benchmark for evaluating AI agents on workspace learning with large-scale file dependencies, featuring realistic workspaces (20K+ files, 74 types), 388 tasks with dependency graphs, and 7,399 rubrics. Current AI agents perform poorly (best 68.7%, average 47.4%) compared to humans (80.7%).
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks evaluate agents on pre-specified or synthesized files with limited real-world dependencies, leaving workspace-level evaluation (where agents must handle explicit and implicit dependencies among heterogeneous files) underexplored.
AI.method: Constructed realistic workspaces with 5 worker profiles, 74 file types, 20,476 files (up to 20GB), curated 388 tasks with individual file dependency graphs, evaluated across 7,399 rubrics. Also created Workspace-Bench-Lite (100 tasks, ~70% cost reduction). Tested 4 agent harnesses and 7 foundation models.
AI.result: Best agent achieves only 68.7% vs human 80.7%; average performance across agents is 47.4%. Current agents remain far from reliable workspace learning.
AI.conclusion: Workspace learning with large-scale file dependencies remains a significant challenge for current AI agents, highlighting a substantial gap between AI and human performance in this domain.

title: MASRA: MLLM-Assisted Semantic-Relational Consistent Alignment for Video Temporal Grounding
summary: Video Temporal Grounding (VTG) faces a cross-modal semantic gap that often leads to background features being incorrectly aligned with the query, while directly matching the query to moments results in insufficient discriminability and consistency of temporal semantics. To address this issue, we propose MLLM-Assisted Semantic-Relational Consistent Alignment (MASRA), a training-time MLLM-based optimization framework for VTG. MASRA leverages an MLLM during training to produce two forms of textual priors, namely event-level descriptions with temporal spans and clip-level captions, and instantiates two MLLM-assisted alignments. Event Semantic Temporal Alignment (ESTA) aligns temporal context with event semantics to explicitly strengthen the correspondence between semantics and temporal events and improve span-level separability. Local Relational Consistency Alignment (LRCA) constructs a textual relation matrix derived from clip-level captions and aligns it with the temporal feature similarity matrix in the model, enhancing temporal consistency while capturing local structural information. MASRA includes two simple supporting modules, semantic-guided enhancement and second-order relational attention, to better utilize the learned semantic context and relational structure. Moreover, we introduce Decoupled Alignment Interaction (DAI) with a context-aware codebook to adaptively absorb query-irrelevant semantics and alleviate the cross-modal gap. The MLLM is only invoked during training and is not used at inference. Extensive experiments show that MASRA outperforms existing methods, and ablation studies validate its effectiveness.

AI.tldr: MASRA is a training-time MLLM-based optimization framework for Video Temporal Grounding that uses textual priors (event descriptions and clip captions) from an MLLM to improve cross-modal alignment via Event Semantic Temporal Alignment (ESTA) and Local Relational Consistency Alignment (LRCA), achieving SOTA results.
AI.motivation: VTG suffers from a cross-modal semantic gap where background features misalign with queries and direct moment-query matching lacks discriminability and temporal consistency.
AI.method: Uses MLLM during training to generate event-level descriptions (with temporal spans) and clip-level captions, then applies two alignments: ESTA (aligns temporal context with event semantics for span separability) and LRCA (aligns text relation matrix with temporal feature similarity matrix). Includes semantic-guided enhancement, second-order relational attention, and Decoupled Alignment Interaction (DAI) with a context-aware codebook to absorb query-irrelevant semantics.
AI.result: Outperforms existing methods on VTG benchmarks; ablation studies validate the effectiveness of each component.
AI.conclusion: MASRA effectively bridges the cross-modal semantic gap in VTG by leveraging MLLM-derived textual priors at training time, with no extra inference cost, and sets new SOTA performance.

title: Calibration of the underlying surface parameters for urban flood using latent variables and adjoint equation
comment: 27 pages, 8 figures, 2 table, submitted to Journal of Flood Risk Management
summary: Calibrating the urban underlying surface parameters is crucial for urban flood simulation. We formulate the parameter calibration problem into an optimization problem within the Bayesian framework using the maximum likelihood principle. We adopt the urban flood dynamical system model as the surrogate model and innovatively introduce latent variables inspired by machine learning to represent more uncertainties, which can also be compatible with common physical parameter calibration. For more efficient optimization, we construct the adjoint equation of the surrogate model to obtain gradient information and propose the parameter sharing technique and the localization technique to reduce the computation complexity of the adjoint equation. A simple case verifies the proposed method can converge quickly and is insensitive to the observation time interval. In the case derived from Test 8A, we calibrate Manning's coefficient of urban roads, with a maximum relative error of 13.88% and a minimum of 1.16%.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a Bayesian optimization framework for calibrating urban underlying surface parameters in flood simulation, using a dynamical system surrogate model with latent variables, adjoint-based gradients, and techniques like parameter sharing and localization to improve efficiency. The method converges quickly and achieves calibration errors between 1.16% and 13.88% for Manning's coefficient.
AI.motivation: Calibrating urban underlying surface parameters is critical for accurate urban flood simulation, yet remains a challenging optimization problem.
AI.method: The problem is formulated as a Bayesian optimization using maximum likelihood. A dynamical system surrogate model is used, with latent variables introduced (inspired by machine learning) to capture more uncertainties. The adjoint equation of the surrogate is constructed for gradient information. Parameter sharing and localization techniques reduce computational complexity of the adjoint.
AI.result: In Test 8A, Manning's coefficient of urban roads was calibrated with relative errors ranging from 1.16% (minimum) to 13.88% (maximum). The method converges quickly and is insensitive to observation time intervals.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Bayesian calibration framework with adjoint-based gradients and computational acceleration techniques is effective for urban flood parameter calibration, showing robust convergence and accuracy.

title: Where Paths Split: Localized, Calibrated Control of Moral Reasoning in Large Language Models
summary: Large language models often display heterogeneous moral preferences across settings. We study inference-time steering toward a desired ethical framework while preserving general competence. We present Convergent-Divergent Routing, which traces and edits minimal branch points inside transformer blocks where ethical-framework-related pathways first converge and then diverge. Gating non-target branches at these loci blocks the downstream propagation while leaving upstream computations intact. We find that this intervention alone increases targeted ethical-framework reasoning. To achieve fine-grained control, we adapt Common Spatial Patterns to the residual stream and extract, for each branch-point layer, a pair of directions that discriminate between utilitarian and deontological frameworks. We then introduce Dual Logit Calibration, a closed-form, minimum-$\ell_2$-norm update that moves the residual within this two-dimensional subspace so the resulting directional projections align with user-specified preference weights. Experiments on real-life moral dilemmas show that our method reliably achieves preference calibration and largely preserves general capabilities, outperforming recent baselines while providing an interpretable mechanism.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Convergent-Divergent Routing and Dual Logit Calibration to steer LLMs toward desired ethical frameworks (utilitarian/deontological) by editing branch points in transformer blocks, achieving fine-grained moral preference calibration while preserving general capabilities.
AI.motivation: Large language models exhibit inconsistent moral preferences across different contexts, making it difficult to align them with specific ethical frameworks without sacrificing general competence. The authors aim to achieve controllable, interpretable ethical steering.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: (1) Convergent-Divergent Routing identifies and edits minimal branch points in transformer blocks where ethical-framework pathways converge and diverge, gating non-target branches. (2) Dual Logit Calibration uses Common Spatial Patterns on the residual stream to extract discriminative directions for utilitarian vs. deontological frameworks, then applies a closed-form minimum-ℓ2-norm update to align residual projections with user-specified preference weights.
AI.result: The method reliably achieves preference calibration on real-life moral dilemmas, largely preserves general capabilities, and outperforms recent baselines with an interpretable mechanism.
AI.conclusion: Convergent-Divergent Routing combined with Dual Logit Calibration provides an effective, interpretable approach for fine-grained ethical framework steering in LLMs without degrading general performance.

title: GRPO-TTA: Test-Time Visual Tuning for Vision-Language Models via GRPO-Driven Reinforcement Learning
summary: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) has recently shown strong performance in post-training large language models and vision-language models. It raises a question of whether the GRPO also significantly promotes the test-time adaptation (TTA) of vision language models. In this paper, we propose Group Relative Policy Optimization for Test-Time Adaptation (GRPO-TTA), which adapts GRPO to the TTA setting by reformulating class-specific prompt prediction as a group-wise policy optimization problem. Specifically, we construct output groups by sampling top-K class candidates from CLIP similarity distributions, enabling probability-driven optimization without access to ground-truth labels. Moreover, we design reward functions tailored to test-time adaptation, including alignment rewards and dispersion rewards, to guide effective visual encoder tuning. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks demonstrate that GRPO-TTA consistently outperforms existing test-time adaptation methods, with notably larger performance gains under natural distribution shifts.

AI.tldr: GRPO-TTA adapts Group Relative Policy Optimization for test-time adaptation of vision-language models, using group-wise prompt optimization without labels, achieving superior performance across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether GRPO, which is effective in post-training LLMs/VLMs, can also significantly enhance test-time adaptation for vision-language models.
AI.method: Reformulates class-specific prompt prediction as group-wise policy optimization; constructs output groups via top-K class candidates from CLIP similarity; designs alignment and dispersion reward functions for label-free visual encoder tuning.
AI.result: GRPO-TTA consistently outperforms existing TTA methods, with larger gains under natural distribution shifts, as demonstrated across diverse benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: GRPO can be effectively repurposed for test-time adaptation in VLMs, offering a new direction for label-free, group-wise optimization at inference time.

title: ZeRO-Prefill: Zero Redundancy Overheads in MoE Prefill Serving
summary: Production LLM workloads increasingly serve discriminative tasks, such as classification, recommendation, and verification, whose answers are read from the logits of a single prefill pass with no autoregressive decoding. Serving these prefill-only workloads on mixture-of-experts (MoE) models is bottlenecked not by compute but by the distributed execution required to fit the model: existing parallel strategies (tensor, expert, and pipeline parallelism) trade memory pressure for redundant computation, communication, and synchronization, severely degrading MoE prefill serving efficiency. We observe that these overheads stem from coupling expert placement with synchronous activation routing -- a design inherited from the decoding era. The long, compute-bound forward passes of large-batch prefill open a per-layer window wide enough to stream expert weights in the background, replacing per-layer activation AllToAll with asynchronous weight AllGather fully overlapped with computation. We propose ZeRO-Prefill, a prefill-only serving system whose backend, AsyncEP (Asynchronous Expert Parallelism), gathers experts by weight rather than routing them by activation, and whose frontend co-enforces a physically-derived saturation threshold through prefix-aware routing and true-FLOPs load tracking. On Qwen3-235B-A22B across four hardware/precision configurations, ZeRO-Prefill delivers 1.35-1.37x throughput over the strongest distributed baseline on real-world workloads and up to 1.59x on long-context synthetic workloads, sustaining 29.8-36.2% per-GPU model FLOPs utilization.

AI.tldr: ZeRO-Prefill is a serving system for prefill-only MoE workloads that replaces synchronous activation routing with asynchronous weight gathering, achieving 1.35-1.59x throughput improvements by overlapping communication with computation.
AI.motivation: Existing parallel strategies (tensor, expert, pipeline parallelism) cause redundant computation, communication, and synchronization for MoE prefill-only workloads because they couple expert placement with synchronous activation routing—a design inherited from the decoding era.
AI.method: AsyncEP (Asynchronous Expert Parallelism) gathers experts by weight via asynchronous AllGather fully overlapped with computation, replacing per-layer activation AllToAll. The frontend co-enforces a saturation threshold via prefix-aware routing and true-FLOPs load tracking.
AI.result: On Qwen3-235B-A22B across four configurations: 1.35-1.37x throughput over strongest distributed baseline on real-world workloads, up to 1.59x on long-context synthetic workloads, sustaining 29.8-36.2% per-GPU model FLOPs utilization.
AI.conclusion: The long, compute-bound forward passes of large-batch prefill open a per-layer window wide enough to stream expert weights in the background, making asynchronous weight gathering more efficient than synchronous activation routing for MoE prefill serving.

title: Self-Improvement for Fast, High-Quality Plan Generation
summary: Generative models trained on synthetic plan data are a promising approach to generalized planning. Recent work has focused on finding any valid plan, rather than a high-quality solution. We address the challenge of producing high-quality plans, a computationally hard problem, in sub-exponential time. First, we demonstrate that, given optimal data, a decoder-only transformer can generate high-quality plans for unseen problem instances. Second, we show how to self-improve an initial model trained on sub-optimal data. Each round of self-improvement combines multiple model calls with graph search to generate improved plans, used for model fine-tuning. An experimental study on four domains: Blocksworld, Logistics, Labyrinth, and Sokoban, shows on average a 30% reduction in plan length over the source symbolic planner, with over 80% of plans being optimal, where the optimum is known. Plan quality is further improved by inference-time search. The model's latency scales sub-exponentially in contrast to the satisficing and optimal symbolic planners to which we compare. Together, these results suggest that self-improvement with generative models offers a scalable approach for high-quality plan generation.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that decoder-only transformers can generate high-quality plans using self-improvement, achieving ~30% shorter plans than symbolic planners with sub-exponential latency.
AI.motivation: Existing generative planning models focus on finding any valid plan rather than high-quality (optimal or near-optimal) solutions, and producing high-quality plans is computationally hard.
AI.method: Train a decoder-only transformer on optimal data, then use self-improvement via multiple model calls combined with graph search to iteratively generate better plans for fine-tuning.
AI.result: 30% average reduction in plan length over source symbolic planner across Blocksworld, Logistics, Labyrinth, and Sokoban; over 80% optimal plans where optimum is known; sub-exponential inference latency.
AI.conclusion: Self-improvement with generative models offers a scalable approach for high-quality plan generation.

title: TsallisPGD: Adaptive Gradient Weighting for Adversarial Attacks on Semantic Segmentation
summary: Attacking semantic segmentation models is significantly harder than image classification models because an attacker must flip thousands of pixel predictions simultaneously. Standard pixel-wise cross-entropy (CE) is ill-suited to this setting: it tends to overemphasize already-misclassified pixels, which slows optimization and overstates model robustness. To address these issues, we introduce TsallisPGD, an adversarial attack built on the Tsallis cross-entropy, a generalization of CE parameterized by $q$, which adaptively reshapes the gradient landscape by controlling gradient concentration across pixels. By varying $q$, we steer the attack toward pixels at different confidence levels. We first show that no single fixed-$q$ is universally optimal, as its effectiveness depends on the dataset, model architecture, and perturbation budget. Motivated by this, we propose a dynamic $q$-schedule that sweeps $q$ during optimization. Extensive experiments on Cityscapes, Pascal VOC, and ADE20K show that TsallisPGD, using a single validation-selected schedule, achieves the best average attack rank across all evaluated settings and improves over CEPGD, SegPGD, CosPGD, JSPGD, and MaskedPGD in reducing accuracy and mIoU on both standard and robust models.

AI.tldr: TsallisPGD introduces a dynamic q-schedule attack using Tsallis cross-entropy, outperforming existing adversarial attacks on semantic segmentation across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Standard CE loss overemphasizes already-misclassified pixels, slowing optimization and overstating robustness in segmentation attacks. A more adaptive loss is needed.
AI.method: TsallisPGD uses Tsallis cross-entropy with a dynamic q-schedule that adaptively controls gradient concentration across pixels, sweeping q during optimization to avoid fixed-q suboptimality.
AI.result: Achieves best average attack rank on Cityscapes, Pascal VOC, and ADE20K; improves over CEPGD, SegPGD, CosPGD, JSPGD, and MaskedPGD in accuracy and mIoU reduction on both standard and robust models.
AI.conclusion: TsallisPGD with a dynamic q-schedule is a more effective segmentation attack than fixed-loss alternatives, revealing greater robustness vulnerabilities.

title: Analytic Bridge Diffusions for Controlled Path Generation
summary:
Most modern bridge-diffusion methods achieve finite-time transport by specifying an interpolation, Schrödinger-bridge, or stochastic-control objective and then learning the associated score or drift field with a neural network. In contrast, we identify a restricted but sufficiently broad and analytically solvable class in which the score, intermediate marginals, and protocol gradients are available in closed form without inner stochastic simulation loops and without neural networks in the optimization loop. We recast the classical linear--quadratic--Gaussian (LQG) stochastic-control structure as a transport problem of the Path Integral Diffusion (PID) type. In classical LQG control, linear dynamics, Gaussian noise, and quadratic costs lead to Riccati equations and closed-form optimal feedback. In LQ-GM-PID, we retain the linear--quadratic stochastic-control backbone, but replace terminal state regulation by a prescribed terminal probability density and allow both the initial and terminal laws to be Gaussian Mixtures (GM).
  Moreover, LQ-GM-PID turns bridge diffusion from a tool for terminal target matching alone into a tool for path shaping. We demonstrate this on a 2D corridor task, a 2D multi-entrance transport task, and a high-dimensional scaling study with $d=32$ and $M=16$ Gaussian-mixture terminal modes, all with sub-50\,ms analytic precompute on a laptop. We position LQ-GM-PID as an analytically solvable reference model for the state-of-the-art neural bridge-diffusion and generative-transport methods: a controlled setting in which neural approximations, score estimates, path-shaping objectives, and protocol-learning procedures can be tested against exact quantities.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LQ-GM-PID, an analytically solvable class of bridge-diffusion methods based on linear-quadratic-Gaussian control with Gaussian mixture terminal densities, achieving closed-form solutions without neural networks or simulation loops, demonstrated on 2D and high-dimensional tasks.
AI.motivation: Most modern bridge-diffusion methods rely on neural networks and stochastic simulation loops, lacking closed-form analytical solutions. The authors identify a restricted but useful class where exact solutions exist, enabling fast computation and serving as a benchmark for neural methods.
AI.method: The paper recasts classical LQG stochastic control as a Path Integral Diffusion (PID) transport problem (LQ-GM-PID). It retains linear dynamics, Gaussian noise, and quadratic costs but replaces terminal state regulation with a prescribed terminal Gaussian mixture density, allowing closed-form score, marginals, and protocol gradients via Riccati equations.
AI.result: Demonstrated on a 2D corridor task, a 2D multi-entrance transport task, and a high-dimensional study (d=32, M=16 Gaussian mixture modes), all achieving sub-50ms analytic precomputation on a laptop without neural networks.
AI.conclusion: Positioned LQ-GM-PID as an analytically solvable reference model for testing neural bridge-diffusion and generative-transport methods, providing exact quantities against which approximations can be validated.

title: AdapShot: Adaptive Many-Shot In-Context Learning with Semantic-Aware KV Cache Reuse
summary: Many-Shot In-Context Learning (ICL) has emerged as a promising paradigm, leveraging extensive examples to unlock the reasoning potential of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, existing methods typically rely on a predetermined, fixed number of shots. This static approach often fails to adapt to the varying difficulty of different queries, leading to either insufficient context or interference from noise. Furthermore, the prohibitive computational and memory costs of long contexts severely limit Many-Shot's feasibility. To address the above limitations, we propose AdapShot, which dynamically optimizes shot counts and leverages KV cache reuse for efficient inference. Specifically, we design a probe-based evaluation mechanism that utilizes output entropy to determine the optimal number of shots. To bypass the redundant prefilling computation during both the probing and inference phases, we incorporate a semantics-aware KV cache reuse strategy. Within this reuse strategy, to address positional encoding incompatibilities, we introduce a decoupling and re-encoding method that enables the flexible reordering of cached key-value pairs. Extensive experiments demonstrate that AdapShot achieves an average performance gain of around 10% and a 4.64x speedup compared to state-of-the-art DBSA.

AI.tldr: AdapShot dynamically optimizes shot counts for in-context learning and reuses KV cache to achieve ~10% performance gain and 4.64x speedup over prior methods.
