AI.conclusion: The Substrate's margin-band majority vote is robust to retrieval imperfections in ways that pure top-k recall metrics don't predict. Code/data are available at the provided GitHub repo.

title: Unified Multimodal Visual Tracking with Dual Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Multimodal visual object tracking can be divided into to several kinds of tasks (e.g. RGB and RGB+X tracking), based on the input modality. Existing methods often train separate models for each modality or rely on pretrained models to adapt to new modalities, which limits efficiency, scalability, and usability. Thus, we introduce OneTrackerV2, a unified multi-modal tracking framework that enables end-to-end training for any modality. We propose Meta Merger to embed multi-modal information into a unified space, allowing flexible modality fusion and robustness. We further introduce Dual Mixture-of-Experts (DMoE): T-MoE models spatio-temporal relations for tracking, while M-MoE embeds multi-modal knowledge, disentangling cross-modal dependencies and reducing feature conflicts. With a shared architecture, unified parameters, and a single end-to-end training, OneTrackerV2 achieves state-of-the-art performance across five RGB and RGB+X tracking tasks and 12 benchmarks, while maintaining high inference efficiency. Notably, even after model compression, OneTrackerV2 retains strong performance. Moreover, OneTrackerV2 demonstrates remarkable robustness under modality-missing scenarios.

AI.tldr: OneTrackerV2 is a unified multi-modal tracking framework that supports any modality via end-to-end training, using Meta Merger for modality fusion and Dual Mixture-of-Experts (T-MoE + M-MoE) for spatio-temporal and cross-modal modeling, achieving SOTA across 5 tasks and 12 benchmarks with high efficiency and robustness.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-modal tracking methods train separate models for each modality or rely on pretrained models to adapt, limiting efficiency, scalability, and usability. A unified framework is needed.
AI.method: Proposes OneTrackerV2 with Meta Merger (unified multi-modal embedding) and Dual MoE (T-MoE for spatio-temporal relations, M-MoE for multi-modal knowledge disentanglement). Uses shared architecture, unified parameters, single end-to-end training.
AI.result: Achieves SOTA across 5 RGB/RGB+X tracking tasks and 12 benchmarks, with high inference efficiency, strong performance after compression, and robustness under modality-missing scenarios.
AI.conclusion: A unified one-for-all tracker is feasible, outperforming modality-specific models while being efficient and robust to missing modalities.

title: Pairwise matrices for sparse autoencoders: single-feature inspection mislabels causal axes
summary: The standard sparse-autoencoder (SAE) interpretability protocol labels each feature from its top-activating contexts and validates by single-feature steering. We propose the pairwise matrix protocol, co-varying steering coefficient with joint condition, and report three findings the standard one-corner protocol misses on Qwen3-1.7B-Instruct, replicated on Gemma-2-2B-it. First, a feature labelled "AI self-disclaimer" from its top contexts produces an inverted U-shape under a coefficient sweep: at c=+500 the model substitutes a fluent contemplative-philosopher voice for the disclaimer. Two further features anchor the criterion (one monotonic, one pure breakdown). Second, three near-orthogonal cluster-specific features that individually steer a philosophy-of-mind register, jointly suppressed at c=-500, damage grounded composition on recipes and engine explanations as well as introspective prompts; single-feature suppression at the same magnitude leaves controls intact. Third, a matched-geometry comparison of single-feature, joint, and random-direction perturbations (norm ~1.55, cosine ~0.64) yields three distinct output regimes: single-feature substitutes strategy filler, random direction substitutes diverse content, joint suppression alone produces placeholder text. Coherence loss is direction-pattern-dependent, not magnitude-dependent. All three findings reproduce on Gemma with model-specific damage signatures; the matched-geometry control is CI-separated by ~10x. The pipeline also locates a top causally responsible feature in Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct.

AI.tldr: Standard SAE interpretability (top-context labeling + single-feature steering) misses three key phenomena that the proposed pairwise matrix protocol reveals: (1) inverted U-shaped steering effects, (2) joint suppression damaging grounded composition while single suppression leaves controls intact, (3) coherence loss being direction-pattern-dependent rather than magnitude-dependent.
AI.motivation: The standard sparse-autoencoder (SAE) interpretability protocol (top-activating context labeling + single-feature steering) may be insufficient, missing complex co-varying dynamics between features.
AI.method: Proposes the pairwise matrix protocol which co-varies steering coefficient with joint condition. Applies to Qwen3-1.7B-Instruct and replicates on Gemma-2-2B-it, with matched-geometry controls comparing single-feature, joint, and random-direction perturbations.
AI.result: Three findings: (1) inverted U-shape steering for "AI self-disclaimer" feature; (2) joint suppression of three near-orthogonal features damages grounded composition across multiple prompt types; (3) coherence loss depends on direction pattern, not magnitude. Results replicate on Gemma with model-specific signatures.
AI.conclusion: The pairwise matrix protocol reveals interpretability phenomena invisible to the standard single-feature protocol, demonstrating the necessity of multi-feature co-variation analysis for understanding model internals.

title: FluxFlow: Conservative Flow-Matching for Astronomical Image Super-Resolution
summary: Ground-to-space astronomical super-resolution requires recovering space-quality images from ground-based observations that are simultaneously limited by pixel sampling resolution and atmospheric seeing, which imposes a stochastic, spatially varying PSF that cannot be resolved through upsampling alone. Existing methods rely on synthetic training pairs that fail to capture real atmospheric statistics and are prone to either over-smoothed reconstructions or hallucination sources with no physical counterpart in the observed sky. We propose FluxFlow, a conservative pixel-space flow-matching framework that incorporates observation uncertainty and source-region importance weights during training, and a training-free Wiener-regularized test-time correction to suppress hallucination sources while preserving recovered detail. We further construct the DESI--HST Dataset, the large-scale real-world benchmark comprising 19,500 real co-registered ground-to-space image pairs with real atmospheric PSF variation. Experiments demonstrate that FluxFlow consistently outperforms existing baseline methods in both photometric and scientific accuracy.

AI.tldr: FluxFlow is a flow-matching framework for ground-to-space astronomical super-resolution that uses uncertainty-aware training and test-time correction to recover high-quality images, outperforming baselines on a new real-world benchmark of 19,500 ground-to-space pairs.
AI.motivation: Existing methods use synthetic training pairs that fail to capture real atmospheric statistics, leading to over-smoothed reconstructions or hallucinated sources with no physical counterpart.
AI.method: Proposes FluxFlow: (1) pixel-space flow-matching with observation uncertainty and source-region importance weights during training, (2) training-free Wiener-regularized test-time correction to suppress hallucinations, and (3) constructs DESI-HST Dataset with 19,500 real co-registered ground-to-space image pairs.
AI.result: FluxFlow consistently outperforms existing baseline methods in both photometric and scientific accuracy on the real-world benchmark.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively addresses the practical challenges of astronomical super-resolution by leveraging real data and conservative regularization to balance detail recovery with hallucination suppression.

title: Global and Local Topology-Aware Attention with Persistent Homology and Euler Biases for Time-Series Forecasting
summary:
Scientific time series often encode predictive geometric structure, including connectivity, cycles, shell-like geometry, directional changes, and nonlinear neighborhoods, that standard dot-product attention does not explicitly represent. We introduce a topology-aware attention framework that adds such structure to attention logits using persistent homology (H0-H2), anchored Euler characteristic transforms, and kernel-Hilbert channels. A validation-gated local residual captures local topological signals, including a Zeng-style local H0 component, only when held-out validation data support the correction. Exact Vietoris-Rips computations and smooth topological surrogates are evaluated under a no-leakage protocol with train-only calibration, validation-only selection, and test-only reporting.
  We evaluate guarded topology-aware variants across three architecture families: lightweight attention/Ridge, PatchTSTForRegression, and TimeSeriesTransformerForPrediction. Experiments include synthetic benchmarks isolating higher-order topology and real datasets covering CO2, S&P 500 return-window geometry, and NASA IMS bearing degradation. The audit uses matched paired comparisons across seven dataset units, three random seeds, and three chronological splits, giving 63 paired units per architecture and 189 paired units overall. Topology-aware models show positive paired effects when geometry is predictive, with heterogeneous magnitude across datasets and architectures. Lightweight attention/Ridge improves in 46 of 63 units, with mean relative RMSE reduction of 12.5% and paired randomization p=7.2e-4; PatchTST improves in 33 units and retains the baseline in 20 units, with 23.5% reduction and p=3.5e-5; and TimeSeriesTransformer improves in 47 units, with 47.8% reduction and p<1e-4. The results support topology as a validation-selected, architecture-compatible inductive bias.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a topology-aware attention framework that enriches standard dot-product attention with geometric structure (connectivity, cycles, shells, directional changes) using persistent homology and related topological tools. Evaluated across three architectures on synthetic and real datasets (CO2, S&P 500, NASA IMS bearing), it shows significant RMSE reductions (12.5%-47.8%) when geometry is predictive.
AI.motivation: Scientific time series contain predictive geometric structures (connectivity, cycles, shell-like geometry, directional changes, nonlinear neighborhoods) that standard dot-product attention cannot explicitly capture.
AI.method: Adds topology to attention logits via persistent homology (H0-H2), anchored Euler characteristic transforms, and kernel-Hilbert channels. A validation-gated local residual captures local topological signals only when supported by held-out validation data. Uses exact Vietoris-Rips computations and smooth topological surrogates under a no-leakage protocol.
AI.result: Lightweight attention/Ridge: 46/63 units improved, 12.5% mean RMSE reduction (p=7.2e-4). PatchTST: 33/63 improved, 20 retained, 23.5% reduction (p=3.5e-5). TimeSeriesTransformer: 47/63 improved, 47.8% reduction (p<1e-4). Total 189 paired units across 3 architectures, 7 datasets, 3 seeds, 3 splits.
AI.conclusion: Topology serves as a validation-selected, architecture-compatible inductive bias that improves forecasting when geometric structure is predictive, with heterogeneous magnitude across datasets and architectures.

title: Before Forgetting, Learn to Remember: Revisiting Foundational Learning Failures in LVLM Unlearning Benchmarks
summary: While Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) offer powerful capabilities, they pose privacy risks by unintentionally memorizing sensitive personal information. Current unlearning benchmarks attempt to mitigate this using fictitious identities but overlook a critical stage 1 failure: models fail to effectively memorize target information initially, rendering subsequent unlearning evaluations unreliable. Diagnosing under-memorization and the multi-hop curse as root causes, we introduce ReMem, a Reliable Multi-hop and Multi-image Memorization Benchmark. ReMem ensures robust foundational learning through principled data scaling, reasoning-aware QA pairs, and diverse visual contexts. Additionally, we propose a novel Exposure metric to quantify the depth of information erasure from the model's internal probability distribution. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ReMem provides a rigorous and trustworthy framework for diagnosing both learning and unlearning behaviors in LVLMs.

AI.tldr: ReMem is a benchmark for reliably evaluating memorization and unlearning in LVLMs, addressing the issue that models often fail to memorize target information initially, making unlearning evaluations unreliable.
AI.motivation: Current unlearning benchmarks for LVLMs use fictitious identities but fail to ensure models actually memorize the target information first (stage 1 failure), leading to unreliable evaluations. The root causes are under-memorization and the multi-hop curse.
AI.method: ReMem ensures robust foundational learning through principled data scaling, reasoning-aware QA pairs, and diverse visual contexts. A novel Exposure metric is proposed to quantify the depth of information erasure from the model's internal probability distribution.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that ReMem provides a rigorous and trustworthy framework for diagnosing both learning and unlearning behaviors in LVLMs.
AI.conclusion: ReMem addresses critical flaws in existing unlearning benchmarks by ensuring reliable memorization first, making subsequent unlearning evaluations trustworthy.

title: GeoTopoDiff: Learning Geometry--Topology Graph Priors through Boundary-Constrained Mixed Diffusion for Sparse-Slice 3D Porous Reconstruction
summary: Diffusion-based voxel prior modelling is challenging for the reconstruction of large-scale 3D porous microstructures. Due to the demanding requirements for simultaneously modelling both the continuous pore morphology and the discrete pore-throat topology, the diffusion models require fully observed CT scans to provide topology-faithful priors, which results in an inherent trade-off among throughput, topological fidelity, and field of view in practical industrial applications. We propose GeoTopoDiff, a graph diffusion-based framework for reconstructing 3D porous microstructures from sparse CT slices. GeoTopoDiff transfers the learning of diffusion priors from a voxel-based space to a mixed graph state space, which simultaneously encompasses continuous pore geometry and discrete pore-throat topology. A topology-aware partial graph prior from sparsely observed CT slices is introduced to constrain the reverse denoising process. Experiments on anisotropic PTFE and Fontainebleau sandstone show that GeoTopoDiff reduces morphology-related errors by 19.8% and topology-sensitive transport errors by 36.5% on average. Our findings suggest that the mixed graph state space promotes the diffusion denoising process to reduce posterior uncertainty under a sparse observations. All models and code have been made publicly available to facilitate the exploration of diffusion models in the field of 3D porous microstructures simulation.

AI.tldr: GeoTopoDiff is a graph diffusion framework that reconstructs 3D porous microstructures from sparse CT slices by modeling both continuous pore geometry and discrete pore-throat topology in a mixed graph state space, reducing morphology errors by 19.8% and transport errors by 36.5%.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion-based voxel prior models struggle with large-scale 3D porous microstructures because they require fully observed CT scans to maintain topological fidelity, creating a trade-off between throughput, topological accuracy, and field of view in industrial applications.
AI.method: GeoTopoDiff transfers diffusion priors from voxel space to a mixed graph state space that jointly models continuous pore geometry and discrete pore-throat topology, and uses a topology-aware partial graph prior from sparse CT slices to constrain the reverse denoising process.
AI.result: On anisotropic PTFE and Fontainebleau sandstone, GeoTopoDiff reduces morphology-related errors by 19.8% and topology-sensitive transport errors by 36.5% on average.
AI.conclusion: The mixed graph state space effectively reduces posterior uncertainty under sparse observations, enabling faithful 3D microstructure reconstruction with limited CT data.

title: Moral Sensitivity in LLMs: A Tiered Evaluation of Contextual Bias via Behavioral Profiling and Mechanistic Interpretability
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in settings that require nuanced ethical reasoning, yet existing bias evaluations treat model outputs as simply "biased" or "unbiased." This binary framing misses the gradual, context-sensitive way bias actually emerges. We address this gap in two stages: behavioral profiling and mechanistic validation. In the behavioral stage, we introduce the Moral Sensitivity Index (MSI), a metric that quantifies the probability of biased output across a graduated, seven-tier stress test ranging from abstract numerical problems to scenarios rooted in historical and socioeconomic injustice. Evaluating four leading models (Claude 3.5, Qwen 3.5, Llama 3, and Gemini 1.5), we identify distinct behavioral signatures shaped by alignment design: for instance, Gemini 1.5 reaches 72.7% MSI by Tier 5 under socioeconomic framing, while Claude exhibits sharp suppression consistent with identity-based safety training. We then verify these behavioral patterns mechanistically. We select criminal-bias scenarios, which produced the highest MSI scores across models, as probes and apply logit lens, attention analysis, activation patching, and semantic probing to a controlled set of six models spanning three capability tiers: small language models (SLMs), instruction-tuned base models, and reasoning-distilled variants. Circuit-level analysis reveals a U-curve of bias: SLMs exhibit strong criminal bias; scaling to instruction-tuned models eliminates it; reasoning distillation reintroduces bias to SLM-like levels despite identical parameter counts, suggesting distillation compresses reasoning traces in ways that reactivate shallow statistical associations. Critically, the socially loaded cues that drive high MSI scores activate the same bias-driving circuits identified mechanistically, providing cross-stage validation.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Moral Sensitivity Index (MSI) to measure LLM bias on a graduated scale, revealing distinct bias patterns across models and validating them mechanistically. Key finding: reasoning distillation reintroduces criminal bias to levels matching small models, despite identical parameter counts.
AI.motivation: Existing bias evaluations treat outputs as binary ("biased" or "unbiased"), but real-world bias emerges gradually and context-dependently. This paper aims to address this gap by providing a more nuanced, graduated measurement of bias in LLMs.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: (1) Behavioral profiling using the Moral Sensitivity Index (MSI), a seven-tier stress test; (2) Mechanistic validation via logit lens, attention analysis, activation patching, and semantic probing on six models across three capability tiers (SLMs, instruction-tuned, reasoning-distilled).
AI.result: Gemini 1.5 reaches 72.7% MSI by Tier 5 under socioeconomic framing; Claude shows sharp suppression. Mechanistically, a U-curve of bias emerges: SLMs show strong criminal bias, instruction-tuning eliminates it, but reasoning distillation reintroduces bias to SLM-like levels.
AI.conclusion: Socially loaded cues driving high MSI scores activate the same bias-driving circuits identified mechanistically, providing cross-stage validation. Reasoning distillation compresses reasoning traces, reactivating shallow statistical associations that lead to bias.

title: ReLeaf: Benchmarking Leaf Segmentation across Domains and Species
comment: Accepted at Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR) Workshops 2026. Code and dataset available at https://github.com/cropandweed/releaf
summary: Rising global food demand and growing climate pressure increase the need for sustainable, precise agricultural practices. Automated, individualized plant treatment relies on fine-grained visual analysis, yet leaf-level segmentation remains underexplored despite its value for assessing crop health, growth dynamics, yield potential and localized stress symptoms. Progress is limited by a lack of dedicated datasets, especially regarding species coverage, and by the absence of systematic evaluations of modern instance-segmentation architectures for this task. We address these gaps by surveying current data and identifying four suitable, publicly available leaf-segmentation datasets. Using them, we compare one-stage, two-stage and Transformer-based detectors and identify a YOLO26 model configuration to provide the best trade-off for real-world precision-agriculture tasks. Extensive cross-domain generalization experiments reveal substantial performance drops across plant species and recording setups, especially for models trained solely on laboratory data. To strengthen data availability, we introduce a new benchmark dataset with leaf-level masks for 23 plant species, created via semi-automatic annotation of selected CropAndWeed images. A model trained on all four existing datasets achieves a mean mAP50-95 of 83.9% across their corresponding test sets and 40.2% on our new benchmark, demonstrating improved generalization and highlighting the need for diverse leaf-segmentation datasets in robust precision agriculture.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the underexplored area of leaf-level segmentation for precision agriculture by surveying existing datasets, comparing instance-segmentation architectures (one-stage, two-stage, Transformer-based), finding YOLO-based models offer the best trade-off, and introducing a new benchmark dataset of 23 plant species. Models trained on multiple datasets achieve 83.9% mAP on existing test sets but drop to 40.2% on the new benchmark, highlighting the need for diverse data.
AI.motivation: Leaf-level segmentation is valuable for assessing crop health, growth, yield, and stress, but progress is limited by lack of dedicated datasets, insufficient species coverage, and absence of systematic evaluations of modern instance-segmentation models for this task.
AI.method: Surveyed existing leaf-segmentation datasets, identified four suitable public datasets, compared one-stage (YOLO-based), two-stage (Mask R-CNN), and Transformer-based detectors, and introduced a new benchmark dataset with leaf-level masks for 23 plant species via semi-automatic annotation of CropAndWeed images.
AI.result: YOLO-based models provide the best trade-off for real-world precision agriculture. Cross-domain generalization shows substantial performance drops across species and setups. Model trained on all four datasets achieves 83.9% mAP50-95 on test sets and 40.2% on the new benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Diverse leaf-segmentation datasets are crucial for robust precision agriculture; existing models still struggle with generalization across species and recording conditions, highlighting the need for broader data coverage and further research.

title: Beyond Activation Alignment: The Geometry of Neural Sensitivity
summary: Activation-alignment measures such as Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA), Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA), and Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) are widely used to compare biological and artificial neural representations. Recent theoretical work interprets many of these methods as assessing agreement between optimal linear readouts over broad families of global tasks. However, agreement at the level of global readouts does not determine how a system uses local stimulus evidence. Specifically, representations may align in activation space yet differ in their sensitivity to small perturbations. To address this challenge, we introduce a complementary framework based on local decodable information, which focuses on a representation's ability, under noise, to discriminate small perturbations within a specified stimulus-coordinate subspace. Building on Fisher information and local representation geometry, we summarize each representation using the expected projected pullback/Fisher metric over that subspace. This formulation induces a second-moment family of local discrimination tasks, for which the resulting operator provides a minimal, complete dataset-level summary of expected discriminability. We compare these regularized signatures using a log-spectral distance on the manifold of symmetric positive definite (SPD) matrices, yielding the Spectral Riemannian Alignment Score (S-RAS) and a uniform multiplicative certificate over the corresponding family of lifted task values. Empirically, this framework enables the recovery of corresponding layers across independently trained artificial neural networks, supports transferable class-conditional probes, reveals controlled dissociations between standard and robust training, and uncovers stimulus-coordinate family effects across mouse visual cortex using the Allen Brain Observatory static gratings dataset.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces S-RAS (Spectral Riemannian Alignment Score), a novel framework for comparing neural representations based on local decodable information and Fisher information geometry, moving beyond global activation-alignment measures like RSA, CCA, and CKA to capture sensitivity to local stimulus perturbations.
AI.motivation: Existing activation-alignment measures (RSA, CCA, CKA) only assess global readout agreement but fail to capture how representations differ in their local sensitivity to small perturbations, limiting our understanding of fine-grained representational differences.
AI.method: The framework builds on Fisher information and local representation geometry, summarizing each representation using the expected projected pullback/Fisher metric over a stimulus-coordinate subspace. This yields a second-moment family of local discrimination tasks, compared via a log-spectral distance on the manifold of SPD matrices (S-RAS).
AI.result: The framework recovers corresponding layers across independently trained ANNs, supports transferable class-conditional probes, reveals dissociations between standard and robust training, and uncovers stimulus-coordinate family effects across mouse visual cortex (Allen Brain Observatory).
AI.conclusion: S-RAS provides a complementary, geometrically principled tool for comparing neural representations at a local, perturbation-sensitive level, enabling insights that global alignment measures miss.

title: A Robust Unsupervised Domain Adaptation Framework for Medical Image Classification Using RKHS-MMD
summary: Labeling medical images is a major bottleneck in the field of medical imaging, as it requires domain-specific expertise, and it gets further complicated due to variability across different medical centers and different imaging devices. Such heterogeneity introduces domain shifts and modality discrepancies, which limits the generalization of trained models. To address this important challenge, we propose an unsupervised domain adaptation framework that combines transfer learning with a Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space based Maximum Mean Discrepancy loss for the alignment of source and target domains. By jointly optimizing classification and RKHS-MMD losses, the methodology enhances generalization to unannotated medical datasets while diminishing reliance on manual annotation. Experimental evaluations presented on two chest X-ray datasets, which are obtained from different medical centers, show outstanding improvements over models trained without adaptation. Furthermore, we perform a comparative study to see that RKHS-MMD performs better than the standard Maximum Mean Discrepancy in reducing modality gap, emphasizing its effectiveness for medical image classification and also its strong capability in advanced AI-driven medical diagnostics.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an unsupervised domain adaptation framework using transfer learning with RKHS-based Maximum Mean Discrepancy loss to align source and target domains for medical image classification, reducing the need for manual labeling.
AI.motivation: Labeling medical images is a major bottleneck due to required domain expertise, and data heterogeneity across medical centers and devices causes domain shifts and modality discrepancies, limiting model generalization.
AI.method: An unsupervised domain adaptation framework combining transfer learning with RKHS-MMD loss for aligning source and target domains, jointly optimizing classification and RKHS-MMD losses.
AI.result: Experiments on two chest X-ray datasets from different medical centers show significant improvements over models without adaptation, and RKHS-MMD outperforms standard MMD in reducing modality gap.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach enhances generalization to unannotated medical datasets, reduces reliance on manual annotation, and is effective for AI-driven medical diagnostics.

title: Self-Mined Hardness for Safety Fine-Tuning
summary: Safety fine-tuning of language models typically requires a curated adversarial dataset. We take a different approach: score each candidate prompt's difficulty by how often the target model's own rollouts are judged harmful, then fine-tune on the hardest prompts paired with the model's own non-jailbroken rollouts. On Llama-3-8B-Instruct and Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct, this approach cuts the WildJailbreak attack success rate from 11.5% and 20.1% down to 1-3%, but pushes refusal on jailbreak-shaped benign prompts from 14-22% to 74-94%. Interleaving the same hard prompts 1:1 with adversarially-framed benign prompts (prompts that look like jailbreaks but have benign intent) cuts that refusal back down to 30-51% on 8B and 52-72% on 3B, at a cost of 2-6 percentage points of attack success rate. Within the mixed regime, training on the hardest half of the eligible pool rather than a random half cuts the remaining ASR by 35-50% (about 3 percentage points) on both models.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes fine-tuning language models on their own hardest-to-defend prompts (measured by rollout harmfulness) rather than curated adversarial datasets. This dramatically reduces jailbreak success (to 1-3%) but causes severe over-refusal (74-94% on benign jailbreak-shaped prompts). Mixing hard prompts 1:1 with adversarially-framed benign prompts reduces refusal to 30-72% with only a small ASR increase, and selecting the hardest half of prompts further reduces ASR by 35-50%.
AI.motivation: To improve safety fine-tuning without relying on manually curated adversarial datasets, by using the model's own judgment of prompt difficulty.
AI.method: Score prompt difficulty by how often the target model's own rollouts are judged harmful; fine-tune on hardest prompts paired with non-jailbroken rollouts; then interleave hard prompts with adversarially-framed benign prompts to mitigate over-refusal.
AI.result: On Llama-3-8B-Instruct and Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct: WildJailbreak ASR drops from 11.5%/20.1% to 1-3%; refusal on jailbreak-shaped benign prompts rises from 14-22% to 74-94%; mixing brings refusal down to 30-72% with 2-6 percentage point ASR increase; hardest-half training cuts remaining ASR by 35-50%.
AI.conclusion: Self-supervised hard prompt selection is effective for safety but causes over-refusal; mixing with benign adversarial-framed prompts and selecting the hardest half of the pool provides a practical balance between safety and usability.

title: Enhancing Visual Question Answering with Multimodal LLMs via Chain-of-Question Guided Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary: With advances in multimodal research and deep learning, Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have emerged as a powerful paradigm for a wide range of multimodal tasks. As a core problem in vision-language research, Visual Question Answering (VQA) has increasingly employed MLLMs to improve performance, particularly in open-domain settings where external knowledge is essential. In this work, we aim to further enhance retrieval-based VQA by more effectively integrating MLLMs with structured reasoning and knowledge acquisition. We introduce a logical prompting strategy that fuses Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning with Visual Question Decomposition (VQD), termed CoVQD, to guide retrieval toward more accurate and relevant knowledge for MLLM inference. Building on this idea, we propose a new framework, CoVQD-guided RAG (CgRAG), which enables MLLMs to access more comprehensive and coherent external knowledge while benefiting from structured visual-text reasoning guidance, thereby improving generalization and reliability in complex cross-domain VQA scenarios. Extensive experiments on E-VQA, InfoSeek, and OKVQA benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes CoVQD, a logical prompting strategy combining Chain-of-Thought reasoning with Visual Question Decomposition, and CgRAG, a retrieval-augmented generation framework that integrates this strategy to enhance MLLM-based VQA by guiding retrieval toward more accurate external knowledge.
AI.motivation: To improve retrieval-based Visual Question Answering (VQA) by more effectively integrating Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) with structured reasoning and knowledge acquisition, particularly in open-domain settings where external knowledge is needed.
AI.method: Proposes CoVQD (logical prompting fusing Chain-of-Thought reasoning with Visual Question Decomposition) and CgRAG (CoVQD-guided Retrieval-Augmented Generation framework) that guides retrieval toward accurate, relevant knowledge and enables structured visual-text reasoning for MLLMs.
AI.result: Experiments on E-VQA, InfoSeek, and OKVQA benchmarks demonstrate effectiveness of the proposed method in improving generalization and reliability in complex cross-domain VQA scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The CoVQD and CgRAG framework effectively enhances MLLMs' performance on retrieval-based VQA by combining structured reasoning with knowledge acquisition, leading to more accurate and reliable answers.

title: Text-Conditional JEPA for Learning Semantically Rich Visual Representations
summary: Image-based Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (I-JEPA) offers a promising approach to visual self-supervised learning through masked feature prediction. However with the inherent visual uncertainty at masked positions, feature prediction remains challenging and may fail to learn semantic representations. In this work, we propose Text-Conditional JEPA (TC-JEPA) that uses image captions to reduce the prediction uncertainty. Specifically, we modulate the predicted patch features using a fine-grained text conditioner that computes sparse cross-attention over input text tokens. With such conditioning, patch features become predictable as a function of text, thus are more semantically meaningful. We show TC-JEPA improves downstream performance and training stability, with promising scaling properties. TC-JEPA also offers a new vision-language pretraining paradigm based on feature prediction only, outperforming contrastive methods on diverse tasks, especially those requiring fine-grained visual understanding and reasoning.

AI.tldr: TC-JEPA improves visual self-supervised learning by using image captions as text conditioning to reduce prediction uncertainty in masked feature prediction, leading to more semantic representations and better downstream performance.
AI.motivation: I-JEPA's masked feature prediction suffers from inherent visual uncertainty at masked positions, making it challenging to learn semantic representations.
AI.method: TC-JEPA modulates predicted patch features using a fine-grained text conditioner with sparse cross-attention over input text tokens (image captions), making patch features predictable as a function of text.
AI.result: TC-JEPA improves downstream performance, training stability, and scaling properties. It outperforms contrastive methods on diverse tasks, especially those requiring fine-grained visual understanding and reasoning.
AI.conclusion: TC-JEPA offers a new vision-language pretraining paradigm based on feature prediction only, effectively reducing prediction uncertainty through text conditioning.

title: Multimodal Learning on Low-Quality Data with Conformal Predictive Self-Calibration
summary: Multimodal learning often grapples with the challenge of low-quality data, which predominantly manifests as two facets: modality imbalance and noisy corruption. While these issues are often studied in isolation, we argue that they share a common root in the predictive uncertainty towards the reliability of individual modalities and instances during learning. In this paper, we propose a unified framework, termed Conformal Predictive Self-Calibration (CPSC), which leverages conformal prediction to equip the model with the ability to perform self-guided calibration on-the-fly. The core of our proposed CPSC lies in a novel self-calibrating training loop that seamlessly integrates two key modules: (1) Representation Self-Calibration, which decomposes unimodal features into components, and selectively fuses the most robust ones identified by a conformal predictor to enhance feature resilience. (2) Gradient Self-Calibration, which recalibrates the gradient flow during backpropagation based on instance-wise reliability scores, steering the optimization towards more trustworthy directions. Furthermore, we also devise a self-update strategy for the conformal predictor to ensure the entire system co-evolves consistently throughout the training process. Extensive experiments on six benchmark datasets under both imbalanced and noisy settings demonstrate that our CPSC framework consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods. Our code is available at https://github.com/XunCHN/CPSC.

AI.tldr: CPSC is a unified framework using conformal prediction to address both modality imbalance and noisy corruption in multimodal learning through self-calibrating representation fusion and gradient flow.
AI.motivation: Multimodal learning suffers from low-quality data (modality imbalance and noisy corruption), which are often treated separately but share a common root in predictive uncertainty about reliability of modalities and instances.
AI.method: Two modules: (1) Representation Self-Calibration - decomposes unimodal features and selectively fuses robust components via conformal predictor; (2) Gradient Self-Calibration - recalibrates gradients based on instance-wise reliability scores. Includes a self-update strategy for the conformal predictor to co-evolve during training.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods on six benchmark datasets under both imbalanced and noisy settings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CPSC framework effectively unifies handling of modality imbalance and noisy corruption via conformal prediction-based self-calibration, achieving superior results across diverse multimodal benchmarks.

title: Posterior-First Neural PDE Simulation: Inferring Hidden Problem State from a Single Field
summary: Neural PDE simulators often receive only a single observed field at deployment. In this setting, a field-to-future predictor can collapse distinct latent problem states into the same deterministic interface, losing the ambiguity needed for reliable rollout and downstream decisions. We propose posterior-first neural PDE simulation: first infer a posterior over the minimal task-sufficient problem state, then condition prediction on that posterior. The resulting theory connects the object, the learning target, and the failure mode: Bayes downstream values factor through this posterior, refinement labels make it learnable by proper scoring rules, and deterministic collapse incurs an ambiguity barrier whenever the true posterior is non-Dirac. Synthetic exact-ambiguity experiments show that point-versus-posterior gaps track the predicted barrier. On metadata-hidden PDEBench tasks, posterior recovery reduces pooled rollout nRMSE from 0.175 to 0.132, closing 59.4% of the direct-to-oracle gap. These results suggest that single-observation neural PDE simulation should be posterior-first rather than monolithic field-to-future prediction.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a "posterior-first" framework for neural PDE simulation: instead of directly predicting future fields from a single observed field, the system first infers a posterior over the task-relevant latent problem state, then conditions predictions on that posterior. Theoretic analysis connects the posterior to Bayes-optimal downstream decisions, identifies an "ambiguity barrier" limiting deterministic approaches, and experiments on PDEBench show significant improvement (nRMSE from 0.175 to 0.132, closing 59.4% of the gap to oracle).
AI.motivation: Current neural PDE simulators that map a single observed field directly to future predictions can collapse distinct latent problem states (e.g., different physical parameters) into the same deterministic interface, losing the ambiguity needed for reliable rollout and downstream decision-making.
AI.method: Posterior-first neural PDE simulation: first infer a posterior distribution over the minimal task-sufficient latent problem state from the single observation, then condition the future prediction on that posterior. Proper scoring rules are used to train the posterior, and the framework is grounded in Bayes decision theory.
AI.result: On synthetic exact-ambiguity experiments, gaps between point and posterior predictions track the theoretical ambiguity barrier. On PDEBench tasks with hidden metadata, posterior recovery reduces pooled rollout nRMSE from 0.175 to 0.132, closing 59.4% of the gap between direct prediction and an oracle model.
AI.conclusion: Single-observation neural PDE simulation should adopt a posterior-first paradigm rather than monolithic field-to-future prediction, as it better handles inherent ambiguity and improves both rollout accuracy and downstream decision reliability.

title: Identity-Consistent Multi-Pose Generation of Contactless Fingerprints
summary: Contactless fingerprint recognition has gained increasing attention due to its advantages in hygiene and acquisition flexibility. However, the absence of physical contact constraints introduces severe nonlinear geometric distortions caused by free finger poses in 3D space, resulting in a substantial cross-modal domain gap between contactless and conventional contact-based fingerprints. Existing solutions largely rely on explicit geometric correction or image enhancement, which are fragile under extreme pose variations. In this paper, we propose Identity-Consistent Multi-Pose Generation of Contactless Fingerprints (IMPOSE), a physics-inspired framework that synthesizes identity-preserving, multi-pose contactless fingerprint samples to empower recognition models. IMPOSE consists of three stages: (1) rolled fingerprint identity generation via latent diffusion with discrete codebook representations, (2) cross-modal translation from rolled to contactless modality guided by Sauvola-based local adaptive binarization as an identity anchor, and (3) physics-based multi-pose simulation through 3D finger model texture mapping and projection. The generated samples maintain strict identity consistency at the ridge topology level and spatial alignment with standard fingerprint coordinate space. Extensive experiments on the UWA and PolyU CL2CB databases demonstrate that fine-tuning fixed-length dense descriptors (FDD) with IMPOSE-synthesized data achieves state-of-the-art cross-modal matching, reducing EER to 8.74% on UWA and 2.26% on PolyU CL2CB. Synthetic data also yields consistent gains across mainstream representations including DeepPrint and AFRNet, and the hybrid strategy combining synthetic and real data achieves the best overall results. The code and generated samples are available at https://github.com/Yu-Yy/IMPOSE.

AI.tldr: IMPOSE is a physics-inspired framework that generates identity-preserving, multi-pose contactless fingerprint samples via three stages (latent diffusion, cross-modal translation with Sauvola binarization, and 3D pose simulation) to bridge the cross-modal domain gap between contactless and contact fingerprints, achieving state-of-the-art cross-modal matching results (EER 8.74% on UWA, 2.26% on PolyU CL2CB).
AI.motivation: Contactless fingerprint recognition suffers from severe nonlinear geometric distortions due to free finger poses in 3D space, creating a large cross-modal domain gap between contactless and contact-based fingerprints. Existing geometric correction or image enhancement methods are fragile under extreme pose variations.
AI.method: IMPOSE has three stages: (1) rolled fingerprint identity generation via latent diffusion with discrete codebook representations, (2) cross-modal translation from rolled to contactless modality guided by Sauvola-based local adaptive binarization as an identity anchor, and (3) physics-based multi-pose simulation through 3D finger model texture mapping and projection.
AI.result: Fine-tuning FDD with IMPOSE-synthesized data achieves SOTA cross-modal matching: EER 8.74% on UWA and 2.26% on PolyU CL2CB. Consistent gains across DeepPrint and AFRNet. Hybrid synthetic+real data achieves best overall results.
AI.conclusion: IMPOSE effectively bridges the cross-modal domain gap by generating identity-consistent multi-pose contactless fingerprint samples, outperforming existing methods. The code and generated samples are publicly available.

title: Ortho-Hydra: Orthogonalized Experts for DiT LoRA
summary: LoRA fine-tuning of diffusion transformers (DiT) on multi-style data suffers from \emph{style bleed}: a single low-rank residual cannot represent several distinct artist fingerprints, and the optimizer converges to their average. Mixture-of-experts LoRA in the HydraLoRA style replaces the up-projection with $E$ heads under a router, but when every expert is zero-initialized the router receives identical gradient from each head and remains at the uniform prior. The experts then evolve permutation-symmetrically, and the network trains as a single rank-$r$ LoRA at $E{\times}$ the cost. We present \textbf{Ortho-Hydra}, a re-parameterisation that combines an OFT-style Cayley-orthogonal shared basis with per-expert \emph{disjoint output subspaces} carved from the top-$(Er)$ left singular vectors of the pretrained weight. Disjointness makes the router's per-expert score non-degenerate at step~$0$, so specialization receives gradient signal before any expert has trained. We test the predicted deadlock on a DiT pipeline by comparing two HydraLoRA baselines, a zero-initialized shared-basis variant and the original $σ{=}0.1$ Gaussian-jitter mitigation, against Ortho-Hydra under a matched optimiser, dataset, and step budget. Neither baseline leaves the uniform prior within the first $1\text{k}$ steps; Ortho-Hydra begins de-uniformising within the first few hundred. End-task generation quality on multi-style data is out of scope; we report the construction, the cold-start mechanism, and the routing dynamics it changes. Code: https://github.com/sorryhyun/anima_lora.

AI.tldr: Ortho-Hydra solves the style bleed problem in multi-style DiT LoRA fine-tuning by using a Cayley-orthogonal shared basis with disjoint per-expert output subspaces, breaking the permutation-symmetric deadlock that plagues zero-initialized HydraLoRA variants.
AI.motivation: Multi-style LoRA fine-tuning of diffusion transformers suffers from "style bleed" (averaging of distinct artistic styles). Mixture-of-experts LoRA (HydraLoRA) attempts to fix this but fails when experts are zero-initialized because the router receives identical gradients, staying at uniform prior and training as a single rank-r LoRA at E× cost.
AI.method: Ortho-Hydra combines an OFT-style Cayley-orthogonal shared basis with per-expert disjoint output subspaces carved from the top-(Er) left singular vectors of the pretrained weight. This ensures the router's per-expert score is non-degenerate at step 0, enabling expert specialization from initialization.
AI.result: Both HydraLoRA baselines (zero-initialized and Gaussian-jitter) stay at uniform prior within first 1k steps; Ortho-Hydra begins de-uniformising within the first few hundred steps, breaking the cold-start deadlock.
AI.conclusion: Ortho-Hydra provides a principled cold-start mechanism for mixture-of-experts LoRA by enforcing disjoint output subspaces, enabling early expert specialization without needing tricks like Gaussian jitter.

title: Conditions for well-posed color recovery in scattering media
summary: Recovering scene color from images captured in scattering media is a fundamental inverse problem in optical imaging. Yet the problem is intrinsically ill-posed as multiple solutions can explain the same observation, and prediction error cannot be controlled without understanding the space of candidate solutions. Here, we present sufficient conditions under which color recovery in a scattering medium becomes well-posed. Observing that ill-posedness stems from (i) projection of spectral signals onto pixel intensities, and (ii) unknown medium parameters, we demonstrate that sensor improvements alone cannot resolve medium-induced distortions without additional constraints. We identify recovery patterns, cross-pixel relationships that naturally occur in images, and prove, for an ideal hyperspectral camera, that they restrict the solution to a unique candidate. This opens the door to a new class of vision algorithms grounded in first principles, enabling quantitative analysis of images in scattering environments.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies sufficient conditions for well-posed color recovery in scattering media, showing that cross-pixel relationships (recovery patterns) can uniquely determine the solution even with unknown medium parameters.
AI.motivation: Color recovery from images in scattering media (e.g., fog, underwater) is fundamentally ill-posed because multiple scene colors can produce the same observed image, making prediction error uncontrollable.
AI.method: The authors analyze the two sources of ill-posedness: (i) spectral-to-pixel projection and (ii) unknown medium parameters. They identify "recovery patterns"—cross-pixel relationships naturally occurring in images—and prove for an ideal hyperspectral camera that these patterns restrict the solution space to a unique candidate.
AI.result: Demonstrates that sensor improvements alone cannot resolve medium-induced distortions. Proves sufficient conditions under which the problem becomes well-posed using recovery patterns.
AI.conclusion: Opens the door to first-principles-based vision algorithms for quantitative analysis of images captured in scattering environments.

title: Do LLMs have core beliefs?
summary: The rise of Large Language Models (LLMs) has sparked debate about whether these systems exhibit human-level cognition. In this debate, little attention has been paid to a structural component of human cognition: core beliefs, truths that provide a foundation around which we can build a worldview. These commitments usually resist debunking, as abandoning them would represent a fundamental shift in how we see reality. In this paper, we ask whether LLMs hold anything akin to core commitments. Using a probing framework we call Adversarial Dialogue Trees (ADTs) over five domains (science, history, geography, biology, and mathematics), we find that most LLMs fail to maintain a stable worldview. Though some recent models showed improved stability, they still eventually failed to maintain key commitments under conversational pressure. These results document an improvement in argumentative skills across model generations but indicate that all current models lack a key component of human-level cognition.

AI.tldr: LLMs lack core beliefs (stable worldview commitments) under conversational pressure, revealing a missing component of human-level cognition despite improved argumentative skills.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether LLMs possess "core beliefs" — foundational truths that anchor human cognition and resist debunking — an underexplored aspect in the debate about LLM human-level cognition.
AI.method: Used Adversarial Dialogue Trees (ADTs) to probe LLMs across five domains (science, history, geography, biology, mathematics), applying conversational pressure to test stability of commitments.
AI.result: Most LLMs failed to maintain stable worldviews; recent models showed improved stability but eventually abandoned key commitments under pressure.
AI.conclusion: While argumentative skills improve across model generations, current LLMs lack core belief systems essential to human-level cognition.

title: Parameter-Efficient Multi-View Proficiency Estimation: From Discriminative Classification to Generative Feedback
summary: Estimating how well a person performs an action, rather than which action is performed, is central to coaching, rehabilitation, and talent identification. This task is challenging because proficiency is encoded in subtle differences in timing, balance, body mechanics, and execution, often distributed across multiple views and short temporal events. We discuss three recent contributions to multi-view proficiency estimation on Ego-Exo4D. SkillFormer introduces a parameter-efficient discriminative architecture for selective multi-view fusion; PATS improves temporal sampling by preserving locally dense excerpts of fundamental movements; and ProfVLM reformulates proficiency estimation as conditional language generation, producing both a proficiency label and expert-style feedback through a gated cross-view projector and a compact language backbone. Together, these methods achieve state-of-the-art accuracy on Ego-Exo4D with up to 20x fewer trainable parameters and up to 3x fewer training epochs than video-transformer baselines, while moving from closed-set classification toward interpretable feedback generation. These results highlight a shift toward efficient, multi-view systems that combine selective fusion, proficiency-aware sampling, and actionable generative feedback.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews three recent methods (SkillFormer, PATS, ProfVLM) for multi-view proficiency estimation on Ego-Exo4D, which achieve state-of-the-art accuracy with significantly fewer parameters and training epochs, while moving toward interpretable generative feedback.
AI.motivation: Estimating how well a person performs an action (proficiency) is crucial for coaching, rehabilitation, and talent identification, but is challenging due to subtle differences in timing, balance, body mechanics, and execution across multiple views and short temporal events.
AI.method: SkillFormer uses a parameter-efficient discriminative architecture for selective multi-view fusion; PATS improves temporal sampling by preserving locally dense excerpts; ProfVLM reformulates proficiency estimation as conditional language generation with a gated cross-view projector and compact language backbone.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on Ego-Exo4D with up to 20x fewer trainable parameters and up to 3x fewer training epochs than video-transformer baselines.
AI.conclusion: Highlights a shift toward efficient, multi-view systems combining selective fusion, proficiency-aware sampling, and actionable generative feedback, moving from closed-set classification to interpretable feedback generation.

title: The Right Answer, the Wrong Direction: Why Transformers Fail at Counting and How to Fix It
comment: 26 pages, 3 figures; Code and reproduction materials: https://github.com/Gpgabriel25/GeometricReadoutBottleneck
summary:
Large language models often fail at simple counting tasks, even when the items to count are explicitly present in the prompt. We investigate whether this failure occurs because transformers do not represent counts internally, or because they cannot convert those representations into the correct output tokens.
  Across three model families, Pythia, Qwen3, and Mistral, ranging from 0.4B to 14B parameters, we find strong evidence for the second explanation. Linear probes recover the correct count from intermediate layers with near-perfect accuracy ($R^2>0.99$), showing that the information is present. However, the internal directions that encode counts are nearly orthogonal to the output-head rows for digit tokens ($|\cos|\leq0.032$). In other words, the model stores the count in a form that the digit logits do not naturally read out.
  We localize this failure with two interventions. Updating only the digit rows of the output head (36,864 parameters) substantially improves constrained next-token digit prediction (60.7 to 100.0% across four tasks), but it does not fix autoregressive generation. By contrast, a small LoRA intervention on attention Q/V weights (7.67M parameters) improves upstream routing and achieves 83.1% +/- 7.2% in true greedy autoregressive generation. Logit-lens measurements confirm the mechanism: the correct digit's vocabulary rank drops from 55,980 to 1, a 50,000x improvement. Additional norm, logit-lens, and cross-task analyses show that the bottleneck generalizes across character counting, addition, and list length, while remaining absent from broader multi-step reasoning benchmarks, including MMLU, GSM8K, and DROP.
  These results identify counting failure as a geometric readout bottleneck rather than a failure of internal representation: the model knows the count but the output pathway is geometrically misaligned with the tokens needed to express it.

AI.tldr: LLMs fail at counting tasks not because they can't compute counts, but because their internal count representations are geometrically misaligned with the output token embeddings needed to express them. Linear probes recover counts with near-perfect accuracy, but the count directions are nearly orthogonal to digit token output heads. Small interventions on the output head (36K params) or attention weights (7.67M params) significantly fix the issue.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether LLMs' poor performance on simple counting tasks stems from a failure to internally represent counts or from a failure to convert those representations into correct output tokens.
AI.method: Used linear probes to recover counts from intermediate layers across Pythia, Qwen3, and Mistral models (0.4B-14B). Measured geometric alignment between count directions and digit output-head rows. Applied two interventions: updating output-head digit rows (36K params) and LoRA on attention Q/V weights (7.67M params). Used logit-lens analysis to trace the mechanism.
AI.result: Linear probes achieve near-perfect count recovery (R²>0.99) from intermediate layers. Count directions are nearly orthogonal to digit output-head rows (|cos|≤0.032). Output-head intervention improves constrained digit prediction from 60.7% to 100%. LoRA on attention weights achieves 83.1% ± 7.2% in autoregressive generation. Correct digit rank drops from 55,980 to 1 (~50,000x improvement).
AI.conclusion: Counting failures in LLMs are caused by a geometric readout bottleneck — the model knows the count internally but cannot express it because the output pathway is misaligned with the required digit tokens. This bottleneck generalizes across character counting, addition, and list length tasks, but does not affect broader reasoning benchmarks (MMLU, GSM8K, DROP).

title: Stream-R1: Reliability-Perplexity Aware Reward Distillation for Streaming Video Generation
summary: Distillation-based acceleration has become foundational for making autoregressive streaming video diffusion models practical, with distribution matching distillation (DMD) as the de facto choice. Existing methods, however, train the student to match the teacher's output indiscriminately, treating every rollout, frame, and pixel as equally reliable supervision. We argue that this caps distilled quality, since it overlooks two complementary axes of variance in DMD supervision: Inter-Reliability across student rollouts whose supervision varies in reliability, and Intra-Perplexity across spatial regions and temporal frames that contribute unequally to where quality can still be improved. The objective thus conflates two questions under a uniform weight: whether to learn from each rollout, and where to concentrate optimization within it. To address this, we propose Stream-R1, a Reliability-Perplexity Aware Reward Distillation framework that adaptively reweights the distillation objective at both rollout and spatiotemporal-element levels through a single shared reward-guided mechanism. At the Inter-Reliability level, Stream-R1 rescales each rollout's loss by an exponential of a pretrained video reward score, so that rollouts with reliable supervision dominate optimization. At the Intra-Perplexity level, it back-propagates the same reward model to extract per-pixel gradient saliency, which is factored into spatial and temporal weights that concentrate optimization pressure on regions and frames where refinement yields the largest expected gain. An adaptive balancing mechanism prevents any single quality axis from dominating across visual quality, motion quality, and text alignment. Stream-R1 attains consistent improvements on all three dimensions over distillation baselines on standard streaming video generation benchmarks, without architectural modification or additional inference cost.

AI.tldr: Stream-R1 proposes a reliability-perplexity aware reward distillation framework that adaptively reweights distillation objectives at both rollout and spatiotemporal-element levels, improving streaming video diffusion model quality across visual quality, motion quality, and text alignment without extra inference cost.
AI.motivation: Existing DMD-based distillation treats all rollouts, frames, and pixels equally, overlooking two key variance axes: Inter-Reliability across student rollouts and Intra-Perplexity across spatial/temporal regions, which caps distilled quality.
AI.method: Stream-R1 introduces a reward-guided mechanism with two levels: (1) Inter-Reliability level rescales each rollout's loss by exponential of a pretrained video reward score; (2) Intra-Perplexity level back-propagates the reward model to extract per-pixel gradient saliency, decomposed into spatial and temporal weights, plus an adaptive balancing mechanism.
AI.result: Stream-R1 achieves consistent improvements on visual quality, motion quality, and text alignment over distillation baselines on standard streaming video generation benchmarks, with no architectural changes or added inference cost.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive reward-guided reweighting of distillation objectives at both rollout and spatiotemporal levels effectively unlocks higher quality in distilled streaming video diffusion models.

title: A Universal Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space from Polynomial Alignment and IMQ Distance
summary: We introduce the Yat kernel $$k_{b,\varepsilon}(\mathbf{w},\mathbf{x})=\frac{(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}+b)^2}{\|\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{w}\|^2+\varepsilon},\qquad b\ge 0,\ \varepsilon>0,$$ a rational hidden-unit primitive whose units are Mercer sections over a shared input/weight space. For $b\ge 0$ the kernel is PSD; for $b>0$ it dominates a scaled inverse-multiquadric (IMQ) in the Loewner order, yielding fixed-kernel universality, characteristicness, and strict positive definiteness on every compact domain. The polynomial numerator opens nonradial alignment channels absent from finite IMQ expansions, witnessed by the directional far-field trace $T_\infty g_\varepsilon(\cdot;\mathbf{w},b)(\mathbf{u})=(\mathbf{u}^\top\mathbf{w})^2$. Algebraically, a second finite difference in the bias recovers any IMQ atom from three positive-bias Yat atoms exactly, sharp at three atoms in every dimension at exact pointwise equality. A trained shared-$(b,\varepsilon)$ Yat layer is therefore a finite learned-center expansion in a fixed universal characteristic RKHS, with closed-form norm $\boldsymbolα^\top\mathbf{K}\boldsymbolα$ and explicit diagonal $(\|\mathbf{x}\|^2+b)^2/\varepsilon$ driving a Rademacher generalization bound.

AI.tldr: Introduces the Yat kernel, a rational hidden-unit primitive that is PSD, universal, characteristic, and strictly positive definite. It dominates the IMQ kernel in Loewner order, opens nonradial alignment channels, and enables exact recovery of IMQ atoms via finite differences. A trained Yat layer operates in a universal RKHS with closed-form norm and explicit generalization bounds.
AI.motivation: To develop a kernel that combines polynomial nonradial expressiveness with the universal approximation and characteristic properties of radial kernels like IMQ, while maintaining closed-form analytical tractability.
AI.method: Proposes the Yat kernel k(w,x) = (w^Tx+b)^2 / (||x-w||^2+ε), proves it is PSD for b≥0, shows it dominates a scaled IMQ in Loewner order, establishes universality/characteristicness/SPD, derives directional far-field behavior, and shows finite-difference recovery of IMQ atoms from three Yat atoms.
AI.result: Yat kernel is PSD, universal, characteristic, and strictly positive definite on compact domains. It dominates scaled IMQ kernel and provides nonradial alignment. Three positive-bias Yat atoms exactly recover any IMQ atom via second finite difference. Yat layer offers closed-form norm and explicit Rademacher generalization bound.
AI.conclusion: The Yat kernel provides a principled bridge between rational quadratic forms and radial basis functions, enabling nonradial feature alignment while retaining the theoretical guarantees of universal RKHS, with practical advantages for finite learned-center expansions.

title: A Deeper Dive into the Irreversibility of PolyProtect: Making Protected Face Templates Harder to Invert
comment: Submitted to TIFS journal on 18 February 2026 (under review). Consists of: 12 pages, 10 figures, 4 tables
summary: This work presents a deeper analysis of the "irreversibility" property of PolyProtect, a biometric template protection method initially proposed for securing face embeddings. PolyProtect transforms embeddings into protected templates via multivariate polynomials, whose coefficients and exponents are distinct for each subject enrolled in the face recognition system. A polynomial is applied to consecutive sets of elements from a given embedding, where the amount of overlap between the sets is a tunable parameter. We begin our irreversibility analysis by demonstrating that PolyProtected templates are easier to invert using a numerical solver based on cosine distance, as opposed to Euclidean distance (used in the earlier PolyProtect work). To make this inversion more difficult, we then propose a "key selection algorithm", which tries to choose "keys" (coefficients and exponents of the PolyProtect polynomial) that enhance the irreversibility of PolyProtected templates, compared to when the keys are purely random. Our experiments show that this algorithm is effective at generating PolyProtected templates that are significantly more difficult to invert, and that it approximately equalises the irreversibility of PolyProtected templates generated using different "overlap" parameters. This allows for better control of the irreversibility versus accuracy trade-off, known to exist across different overlaps. We also show that accuracy in the PolyProtected domain can be affected by the range in which the embedding elements lie, but that this can be improved by normalizing the embeddings prior to applying PolyProtect. This work is reproducible using our open-source code.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes and improves the irreversibility of PolyProtect, a biometric template protection method. The authors show that templates are easier to invert using cosine distance-based solvers, propose a key selection algorithm to enhance irreversibility, and demonstrate that embedding normalization improves accuracy.
AI.motivation: To deepen the understanding of PolyProtect's irreversibility property and address its vulnerability to inversion attacks using cosine distance-based numerical solvers.
AI.method: (1) Demonstrates that cosine distance-based numerical solvers invert PolyProtected templates more effectively than Euclidean distance-based ones. (2) Proposes a "key selection algorithm" to choose polynomial coefficients/exponents that enhance irreversibility over random selection. (3) Normalizes embeddings before applying PolyProtect to improve accuracy.
AI.result: The key selection algorithm generates templates significantly harder to invert and equalizes irreversibility across different overlap parameters. Normalizing embeddings improves accuracy in the PolyProtected domain.
AI.conclusion: The proposed enhancements enable better control of the irreversibility vs. accuracy trade-off in PolyProtect, making it more secure and practical for biometric template protection.

title: Partially Observed Structural Causal Models
summary: Here we introduce Partially Observed Structural Causal Models (POSCMs) that formalize causal systems where latent contexts co-determine both the interaction structure and downstream mechanisms on observed variables. POSCMs provide an extension of structural causal models (SCMs), as a self-contained causal modeling framework for endogenous graphs, allowing for an intervention hierarchy spanning node- and edge-level context and endogenous variable interventions. To enable surgical edge interventions, we adopt a Kolmogorov-Arnold-Sprecher edge-functional decomposition, an existence theorem for representing each node mechanism as a sum of univariate functions of its parents, yielding an explicit parametrization of dyadic functional contributions. We provide an identifiability theory that clarifies which intervention families would suffice to disentangle structure formation from mechanisms. We empirically validate these predictions in a biophysically detailed virtual human retina simulator, constructing intervention protocols that (i) reproduce the non-identifiability predicted when context is latent and no context-level interventions are available, (ii) exhibit structure-mechanism confounding under latent edges when only node interventions are observed, and (iii) recover synaptic input-output relationships via targeted node interventions, consistent with our positive kernel identifiability result. Our work generalizes SCMs in a way that allows it to work in a world closer to the one we live in.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Partially Observed Structural Causal Models (POSCMs), an extension of SCMs that handles latent contexts affecting both causal structure and mechanisms, with theoretical identifiability analysis and validation on a virtual retina simulator.
AI.motivation: Standard SCMs assume fixed causal graphs and fully observed variables, but real-world systems often have latent contexts that jointly influence both which variables interact and how they influence each other. The paper aims to formalize and enable causal reasoning in such partially observed, structure-variant settings.
AI.method: POSCMs extend SCMs to endogenous graphs with latent contexts. They adopt a Kolmogorov-Arnold-Sprecher decomposition for edge-functional representation, enabling surgical edge interventions. An identifiability theory is developed to determine which intervention families can disentangle structure from mechanisms.
AI.result: Empirical validation in a biophysically detailed virtual human retina simulator shows: (i) non-identifiability when context is latent without context-level interventions, (ii) structure-mechanism confounding under latent edges with only node interventions, and (iii) recovery of synaptic relationships via targeted node interventions, matching theoretical predictions.
AI.conclusion: POSCMs generalize SCMs to handle latent contexts and endogenous causal graphs, providing a framework closer to real-world complexity with formal identifiability guarantees and empirical support.

title: DMGD: Train-Free Dataset Distillation with Semantic-Distribution Matching in Diffusion Models
summary: Dataset distillation enables efficient training by distilling the information of large-scale datasets into significantly smaller synthetic datasets. Diffusion based paradigms have emerged in recent years, offering novel perspectives for dataset distillation. However, they typically necessitate additional fine-tuning stages, and effective guidance mechanisms remain underexplored. To address these limitations, we rethink diffusion based dataset distillation and propose a Dual Matching Guided Diffusion (DMGD) framework, centered on efficient training-free guidance. We first establish Semantic Matching via conditional likelihood optimization, eliminating the need for auxiliary classifiers. Furthermore, we propose a dynamic guidance mechanism that enhances the diversity of synthetic data while maintaining semantic alignment. Simultaneously, we introduce an optimal transport (OT) based Distribution Matching approach to further align with the target distribution structure. To ensure efficiency, we develop two enhanced strategies for diffusion based framework: Distribution Approximate Matching and Greedy Progressive Matching. These strategies enable effective distribution matching guidance with minimal computational overhead. Experimental results on ImageNet-Woof, ImageNet-Nette, and ImageNet-1K demonstrate that our training-free approach achieves significant improvements, outperforming state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods requiring additional fine-tuning by average accuracy gains of 2.1%, 5.4%, and 2.4%, respectively.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes DMGD, a training-free diffusion-based dataset distillation framework using dual matching guidance (semantic + distribution) that outperforms SOTA methods requiring fine-tuning on ImageNet subsets.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion-based dataset distillation methods require additional fine-tuning stages and lack effective guidance mechanisms, limiting their efficiency and performance.
AI.method: DMGD introduces (1) Semantic Matching via conditional likelihood optimization without auxiliary classifiers, (2) Dynamic guidance mechanism for synthetic data diversity, (3) OT-based Distribution Matching, and (4) two efficient strategies: Distribution Approximate Matching and Greedy Progressive Matching.
AI.result: On ImageNet-Woof, ImageNet-Nette, and ImageNet-1K, DMGD achieves average accuracy gains of 2.1%, 5.4%, and 2.4% over SOTA fine-tuning methods, while being training-free.
AI.conclusion: The proposed training-free dual matching guidance framework is effective and efficient for diffusion-based dataset distillation, eliminating the need for costly fine-tuning stages.

title: RFPrompt: Prompt-Based Expert Adaptation of the Large Wireless Model for Modulation Classification
summary: Automatic modulation classification (AMC) in real-world deployments demands robustness to distribution shifts arising from hardware impairments, unseen propagation environments, and recording conditions never encountered during training. Although wireless foundation models offer a promising starting point for robust RF representation learning, an important open question is how to adapt them efficiently to out-of-distribution (OOD) downstream tasks without overwriting the structure learned during large-scale pre-training. In this paper, we investigate prompt-based adaptation as a general mechanism for OOD transfer in wireless foundation models. We propose RFPrompt, a parameter-efficient framework that introduces learnable deep prompt tokens while keeping the pretrained backbone frozen, enabling task-specific adaptation with minimal trainable parameters. We instantiate and evaluate this approach on the Large Wireless Model (LWM), a mixture-of-experts wireless foundation model, and study its behavior under both standard and OOD modulation-classification settings. Results show that prompt-based adaptation consistently improves robustness under distribution shift and limited supervision, particularly on real-world over-the-air IQ data, while preserving strong parameter efficiency. These findings suggest that prompt learning is a practical and effective strategy for adapting wireless foundation models to challenging downstream RF environments.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes RFPrompt, a prompt-based adaptation method for wireless foundation models that keeps the pretrained backbone frozen while adding learnable prompt tokens, achieving robust automatic modulation classification under distribution shifts with high parameter efficiency.
AI.motivation: Real-world AMC faces distribution shifts from hardware impairments, unseen environments, and recording conditions not encountered during training; existing wireless foundation models need efficient adaptation methods that preserve pre-trained structure.
AI.method: RFPrompt introduces learnable deep prompt tokens into the frozen Large Wireless Model (LWM) backbone, enabling task-specific adaptation with minimal trainable parameters for OOD transfer.
AI.result: Prompt-based adaptation consistently improves robustness under distribution shift and limited supervision, particularly on real-world over-the-air IQ data, while preserving strong parameter efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Prompt learning is a practical and effective strategy for adapting wireless foundation models to challenging downstream RF environments.

title: Raising the Ceiling: Better Empirical Fixation Densities for Saliency Benchmarking
summary: Empirical fixation densities, spatial distributions estimated from human eye-tracking data, are foundational to saliency benchmarking. They directly shape benchmark conclusions, leaderboard rankings, failure case analyses, and scientific claims about human visual behavior. Yet the standard estimation method, fixed-bandwidth isotropic Gaussian KDE, has gone essentially unchanged for decades. This matters now more than ever: as the field shifts toward sample-level evaluation (failure case analysis, inverse benchmarking, per-image model comparison), reliable per-image density estimates become critical. We propose a principled mixture model that combines an adaptive-bandwidth KDE based on Abramson's method, center bias and uniform components, and a state-of-the-art saliency model, to capture different spatial and semantic types of interobserver consistency, and optimize all parameters per image via leave-one-subject-out cross-validation. Our method yields substantially higher interobserver consistency estimates across multiple benchmarks, with median per-image gains of 5-15% in log-likelihood and up to 2 percentage points in AUC. For the most affected images -- precisely those most relevant to failure case analysis -- improvements exceed 25%. We leverage these improved estimates to identify and analyze remaining failure cases of state-of-the-art saliency models, demonstrating that significant headroom for model improvement remains.
